We should celebrate different cultures, celebrate diversity and everybody is welcome.” For vendors like Angeles Angeles of First Nations Trading, the powwow was a place to share traditional crafts and knowledge with the public. “My mission is mainly to share our culture and our ancient knowledge through wearable art, handmade crafts and medicines,” Angeles said. “I'm reintroducing native medicines like plant medicine, the plants that we think of as weeds, but we Native Americans have used for centuries.” For Angeles, providing knowledge for younger generations is a very important task and special experience. “Everything we do is thinking seven generations ahead, not for us, but for the future,” she said. Editor's note: This story was updated Wednesday afternoon to correctly reflect that the powwow was held in collaboration with the Center for Multicultural Affairs, not Student Affairs. The Chronicle regrets the error.

title: Nearly 7 in 10 first-years don't utilize campus mental health resources
subtitle: This story is part of a series about the Class of 2029 based on a survey conducted by The Chronicle in January and February 2026.
author: Zev van Zanten
text: Editor's note: This story is part of a series about the Class of 2029 based on a survey conducted by The Chronicle in January and February 2026. You can read more about our methodology and limitations here or read all our survey coverage here . Partway through the Class of 2029’s first year on campus, we asked them about the Duke-supplied resources they had engaged with to support their transition to college life. The vast majority, 92.40%, had used at least one academic, mental health or physical health and wellness resource offered by the University. About a third of surveyed first-years indicated they had not engaged with any of the Duke-supplied academic resources listed. They included Ask a Librarian ; the Career Center ; Directors of Academic Engagement ; GRE/MCAT Prep ; group tutoring; LD/ADHD Support; Learning Consultations; Office of Health Professions Advising ; other pre-professional advising; pre-graduate advising ; the STEM Advancement through Group Engagement Program ( SAGE ); Student Disability Access Office ; Study Connect at the Academic Resource Center; Subject Specialists at Duke Libraries and the Thompson Writing Program writing studio . For the students that had, the Thompson Writing Program, Career Center and SAGE were the most popular, used by 26.00%, 19.60% and 18.80% of students respectively. These programs were also the most used by first-years in last year’s survey . First-years in the Trinity College of Arts & Sciences were more likely to engage with at least one academic resource than those in the Pratt School of Engineering. Only 31.63% of Trinity students said they did not use any of the listed options, compared to 40.74% for Pratt. After the Thompson Writing Program, Career Center and SAGE, respondents in Pratt used Ask a Librarian and Office of Health Professions Advising services the most — though Trinity students made up the majority of users for every service. Respondents studying in arts and humanities fields were mostly likely to engage with University-supplied academic resources, with 72.41% indicating they had used at least one. First-years intending to major in a social science field were next at 67.31%, followed by those in the natural sciences at 65.54%. Engineering first-years used Duke’s academic resources the least, at 58.49%. Respondents with a parent or sibling who also attended Duke were less likely to engage with academic resources on campus and engaged with a much smaller range of total resources. First-generation respondents were more likely to engage with academic resources on campus than their peers — namely SAGE, which 30.61% of first-generation respondents used versus 15.92% of non-first-generation respondents, and group tutoring, used by 20.41% of first-generation students but only 10.45% of non-first-generation students. Compared to academic resources, surveyed first-years were far less likely to engage with campus mental health support opportunities. Over two-thirds of respondents, 68.80%, indicated they had not engaged with any of the campus mental health resources we listed, which included Blue Devils Care , Counseling and Psychological Services , DukeReach , DukeLine , DuWell , Peer for You, Shmoody and the Two-Click to Connect Form. For the remaining students, CAPS proved to be most popular by far, with 19.60% of surveyed first-years having engaged with it, followed by Shmoody and DuWell at 6.40% and 4.00% respectively. Pratt first-years were slightly less likely to engage with mental health resources than their Trinity counterparts, with 70.37% of Pratt respondents indicating they used none of the listed resources versus 68.37% of those in Trinity. This was driven mostly by a decreased usage of CAPS, with 20.41% of surveyed Trinity first-years having accessed CAPS compared to only 16.67% of Pratt first-years. Legacy students were far less likely to engage with campus mental health resources, with 83.87% of them indicating they had not made use of a single resource. By contrast, only 66.67% of non-legacies had not engaged with campus mental health resources. The gap was similarly pronounced for first-generation and non-first-generation students, with 40.82% of first-generation students engaging with at least one campus mental health resource versus only 28.86% of their counterparts. Physical health and wellness resources, broadly defined, constituted by far the most widely used set of campus resources among the Class of 2029. A large majority of surveyed students, 86.0%, said they engaged with at least one of the resources listed. Our survey listed activities at the Wellness Center , the Arts Annex , the Campus Pharmacy , Campus Smiles Dentistry , diet and nutrition lecture courses, Feed Every Devil food point bank, group fitness classes at Wilson or Brodie Recreation Center , nutrition services at Student Health, Outdoor Adventures with Personal Trips , Student Health at the Wellness Center, and weight/exercise rooms at Wilson or Brodie Recreation Centers. Usage was down from last year, where 94.64% of respondents had used at least one physical health or wellness resource — though we surveyed first-years earlier in the year this time, so they had less time to engage with resources than our Class of 2028 respondents. Consistent with previous years, Wilson and Brodie were the most popular physical health resource — and the only one used by a majority of our respondents, with 67.60% of surveyed first-years having used the weight and exercise rooms and about a fifth having taken group fitness classes at either facility. The only other physical health and wellness resources used by over a quarter of respondents were Student Health at the Wellness Center and the Campus Pharmacy. Compared to Trinity first-years, Pratt students were less likely to engage with campus physical health and wellness resources, with 16.67% of Pratt respondents not using any compared to 13.27% of Trinity’s. First-generation respondents were much more likely to use campus physical health resources, with only 6.25% indicating zero usage compared to 15.92% of their peers. The same was true for legacy students, who were much more likely than their peers to use at least one resource at 22.58% versus 12.79%. When it came to discovery of campus resources, 74.40% of students found out about things by word of mouth, followed by 40.00% from email lists and 22.00% by social media. Respondents also cited Blue Book and The Chronicle, although at lower rates. We also asked the Class of 2029 about some of their physical health habits, including dining and sleep. A majority of respondents, 73.60%, said they did not run out of food points by the end of the fall semester. Of those with food points remaining, a plurality of 47.83% said they had many left over; another 29.89% said they were rationing food points near the end, either by eating smaller meals or less frequently; and the remaining 22.28% had just the right amount. Food point usage was consistent across income levels for the most part, with some departures near both ends of the spectrum. First-years with estimated household incomes below $40,000 were the most likely to have run out of food points, while those with incomes between $250,000 and $500,000 were the most likely to have many food points left over. Almost all our surveyed first-years used equivalency: the system by which first-year students can replace a breakfast or dinner meal swipe at Marketplace, the East Campus dining hall, with an allotment of food points to be used at certain dining vendors during specified times. Nearly half said they used the system two or three times per week, the most commonly reported frequency. Nine in 10 respondents said they regularly got between six and eight hours of sleep per night; seven hours was the most commonly reported.

title: Sound check: The student musicians at the heart of Duke’s music scene
subtitle: Students a part of Small Town Records, Duke’s fully student-run record label, are a community of performers bringing the music scene and student voices to campus.
author: Claire Cranford
text: Somewhere on the lower level of Bryan Center lies a recording studio many students don’t know exists. Inside are professional-grade microphones, soundproofed walls and, on any given weekday, at least one undergraduate trying to find their artistic voice. The studio is home to Small Town Records — Duke’s fully student-run record label — and serves as a hub for a relatively underground music scene. For many, Duke’s creative arts offerings may not be the first thing that comes to mind, with students leaning towards pre-professional pathways such as finance, consulting and law. Yet within Duke’s smaller community of student musicians, another culture is taking shape. A rapper from the Bronx cuts tracks at 3 a.m. A physics student lets a song sit for two weeks before she decides if she likes it. A first-year from Greenville, South Carolina, is figuring out how to play his guitar in his dorm without waking up his neighbors. And a senior is preparing to close out college the same way she started it: on a stage. Ask any of them when they started making music, and the general answer is the same: early in life. Hannon Stern, a sophomore singer-songwriter who described music as “a language that just is very intuitive to me,” started writing songs in sixth grade on the piano — bad ones, she’ll tell you, and she was okay with them being bad. “I liked the feeling it gave me in the privacy of my room,” she said. And her music was private for years, shared through voice memos to friends and only once at a songwriting camp during high school. Grace Davis, a senior neuroscience major just days away from graduation, also traces her musical roots to a young age. At four years old, she was a Taylor Swift devotee. By five, she was begging her parents for a guitar. “They said, ‘if you play piano for a year and you still want to play guitar, we'll buy you one,’” she laughed. She got the guitar a year later, and has been playing ever since. Jaden Small, stagename JDon!, a junior Reginaldo Howard Scholar studying political science and history, was gifted his first microphone from his father, who also made music. “It's the same one I use to this day,” he said. And Jack Stapleton, a first-year from Greeneville, S.C., got his start slightly differently. At 11, he began messing around with Garageband on his phone, lying to his friends that he could play the guitar presets he was showing them. Until one of his friends called his bluff, asking him to play it on a guitar. “I couldn’t do it,” he said. “Funny enough, that’s how I started wanting to learn.” Fast forward eight years, he showed up to move-in day with a guitar and amp, having already discovered STR on Instagram. None of them came to Duke specifically for music, yet each of them found it anyway. “I didn't even really know what people my age were doing. I didn't know what performances people were going for and how people were recording their music,” Stern said of the time before she came to Duke. “I was learning that world, and then I got to college, and still had no intention of releasing music. [Then I] auditioned for Small Town Records, and then it just became such a central part of my life.” But Duke’s academic culture, relentlessly pre-professional and chronically busy, does not make space for art by default, they said. These students have to carve it out. Small described the Duke environment as “very corporate,” and has spent the past few summers — including the upcoming one — interning at J.P. Morgan. And rap and finance are not an obvious dynamic duo. “Trying to find time for both has been particularly challenging, but ultimately, it's something that you're gonna have to deal with,” he said. His solution is the same one he’d offer for most things: he treats his music like it matters just as much as anything else. “It seems like a no-brainer. The commitment that I have for myself and my music is of equal importance to any class that I take at Duke,” Small explained. Stapleton, pre-law and studying public policy and neuroscience, wants to go into survivor’s rights law to defend victims of sexual assault and abuse. Although academics take priority, he treats playing the guitar like a relaxing brain break to which he devotes much of his spare time. “Music's been a really good outlet for me, and always has,” Stapleton said. “When I was in high school, I called it my therapy.” Stapleton and his band, which formed during orientation week, treat their rehearsals like a club. They show up at least once a week and work towards gigs whenever they have “free time to be able to get together and just play some music.” Davis, who is heading towards law school, finds that having performances on her calendar makes her music commitment non-negotiable. “I might have an exam this week, but I also have a performance this week," she said, “and so both become priorities for me.” Without the accountability of STR, which lines up shows and creates expectations, it might be easy to let music fall to the wayside. And that’s why nearly every student musician emphasized STR’s importance. If you asked Small, Davis, Stapleton or Stern to describe STR, they would tell you it’s so much more than a record label — it’s the underpinning ecosystem of Duke’s music scene. STR has fewer than 10 signed artists at any given time. Everyone else is an audio engineer, producer, social media manager, member of the house band, visual media creator or logistics and marketing. “It's such a great community of people, and just a world of resource,” Stern said. “It's amazing — I feel like I've taken advantage of so much, and I haven't even taken advantage of a fraction of it yet.” Davis explained that for the artists, who are “the minority” at STR, “there’s a much larger group of people backing them up and making everything happen.” Since becoming a signed artist her sophomore year, Davis has released two songs through STR, with access to a marketing team for promotion and a visuals team for cover art. But for both Stern and Davis, the community extends beyond production and promotion at the studio. “It's seeing people around campus. It's performing around campus. It's like meeting up with a band in a random location to practice,” Stern said, a dynamic Davis described as part of Duke’s “small but mighty” student musician community. Small occupies a unique position within it. He describes himself as the only hip-hop rapper on campus, operating in a landscape largely dominated by indie singer-songwriters and country artists. Rather than treating that as isolation, he takes his place in STR as a creative advantage. “[By] being able to get criticism or get input from people that are doing other types of music, we can make sure that the sound is actually reaching a diverse audience,” he said. He tends to work lean, producing his beats and music in his dorm room with an audio interface, mic stand and laptop — no entourage required. Davis wants to see the scene grow outward, beyond the self-selecting group that already knows STR exists. She points to the Duke Music Coalition as a promising development: a newer organization that creates performance opportunities for any student, regardless of how deep their musical background runs. "Duke isn't really known for being an artsy school," she said, "but I think there are a lot of students who just don't know how to connect with people who are doing the same things they are." Stapleton noted that connection is also shaped — and sometimes limited — by campus infrastructure. He pointed to the logistical hurdles of putting on outdoor performance, from reserving BC Plaza in advance to navigating noise ordinances and wrangling approvals. "Welcome to Small Town" — one of STR's signature annual events held on the quad — takes serious coordination just to happen. Despite their love for it, none of the four sees music as their primary career. All of them are mostly certain it probably won’t go anywhere, though Small is the most openly ambitious. Small talks about scalability and a digital economy that would make music a viable future for him. "I'm always making music. I'm only trying to get better," he says. If music doesn't scale the way he wants, he'll be in finance in New York — and he'll still be making music. For him, the two paths are not mutually exclusive. Small’s inspirations are A Boogie wit da Hoodie, a fellow Bronx native and his most-streamed artist since sixth grade, as well as Drake and Gunna — artists he said are defined by range and a refusal to be boxed in. His biggest full-circle moment was opening for A Boogie during the P-Checks concert last year. Stapleton performs Australian indie-influenced, guitar-driven rock with his band and cites Royal Otis and Mac DeMarco as influences. But he’s honest about his ceiling. “I'm pre-law. This isn't a thing where I'm gonna drop out and move to LA," he said. But Stapleton talks about music the way he talks about everything else he cares about: with total commitment. "I don't ever commit to something unless I can give my 110%,” he said. Davis is preparing to open for this year's LDOC — another full-circle moment for someone who spent four years quietly building toward stages she didn't know she'd reach. "My high school self would probably freak out," she says. After graduation, she plans to pursue civil rights and family law. For Davis, music will run alongside her career, not underneath it. And Stern, the perfectionist who lets her songs marinate for weeks before she decides if she loves them, turns uninspired afternoons into Joni Mitchell guitar tunings and pulls a drummer, bassist and guitarist into the studio for collaborative recordings just because she can. The physics major is at a crossroads with what comes next. For now, though, she’s grateful for the uncertainty. “Part of me will always be open to music, and I think it's just kind of based on whatever opportunity presents itself,” Stern said. “The short answer is I don't see it as a career. And then the long answer is, I don't see anything yet as a career.”

title: Duke Gardens staff, volunteers collaborate to help plants shine
subtitle: What looks like effortless beauty at the Gardens — from the blooms to the carefully arranged landscapes — is the product of daily labor and year-round planning.
author: Rebecca Fan
text: Every day, visitors arrive at Duke Gardens, and the plants have already been checked, paths cleared and beds quietly tended. What looks like effortless beauty at the Gardens — from the blooms to the carefully arranged landscapes — is the product of daily labor and year-round planning. Behind the quiet paths and the lively South lawn, who are those responsible for the beautiful blossoms year-round? The work falls to a coordinated team of staff horticulturists, interns and volunteers, supported by a facilities specialist and a plant collections manager who maintains a detailed database of the Gardens’ plant collections. Together, the team, led by Director of Horticulture Robert Mottern, keeps one of Duke’s most visited spaces open every day of the year, welcoming more than 650,000 visitors annually. In his role, Mottern oversees both the day-to-day maintenance of the Gardens’ plant life and long-term planning for landscape infrastructure, including patios, pathways, irrigation systems, drainage, garden structures and seasonal plantings. The Duke Gardens are divided into four distinct sections — the Historic Gardens, H.L. Blomquist Garden of Native Plants, W.L. Culberson Asiatic Arboretum and the Gateway Gardens — each managed by a team of horticulturalists who are experts in their respective areas and largely manage their sections independently. “I really let each individual team make most of the decisions in their areas because I want everyone to have a part of ownership in their area ... and their thoughts and ideas represented,” Mottern said. Behind the visible displays, Mottern noted the “constant manipulation of plants,” with spaces intentionally shaped and maintained through specific plant selection, design and routine trimming for both aesthetic and safety reasons. He added that maintenance also includes removing dead trees, cleaning up and repairing damage caused by storms, repainting the iconic Meyer bridge , as well as routinely managing disease and pests. In addition to full-time staff, the daily upkeep of the Duke Gardens relies on a broad network of students, interns and volunteers to help with daily operations. During the academic year, about 15 to 16 work-study students assist with tasks ranging from mulching to assisting weekend visitors . Each summer, the Gardens also hire interns from horticulture programs across the country to support routine maintenance, including watering the Gardens’ many container plantings. Volunteers are also a significant part of the Gardens’ backbone. Mottern said between 30 and 50 horticulture volunteers regularly contribute hours each week, while others support departments across the Gardens, including education, special events and visitor services. “It takes a lot of people, a lot of hands and a lot of coordination to maintain the Gardens like we do,” he said. Much of the care that comes with maintenance is labor intensive. This includes hand-pulling weeds, mulching, pruning and rotating the seasonal plants in and out. The process begins in the greenhouse, where plants are moved into containers before being transferred to different areas of the Gardens and eventually replanted each season. According to Mottern, replanting typically takes place in the spring and fall, the busiest periods for the Gardens team. Each year, Duke Gardens also prepares months in advance for the influx of visitors during cherry blossom season, an effort so extensive it has come to be known as “ Operation Cherry Blossom .” “It’s constant work,” he said. “... We’re always busy with something. It’s just that some seasons are a little bit busier than others.” For Lilyann Gardner, a first-year Master of Fine Arts student, working behind the scenes has become valuable experience. Gardner works as a student intern in the Historic Gardens, where her responsibilities shift with the seasons. Depending on the time of year, Gardner helps with watering, weeding, pruning and planting — much of the hands-on labor at the core of Duke Gardens’ upkeep. Currently, much of her work has focused on preparing the terraces by removing old plantings, tilling soil and installing bulbs for future blooms — work Gardner described as “really gratifying.” Behind the work is also a strong emphasis on sustainability, something Gardner said many visitors may not notice. She explained that when seasonal flowers such as tulips and daffodils are removed after a season, all plant material is composted and reused to create new soil and mulch for future plantings. Mottern said that behind all the planning and labor, the Duke Gardens are ultimately meant to be a place where visitors can explore, learn and take inspiration home. By showcasing “unique and unusual plants” and designing them in what he called “the most exciting way to use certain plant material,” Mottern hopes that people will “take home ideas and maybe represent what they see here at Duke Gardens ... in their own yards.” He added that Duke Gardens’ year-round, free admission makes it a rare resource among university gardens. “We just want people to come in here, enjoy the Gardens and respect the Gardens but just really enjoy this space,” Mottern said. “... There’s lots of universities that have arboretums and gardens, but not really [of Duke Gardens’] caliber.” Gardner described her time working at the gardens as centered on “community” and “teamwork.” Although each individual action may be small, together, the efforts of staff and volunteers certainly don’t go unnoticed. “There’s a lot of people that are doing work behind the scenes, and they’re really making it beautiful, day in and day out,” Gardner said. “It’s just wonderful to then be able to see the Duke community, Durham community come out and enjoy the fruits of that labor.”

title: First-years report strong sense of community, highly selective pre-professional societies
subtitle: For the ninth consecutive year, The Chronicle asked members of the first-year class about their social experiences on campus so far, ranging from Greek life affiliation and residential life to opinions on orientation and involvement with affinity groups.
author: Annie Eilers
text: Editor's note: This story is part of a series about the Class of 2029 based on a survey conducted by The Chronicle in January and February 2026. You can read more about our methodology and limitations here or read all our survey coverage here . For the ninth consecutive year, The Chronicle asked members of the first-year class about their social experiences on campus so far, ranging from Greek life affiliation and residential life to opinions on orientation and involvement with affinity groups. A semester into their Duke careers, a large majority of the Class of 2029 — over four-fifths of our respondents — reported having found community. The most common places first-years reported having found community on campus were orientation week, dorms and clubs or extracurriculars. The Class of 2029 is the fourth first-year class to matriculate under the QuadEx system, which launched in 2021. All first-years are randomly assigned to East Campus dorms that connect to a specific quad on West Campus. The University created QuadEx with the intention of fostering a “culture of belonging,” although the system has received pushback over the years . Members of the Class of 2029 have more positive opinions of QuadEx than previous first-year classes, with 56.40% of respondents reporting somewhat or strongly favorable opinions of the residential system, up from 52.23% last year . Over a fourth of respondents were neutral on the program, up from one in five last year. First-years’ perceptions of QuadEx vary by their assigned quad connection. First-years connected to Edens Quad were by far the most likely to have unfavorable opinions of the residential system, while those connected to Wannamaker, Craven and Keohane Quads reported the most favorable attitudes. At least half of respondents in all income groups reported favorable opinions of QuadEx. First-years’ opinions of QuadEx varied less across income groups than two years ago . About three-quarters of our first-years reported feeling at least some sense of community in their East Campus dorm. Residents of Pegram and Alspaugh filled out the survey at the highest relative rates, with 29 out of approximately 125 Pegram residents and 25 of approximately 120 Alspaugh residents responding. Over half of respondents in Alspaugh reported a strong sense of community in their dorm, with 84.00% reporting at least some sense of community. In Pegram, 93.10% of respondents reported feeling at least some sense of community in their dorm, with a third characterizing it as strong. In Brown, Trinity and Bell Tower, at least two in five respondents reported feeling very little to no sense of community in their dorms. Trinity and Bell Tower both connect to Edens Quad, in which the highest proportion of first-years reported having unfavorable opinions of QuadEx. Five years after Duke’s fraternities and sororities disaffiliated from the University, over two-thirds of our respondents said they did not attempt to join Greek life this spring. Of those who did rush a sorority or fraternity, three-fourths received a bid. Women were more likely to rush than men, and of those who did rush, men were more likely to not receive a bid. Most respondents, 63.20%, said they had not rushed a pre-professional society at Duke, such as Delta Sigma Pi Business Fraternity or Phi Delta Epsilon Pre-Medicine Fraternity. Of those who rushed, only 36.96% received a bid — down about 12 percentage points from last year’s survey. Respondents who had rushed pre-professional societies at Duke were much more likely to be interested in also rushing a sorority or fraternity. While a minority of respondents had rushed Greek life, over half of those who had rushed a pre-professional society did. Notably, being accepted into a pre-professional society was also associated with receiving a bid from a sorority or fraternity. Interest in selective living groups appears to be lower than in Greek life among the Class of 2029. A minority of respondents, 22.40%, were at least moderately interested in rushing an SLG, but over half reported no interest at all. Members of the Class of 2029 had somewhat more interest in living-learning communities than in SLGs. A slightly higher proportion of respondents, 26.40%, had at least a moderate interest in joining an LLC, and 42.00% of students reported having no interest whatsoever. In line with trends from previous years, first-years with higher estimated family incomes were more likely to rush a Greek sorority or fraternity — especially those with household incomes over $500,000. Only about one in five students who rushed came from families with household incomes roughly at or below the federal median, though our full sample was similarly skewed — roughly one-fourth of our respondents reported incomes at or below the federal minimum. Interest in SLGs and LLCs was more consistent across income levels than interest in Greek life. Interest in LLCs was somewhat correlated with income — those with estimated family incomes above $500,000 were the least interested in LLCs, while those with estimated family incomes below $40,000 were most interested in LLCs. Interest in SLGs displayed less of a consistent trend in variation across income levels. Interestingly, those with estimated family incomes below $40,000 and above $500,000 were the only groups in which the majority of respondents who rushed a pre-professional society were accepted. First-years with estimated family incomes exceeding $500,000 were by far the most interested in pre-professional societies, with exactly half of our respondents in that income bracket having rushed. The Class of 2029 is the fourth to begin their Duke experience with mandatory Experiential Orientation (ExO) programming. During their first week on campus, first-years spend time in one of several project groups meant to build community and a sense of belonging before classes start. Members of the Class of 2029 have overall very positive perceptions of ExO. Four in five respondents expressed favorable opinions of the program, with over half reporting strongly favorable opinions. Only about a tenth of respondents reported unfavorable feelings. Nearly half of first-years, 44.80%, reported being very or extremely interested in tenting to secure a spot inside Cameron Indoor Stadium for a home men’s basketball game against rival North Carolina during their time at Duke. An additional 26.00% of respondents expressed moderate interest; 15.20% expressed slight interest; and only 14.00% were not interested at all. Nearly half of our respondents reported no involvement with a student-run affinity group on campus. One in five of our first-years said they were not involved at the time of completing the survey but that they intended to be in the future, and slightly fewer said they were already a member. The remainder had attended one or more events held by an affinity group but were not a member. Black or African American students were by far the most likely to be members of an affinity group, with about three-fifths of our respondents in this group indicating so. About a fifth of Asian and Hispanic/Latinx students each reported the same. Students in these two groups were the most likely to say they intended to join an affinity group in the future. White respondents were the least likely to be involved in an affinity group or want to in the future. (A very small number of respondents self-identified as Middle Eastern/North African, American Indian or Alaska Native, Native Hawaiian or Pacific Islander, or a race or ethnicity not listed, so the results we reported for these groups may not be reflective of the full first-year population with these identities.) Just over half of our respondents, 53.20%, said they had visited or attended an event for an identity and cultural center. The Center for Multicultural Affairs was selected the most by our respondents, followed closely by Jewish Life at Duke. None of our respondents reported involvement with the Women’s Center. Respondents who were involved with the Mary Lou Williams Center for Black Culture or the Center for Multicultural Affairs were most likely to also be a member of a student-run affinity group. Those involved with the Center for Muslim Life or Jewish Life at Duke were least likely to report affinity group involvement.

title: Duke expands writing and select seminar class sizes amid cost pressures
subtitle: First-year writing courses will expand to 17 students next fall, a stark increase from the 12-student cap Duke had in 2021. The Journalism and Media Studies program has also been asked to expand its class sizes.
author: Mia Taubenblat
text: When Sheryl Emch was abruptly informed that the Thompson Writing Program would increase class sizes from 12 to 15 students in fall 2021, she and other TWP faculty were concerned the shift would be a slippery slope. Emch, a lecturing fellow in TWP, was correct. Starting in fall 2026, there's a proposal to cap Writing 120 courses, the required first-year writing seminar previously known as Writing 101, to 17 students. The class size expansions are not unique to TWP. In the Journalism & Media Studies program, all writing seminars but one increased their ceiling from 12 to 15 students this academic year. Some first-year seminars in the Constellation program had class sizes larger than 18, the well-established upper limit for a “seminar” in the Trinity College of Arts & Sciences. According to faculty familiar with the matter, Duke initiated these changes over the past five years to cut costs. Increasing class sizes, even by just two or three students each, alleviates the need for professors to teach supplementary classes or for departments to hire more adjuncts to serve their students. The changes took place despite widespread disapproval from professors surrounding pedagogical concerns, faculty workload and classroom space. “Just three more students doesn’t sound like a lot, but I think it can definitely shift the dynamic in a classroom,” said Kim Krzywy, academic engagement director for the Journalism & Media Studies program. “Our preference is always for a smaller class size.” For TWP, the initial expansion from 12 to 15 students began during the COVID-19 pandemic, when Duke needed to make budget cuts following a decline in revenue entering the 2021-22 academic year. The TWP increased class sizes to refrain from hiring additional adjunct professors that semester. There was no formal process for gathering input from TWP faculty about the increase before its rollout, Emch said. Writing 101 courses had enforced an enrolled cap of 12 students since 2000. “I felt like it was very disrespectful to not at least tell us what was going on and why they felt they had to make that decision,” Emch said. “It just happened.” This time around there was a bit more foreshadowing with the growth of the Constellations courses. Both TWP faculty and TWP Director Denise Comer voiced their concerns about the increase to 17 students in faculty meetings, though Comer did not sign an open letter obtained by The Chronicle from TWP faculty to oppose the change and did not respond to The Chronicle’s request for comment on the increase. In a Tuesday morning reply after publication to The Chronicle's request for comment, Owen Covington, Trinity’s associate dean for communications & marketing, wrote that enrollment in some TWP courses next fall will be increased to 16 or 17 students based on need but the formalized cap to 17 students will occur in the 2027-28 academic year "based upon overall enrollment growth." TWP professors interviewed for this story were not made aware of that decision at the time of publication. Covington wrote that decisions on class size are made with input from department chairs and program directors and they take into account factors of student interest, overall enrollment numbers and departmental resources. He denied the narrative that they are driven by "cost-cutting measures" but rather through a "strategic approach to faculty hiring and a thorough examination of teaching capacity." "Studies have shown that minimally increasing class size has little to no impact on student learning and protecting the integrity of student learning continues to be a priority," Covington added. The Chronicle has asked Covington for more details on the studies being referenced. The increase to a hard cap of 17 students is due to Duke attempting to increase enrollment for the Class of 2030 and the incoming transfer class, said Charlotte Asmuth, an assistant professor of the practice of the TWP. According to Asmuth, TWP faculty were told in meetings that Duke is aiming to have an incoming class size of 1776 in honor of the United States’ 250th anniversary. Many transfer students need to take Writing 120 along with first-years. In fall 2026, TWP is planning not to allow any instructors to teach supplemental classes or hire additional instructors in order to cut costs, necessitating the increased size, Emch was told. The change to 15 students increased TWP faculty’s work by 25%, said Marcia Rego, associate professor of the practice in the TWP. Now, the increase to 17 students changes the identity of Writing 120 classes. “The most important change is in the quality of the education that we’re giving students. Students don’t have as much quality one-on-one feedback,” Rego said. “If it increases again, it’s going to make it practically impossible.” As a University that prides itself on small student-professor ratios, Rego said she “doesn’t really understand the change.” Asmuth and Emch both highlighted how Writing 120 courses serve an additional role in first-years’ lives beyond academics, providing a support system. Professors in smaller seminar classes are often the first to notice that students are struggling physically or mentally, Asmuth explained. Emch added that for some first-year students, Writing 120 is their only small class where they know other students well and interact with the faculty. “It’s even more important, right when students are in their first year, to have a class that helps foster that kind of community, both academically and personally,” Emch said. Emch’s course, Coming of Age & Happiness, asks students to write about their personal lives. For her, teaching a small class size of 12 students was “pretty dreamy,” since her course is designed around forming connections. “In order for [students] to trust each other with your ideas, your writing, we need to really know each other and feel safe with each other,” Emch said. She also noted that the smaller size helps students feel comfortable sharing incomplete ideas and having their ideas challenged. The burden is felt in the JAM program as well. In summer 2025, the Journalism & Media Studies program first considered increasing most of its seminar class sizes from 12 to 15 students, according to Krzywy. The Sanford School of Public Policy, which JAM is a part of, told the program that class sizes needed to expand to “respond to financial challenges,” said Phil Napoli, the director of the DeWitt Wallace Center for Media and Democracy. JAM classes are writing intensive, so an additional three students add a substantial amount of time providing feedback for JAM instructors. Napoli noted that the burden is especially heavy for adjunct professors, who are compensated less. For professors in non-writing intensive courses, the workload increase was not as palpable. But Alison Hill, a senior lecturer of biology, took concern with the lack of autonomy over the increases. She previously taught the course The Biology of Aging as a first-year seminar with a cap of 16 students. But in the fall she had to join the Constellations program to continue teaching the course. Right before the first day of classes, she received an email from the administrators of Constellations asking her to raise her cap to 24 students. Hill ultimately compromised and taught her course with 20 students. That number exceeds the longstanding enrollment cap of 18 students that categorizes a course as a seminar. Two other Constellation courses matched the trend. “What Now? Why Are We Here: Finding Purpose and Meaning in Education” increased to 20 students and “Child Policy Research” increased to 25 students, despite still being advertised to Duke students as first-year seminars. The class size increase has not been announced publicly. Emch first heard of a possible increase to a hard cap of 17 students for Writing 120 courses in January. There were other signs a class size increase was on the horizon. At the end of January two instructors in the TWP were informed they would not have their contracts renewed for next year to reduce TWP expenses, Emch said. Additionally, the program had wanted to increase class sizes this spring to avoid instructors taking on supplemental classes. In the end, it opted against the move, running three supplemental classes taught by two graduate student instructors and Emch — the hires that get paid the least. Next fall, with no additional compensation for increased time commitment for teaching more students, Emch sees her compensation effectively decreasing if she increases her hours. She intends to do so but knows it will take its toll. Asmuth sees the decision having a direct impact on Duke students. “Tuition is increasing, and yet individualized attention from professors is decreasing,” Asmuth said. Editor's note: This story was updated Tuesday morning with comment from Owen Covington, Trinity’s associate dean for communications & marketing.

title: Why Duke is expanding its health system while the University cuts costs
subtitle: Behind the scenes of the University’s austerity, the health system has been growing its reach through a series of substantial acquisitions and investments that will double the number of hospitals in the system in the coming years.
author: Zoe Kolenovsky
text: The past year has revealed a tale of two Dukes — the University cutting back, and its health system building out. At the University, institutional leaders scrambled to cut costs in response to federal funding shortfalls. Departments began bleeding faculty and staff over the summer after a one-two punch of voluntary buyouts and layoffs saw 726 employees depart by September. Administrators pushed toward an ambitious 10% reduction across the University’s expense base, as it braced for less research funding opportunities and potential losses through reimbursement rates. At Duke Health, things look a little different. Behind the scenes of the University’s austerity, the health system has been growing its reach through a series of substantial acquisitions and investments that will double the number of hospitals in the system in the coming years. Such spending appears to clash with central administration’s messaging around the need for belt-tightening. In actuality, the moves reflect separate financial structures supporting two institutions. They also reflect a plan set in motion long before President Donald Trump took office for a second term that falls in line with a broader trend of health care market consolidation across the country. The expansion may end up helping Duke invest in long-term cost savings that could soften the blow of federal funding cuts to its academic operations. “If you were the CEO of Duke, you would do the same,” said Ge Bai, professor of accounting and health policy and management at Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health and a leading scholar of health care finances. The Duke University Health System (DUHS) has long been anchored by three major hospitals in the Triangle region: Duke University Hospital, adjacent to the University campus; Duke Regional Hospital, in north Durham; and Duke Raleigh Hospital, a satellite campus located in the state’s capital. DUHS also consists of Duke Children’s Hospital — housed within the Duke University Hospital — and hundreds of smaller specialty care clinics and laboratories scattered across North Carolina. Several other hospitals and medical clinics in rural regions of the state receive support from Duke through its partnership with LifePoint Healthcare . Since early 2025, that network has grown. In January 2025, Duke Health and UNC Health announced plans to partner on the first standalone children’s hospital in the state, to be called N.C. Children’s. A site in Apex, N.C. , was selected for the new complex in July, and construction is slated to begin by 2027. Three months later, DUHS announced it had acquired Lake Norman Regional Medical Center for $284 million and renamed it Duke Health Lake Norman Hospital. The move constituted a bold step for Duke Health — the 123-bed acute-care facility in Mooresville, N.C., near Charlotte, represents the first Duke-owned hospital outside of the Triangle. In June, Duke Health began an expansion of Duke Health Cary for a new inpatient hospital that will offer emergency, surgical and imaging services, along with other treatments, with an expected completion date in 2027. The 85-acre site is already home to two primary care clinics and several other specialty clinics. Then, in December, Duke submitted plans to Durham County to build a new cancer center at its main Durham campus that will become the first proton therapy provider in the state. The price tag is sizable. N.C. Children’s appears to be the most expensive of DUHS’s pending projects, with an estimated total cost of $2 billion, though neither academic institution is contributing capital toward the venture. The 500-bed facility is set to be part of a larger 100-plus-acre complex featuring other care centers and office buildings, all of which has yet to be constructed. Thus far, $320 million in state funds have been allocated to UNC Health for the project. Integrating the Lake Norman facility into the broader health system has not brought significant additional costs, according to Duke Health officials. In a statement to The Chronicle, they wrote they expect the hospital to be “self-sustaining financially” after a “transition period.” Costs for the Duke Health Cary expansion could not be determined from available information, though the project has been funded at least in part through philanthropy . The cancer center carries an expected price tag of $120 million, a portion of which has been funded by a $50 million anonymous donation — the largest philanthropic gift in DUHS history. According to DUHS, the plan to expand has been in place for years. “Investments of this scale require multi-year financial modeling, board approval and operational review,” Duke Health officials wrote in a statement to The Chronicle. “They are not decisions made quickly or in reaction to short-term conditions.” The distinction reflects separate financial structures. Although DUHS is listed as a controlled affiliate in University filings, it funds its operations and capital projects independently — through clinical revenue, debt and philanthropy rather than University operating funds, according to Duke Health officials. There is one important caveat to the decoupled financial structure: although DUHS doesn’t pull from University resources to fund its capital projects, the University regularly receives financial support from the health side, most notably in the form of annual contributions to the School of Medicine totaling $2 billion over the last decade. According to Duke Health officials, those donations support DUHS’s mission as an academic health system. Duke Health frames its expansion as mission driven. Officials describe “strategic growth” as essential to meeting North Carolina’s rising demand for health care amid population growth and the system’s responsibility to expand access to care statewide. But the financial logic is just as clear. Although DUHS is in solid financial shape today, that wasn’t the case a few years ago. In 2022, DUHS was suffering from a “perfect storm” of financial headwinds, including high service and talent costs in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic, an “increasingly challenging” environment for service reimbursements and the added pressure of inflation. By 2024, finances had stabilized , but administrators warned that Duke’s relatively small size compared to peer systems could leave it vulnerable in future downturns. DUHS wasn’t the only one. In the wake of the pandemic, mergers motivated by “financial distress” began climbing . They added to a broader trend of academic health system growth across the country. A 2024 KFF analysis tracking “increasingly consolidated” health care markets recorded 428 hospital and health system mergers between 2018 and 2023 alone. The uptick has been driven in part by population growth, workforce shortages and greater demand for specialty services. But it has also developed as the response to an intensifying fight for market power between health care providers and insurers. Hospitals get most of their revenue from health insurance providers. According to a KFF analysis of national health expenditure data from 2023, private insurers were the largest payors for hospital care at 37%. Thus, the ability to secure higher rates from private insurers can make a world of difference in generating more revenue for hospitals. And when health systems account for a larger share of the market, they have “an upper hand at the negotiating table,” said Bai, the health finance scholar. Those negotiations had become more contentious following the passage of the Affordable Care Act, according to Adrian Diaz, a surgeon who is part of a research group studying health care market consolidation. He explained that some of the act’s provisions empowered payors to consolidate, kicking off a positive feedback loop of growth. “It almost became like a snowball effect: as the payors got more market power, the providers had to get more market power, et cetera, et cetera,” he said. Back in 2024, DUHS had already faced tense negotiations with large insurers, showing how much leverage they held over hospital revenues. A contract dispute between DUHS and UnitedHealth Group — the nation’s largest single insurer — almost resulted in Duke facilities being recategorized as out-of-network. At the time, Duke claimed it was meeting with UnitedHealth “in good faith” but that the insurance giant was “unwilling to work with Duke toward a responsible new agreement despite billions of dollars in profits.” The dispute was ultimately resolved before patients saw their coverage lapse. Duke found itself in a similar situation with the insurer Aetna last year, as the health system sought higher reimbursement rates to “keep pace with rising health care costs and reflect the actual cost of providing patient care.” After months of negotiations, a new contract was reached, and Aetna patients retained their coverage at Duke facilities. Both episodes highlight the stakes of scale: in an industry where size shapes negotiating power, expansion presented an opportunity for DUHS to put itself in as advantageous a position as possible when those financial relationships are up for reevaluation. Bai laid out three additional ways that expansions like the one DUHS is pursuing can end up boosting revenues for health care providers. First is through federal programs that offer discounted health care products for large providers. The 340B Program requires pharmaceutical companies to provide drugs at discounted prices to some nonprofit hospitals that participate in Medicaid. Only certain hospitals are eligible, such as “disproportionate share hospitals” — those that serve a large number of patients on Medicaid or without insurance — and “sole community hospitals” — those that provide the only inpatient hospital services to a particular geographic region. Eligibility for the reduced drug prices is determined on an individual hospital basis, so if a health system doesn’t qualify for the reduced drug prices, acquiring one that does — such as a hospital in a rural region where health care options are slim — wouldn’t automatically extend those benefits across the full system. But some off-site clinics connected to that hospital can benefit from the reduced prices, allowing the larger health system to potentially route its outpatient services through a lower-cost pipeline. DUHS might not actually need to grow its footprint to access these benefits, though. Bai published a study last year that tracks a growing trend of urban hospitals “dually classifying” as administratively rural over the past decade, which allows them to benefit from the 340B Program’s reduced pricing for rural providers while retaining other benefits they have as urban providers. Many large academic medical centers were on the list — including Duke University Hospital in Durham. Second is referral capturing. Health systems are acquiring physician practices at an increasing rate , establishing partnerships that reduce physicians' administrative burden and attract more patients — and more revenue — to hospitals. The merits of such vertical integration in terms of its impact on the quality of care delivered have been debated , but the financial benefits for hospitals are undeniable. When patients require additional care, these newly acquired physicians refer them to other practices within the larger health system. So, the health system has captured more than just the business the provider brings; its wider network has captured business from the physician’s referrals, too. Finally, and perhaps the most straightforward method, integrating a larger network of care facilities allows health systems to dilute their administrative costs across multiple revenue streams, boosting profit margins. Duke’s dual reality is less a contradiction than a balancing act. Keeping DUHS’s finances strong is critical for the entire institution. As one faculty member put it during an Academic Council meeting at the time, “I've heard that when the health system sneezes, the University gets the flu.” Editor's note: This story was submitted for a course in the DeWitt Wallace Center for Media and Democracy. The Chronicle provided further edits prior to publication.

title: JR DeShazo named inaugural Joel L Fleishman dean of Sanford School of Public Policy
subtitle: DeShazo will replace Manoj Mohanan, who has served as interim dean for the past two years after Judith Kelley stepped down from the role in 2024.
author: Ana Despa
text: JR DeShazo will be the next dean of the Sanford School of Public Policy, Provost Alec Gallimore announced Monday afternoon. DeShazo, currently the dean of the Lyndon B. Johnson School of Public Affairs at the University of Texas at Austin, will begin his time at Sanford on July 1. He will be the first to hold the school’s new endowed deanship, honoring Sanford's founding director Joel L. Fleishman. DeShazo will replace Manoj Mohanan, who has served as interim dean for the past two years after Judith Kelley stepped down from the role in 2024. DeShazo has served as dean at the University of Texas since 2021. Beyond his administrative duties, he researches clean technology policy, environmental equity and environmental economics and has advised the World Bank, the United Nations, the National Science Foundation and the Environmental Protection Agency. “With its world-class faculty, talented students, dedicated staff and accomplished alumni, the Sanford School is well positioned to tackle the most pressing policy challenges of the 21st century,” DeShazo said in a Duke Today release . “I am honored to lead this extraordinary community as we work together collaboratively to elevate Sanford’s educational, research and policy impacts even further.” The hiring ends an eight-month nationwide search by a committee chaired by Kate Bundorf, J. Alexander McMahon distinguished professor of health policy and management. In the press release, Gallimore thanked Mohanan, who was seen as a potential permanent candidate, for his thoughtful leadership. Prior to his appointment as interim dean, Mohanan was the Creed C. Black professor of public policy and senior associate dean for faculty and research. Under Mohanan’s interim leadership, Sanford expanded its educational offerings across emerging policy areas and interdisciplinary pathways, including rolling out new programming like the Technology Policy minor and continuing initiatives to facilitate civil discourse through the flagship Civil Discourse Project. His tenure, though, was largely shaped by a University-wide cost-cutting initiative and a changing higher education landscape. The Duke Center for International Development — housed within Sanford — had to pause its activities in March 2025, after federal cuts to the United States Agency for International Development forced it to seek new funding sources. Sanford leadership also faced controversy last fall when they sent an email to all faculty members directing them to not speak to the media on any inquiries not explicitly related to their research. Before UT Austin, DeShazo spent two decades at UCLA. According to his UT Austin biography , his academic path was shaped in part by overcoming a childhood learning disability, which he has said fuels his commitment to expanding educational access. He holds a doctorate in urban planning from Harvard University, a master's in development economics from Oxford University and a bachelor's from the College of William & Mary, where he was the school's first Rhodes Scholar.

title: Duke’s investments split between STEM growth and humanities expansion
subtitle: After a year of cost-cutting, The Chronicle looked at where Duke is spending its dollars, finding that while a significant amount of external funding has gone to STEM and AI, the University still devotes a lot of its internal budget to the humanities.
author: Luca Gragnoli, Leyuan Zhang
text: After a year of cost-cutting and “strategic realignment” of priorities, Duke has changed where it spends its money. STEM fields, artificial intelligence and quantum-related initiatives have dominated philanthropic giving but hiring decisions and programming expansions seem to favor the humanities. To understand how those priorities play out in practice, The Chronicle examined where Duke has invested the most — and least — across its academic programs, drawing on public announcements, grant data and University communications. The University has largely focused on expanding AI and technology research through a multitude of initiatives, grants and awards. In 2024, the Duke Endowment awarded the University $30 million to increase hiring of new faculty who would elevate research in computing, AI and machine learning. That initiative — known as the Elevating Duke Computing program — fell under the broader Duke Science and Technology initiative, which received a $100 million investment from The Duke Endowment in 2019. In the following year, Duke launched two new interdisciplinary technology programs. In fall 2025, the University launched a master’s pilot degree, called the Master of Engineering in AI and Materials program. The program equips students with the knowledge to “change the world” since “every new technological revolution is built on new materials,” said Cate Brinson, Sharon C. and Harold L. Yoh, III distinguished professor of mechanical engineering and materials science in an accompanying press release. Then, in January, the Sanford School of Public Policy introduced a Technology Policy minor, where students can explore how technology impacts society, and how public policies shape the development and use of technology. Looking forward, Duke plans to implement an interdisciplinary graduate education collaborative in Society-Centered AI in fall 2026. Twelve students, as beneficiaries of a $2.4 million grant, will be able to pursue this certificate each year by studying a combination of courses in computer science, social science and philosophy. On the research front, the University received a $2.66 million grant from the National Science Foundation to build a 96-qubit quantum computer by 2027 in the Duke Quantum Center. By 2033, the team also plans to build a 256-qubit quantum computer, one of the largest academic quantum computers according to Ken Brown, Michael J. Fitzpatrick distinguished professor of engineering, in an accompanying press release . Although fewer and fewer students have been declaring humanities majors over time, humanities classes are not going anywhere. In February, President Vincent Price characterized the humanities as one of the University’s most critical investments. An example of the growing emphasis and investment in the humanities is the new Trinity curriculum since it increases the number of mandatory humanities courses. The University also engaged in a deliberate attempt to target enrollment for arts and humanities students this past transfer admission cycle. Additional initiatives in the humanities include a $5 million award to the Office of Black Church Studies within the Divinity School in 2024 to recruit and support a senior faculty member and a 2025 $2 million award to fund the Provost’s Initiative on Pluralism, Free Inquiry and Belonging for three years. Both awards came from the Duke Endowment. Finally, despite declining enrollment, some humanities departments continue to bolster their faculty ranks. Per data gathered by The Chronicle from Duke’s official public hiring announcements, the humanities saw the greatest share of new hires in 2022, 2024 and 2025, followed by the natural sciences and social sciences. The Chronicle was only able to view public hiring announcements, and data on University hires by field is not publicly available. In particular, in 2025, 50% of new hires were for humanities departments, 31% for the natural sciences and 19% for the social sciences. This trend has held steady at least since 2017 and has resulted in some humanities departments having a significant number of faculty for a small number of students majoring in that field. For example, in 2025 there were 45 students who graduated with a bachelor's degree in history, according to data from the University registrar. The department almost has one faculty member per student, employing 32 primary faculty members. Similarly, in 2025 the romance studies department awarded bachelor's degrees to 26 students. It has 10 primary faculty. The apparent mismatch has to do with Duke's departmental funding formulations, which do not take into account the number of students currently pursuing the program when allocating budgets. According to Owen Covington, associate dean for communications & marketing, the University’s cost-cutting affected how departments were able to invest in faculty. “As part of this work, some departments have seen reductions in hiring and staffing,” Covington wrote. “We have been deliberate about these decisions, focusing on aligning resources with our highest priorities while addressing a long-standing structural budget gap.” Duke rolled out voluntary separation incentive programs accepted by about 5% of the University’s full-time staff in the last year. Similarly, last June, Price announced a retirement incentive program for eligible faculty members, which 82 of 273eligible faculty took. However, amid these budgetary struggles, Covington wrote that the University is “continuing to invest in areas that are central to our mission.” He mentioned examples including funding for doctoral students, support for junior faculty and the launch of a new collaborative research program. “These investments are intended to strengthen both the student experience and the long-term vitality of the faculty,” Covington wrote. He added that this “more targeted” yet “more sustainable” approach helps Duke face “the realities of the current environment.”

title: Inaugural DSG ‘Devil’s Dresser’ event provides free professional attire to students
subtitle: The event sought to tackle some of the “hidden costs” that Duke students face surrounding building a professional clothing wardrobe.
author: Ella Moore
text: After emailing over 1,200 people for donations, “hours of tabling” and four car trips, Duke Student Government presented 655 pieces of clothing for students to peruse and select for its first-ever “ Devil’s Dresser ” event Tuesday. Senior and outgoing DSG president Tara Singh described the Devil’s Dresser as the organization’s biggest project of the session. The event sought to tackle some of the “hidden costs” that Duke students face surrounding building a professional clothing wardrobe. “[The program] means if Duke undergraduates are able to pursue higher education, their next job, their summer internship, that they're able to pursue it with confidence and they don't have the barrier of clothing costs...That's just really meaningful to me,” Singh said. Sophomore Labiba Siddiquei, director of first-generation and low-income affairs for DSG, foresees the Devil’s Dresser as a continuous project that could host events on a semester-by-semester basis in the future. DSG plans to set up a permanent space in Smith Warehouse for students to “browse and borrow” professional clothes as well as a permanent donation box in the Bryan Center. Usually, getting a major project like this off of the ground takes DSG 1-3 years, according to Singh. Thanks to the project’s institutional partners and leadership, the Devil’s Dresser was able to take off much faster. “This project feels like a lightning strike. We have lightning strike projects throughout our time in student [government] where things just happen to go really right,” Singh said. Angela Xiong, a junior and former president of Duke Lower Income, First Generation Engagement, first proposed the idea in the beginning of the school year. It was then overseen by Siddiquei. The students also collaborated with the Duke Alumni Engagement Office, DukeLIFE, and the University Career Center to conduct outreach to both faculty and alumni to source professional attire and accessories that students could reuse. On top of the 655 items presented at the East Campus thrifting event, Siddiquei said DSG still has piles of clothes in the office waiting to be distributed. “Recruitment is a big part of Duke life, not only because of clubs, but also because of pressure to go into industry.” Siddiquei said. “... Things are economically expensive; anything free helps.” Singh also framed the initiative as part of an increasing focus on tackling affordability challenges for Duke students. Besides launching the Devil’s Dresser, DSG has helped initiate and implement the University’s free laundry program and expanded its airport transportation program, offering free transport between East and West campuses and the Raleigh-Durham Airport during semester breaks to help mitigate travel costs for students. DSG also created a new ad-hoc committee , the Council for Economic Opportunity, in October 2025, tasked with navigating “short-term and long-term” solutions to economic accessibility for students. The new group includes DSG officials, a representative from the Karsh Student Advisory Board and Duke Questbridge. “When I think about an effective student government, I think about a student government that makes a tangible intervention in the undergraduate experience,” Singh said. “So seeing an event like this come together, especially given Labiba’s hard work and everyone who made it happen, is really meaningful.” Junior and incoming DSG president Angela Chen said affordability will continue to be a priority. She pointed to the Council of Economic Opportunity as a particular space for spearheading affordability initiatives and both the Devil’s Dresser and the airport transportation program as projects that DSG hopes to “institutionalize” with the University. “Given the many hidden costs associated with being a Duke student and at the same time, the rising cost of tuition, we realize that this is something that we personally experience and also something that students care a lot about,” Chen said.

title: Middle-income students face financial strain at Duke, most legacies apply Early Decision
subtitle: This story is part of a series about the Class of 2029 based on a survey conducted by The Chronicle in January and February 2026.
author: Amy Zhang
text: Editor's note: This story is part of a series about the Class of 2029 based on a survey conducted by The Chronicle in January and February 2026. You can read more about our methodology and limitations here or read all our survey coverage here . How did the Class of 2029 get to Duke? For the ninth consecutive year, The Chronicle surveyed first-years about their family income, legacy status, college application experience and more. Most members of the class knew Duke was the place for them early on — over half of our respondents initially applied during the Early Decision cycle. However, slightly fewer made it through: 48.80% were accepted during Early Decision, while 8.80% were deferred and later accepted in Regular Decision. They were joined by another 36.00% of respondents accepted in the Regular Decision cycle, and the remaining 6.40% were accepted from the waitlist. Nearly two-thirds of respondents indicated that Duke was their first choice out of all of the colleges they applied to, at 62.80%. When asked to select the top three factors that most influenced their decision to attend, survey respondents cited academics and interdisciplinary curriculum the most, followed by student life, then Duke’s brand name. Pre-professional opportunities and the alumni network ranked fourth for mentions, followed by financial aid, then athletics and finally study abroad opportunities. Duke was the first choice for most — but not all — Early Decision applicants, at 87.50% of respondents. By contrast, over two-thirds of Regular Decision respondents — 70.75% — said Duke was not their first choice. The number of colleges that our respondents applied to varied widely. First-years who applied to Duke during the Regular Decision cycle applied to more schools this year than those from last year’s survey , with 86.80% saying they submitted 10 or more applications. A plurality of Regular Decision applicants reported between five and nine acceptances. We analyzed how various elements of students’ backgrounds, from socioeconomics and geography to existing Duke connections and high school resources, influenced their approach to the application process that eventually brought them to Durham. The Class of 2029 comprises a wide range of socioeconomic backgrounds, though their family incomes skew higher than the broader U.S. population. About one-fourth of our respondents reported family incomes below the federal median of $83,730; a majority of 53.20% reported figures between $80,000 and $500,000; and a final 21.60% had family incomes above $500,000. Still, financial strain was fairly common among respondents — 47.60% said paying for their Duke education had created some financial pressure for them or their families, and another 11.60% said that strain was substantial. Far more first-years in the middle income ranges reported difficulty paying for Duke than their peers with low or high family incomes. We examined how financial background may have influenced students’ choices during the college application process, including whether they applied Early Decision or Regular Decision, worked with a private admissions counselor or opted to withhold standardized test scores. In our sample, students from families with the highest and lowest incomes were more likely to apply Early Decision than those in middle income ranges. The distribution was similar for acceptance type, though the vast majority of students accepted off the waitlist — 93.75% — reported annual family incomes above $125,000. The number of waitlisted students in our sample was relatively small, so their incomes may not be representative of all waitlisted first-years. (Kathy Phillips, interim dean of undergraduate admissions, told The Chronicle that the admissions office does not record waitlist acceptances separately from Regular Decision acceptances, so we could not compare our sample size to the full population.) Approximately 32.00% of respondents hired a private admissions counselor, and the proportion of students who did so rose with income. Less than one-tenth of respondents with household incomes below $40,000 paid for private admissions counseling, while the majority of respondents with household incomes above $750,000 did. Just under a fourth of respondents applied without submitting standardized test scores. In our sample, students in lower family income brackets were more likely to have applied test-optional. Three in four respondents were interviewed by a Duke alumnus or alumna as part of the admissions process. A small proportion, 2.40%, said they were offered an interview but didn’t participate, and the remaining 21.20% were not offered an interview. The proportion of students who completed an alumni interview rose with income. Our respondents’ financial aid awards generally corresponded with their family income levels. More students from low-income backgrounds received institutional financial aid — and typically in larger amounts — than their high-income peers. First-years in our sample came from across the country, with a large share coming from the New York City metropolitan area, the Carolinas, Florida and the District of Columbia-Maryland-Virginia region, similar to past years. Fifty-six of our 250 survey respondents listed a hometown zip code in North or South Carolina. Of these students, 42.86% said that the Carolinas Initiative — which provides full-tuition grants for undergraduates from North and South Carolina with family incomes of $150,000 or less — impacted their decision to apply to Duke. Slightly fewer, 37.50%, said the initiative impacted their decision to accept their Duke admission offer. Most of our respondents came from suburban communities at 65.20%, with another 21.20% from urban communities and 13.60% from rural communities. Suburban students made up the majority of respondents in all family income brackets, though their proportion rose with income. No rural students were present in the highest income brackets. About one in 10 of our respondents are international students. They were far less likely than domestic students to apply through the Early Decision cycle or be interviewed by an alum, but they were more likely to submit the SAT or hire a private admissions counselor. Students with preexisting Duke connections were generally more likely to consider the University their top choice and employ more resources during the application process. About 12.40% of respondents said they had a parent or sibling attend Duke, up from 10.71% in last year’s survey . Four in five legacy respondents considered Duke their first choice during the application process, compared to three in five non-legacy respondents. These first-years tended to apply to Duke earlier than their peers — 80.65% of respondents with a legacy connection applied Early Decision, compared to 54.34% of non-legacies. Legacy respondents were more likely to hire a private admissions counselor, at 58.06% compared to 28.31%, and to be interviewed by an alum, at 90.32% compared to 74.43%. They were also more likely to submit standardized test scores: About a fourth of non-legacy respondents said they submitted neither the SAT nor the ACT, compared to just 9.68% of legacy students. Legacy status was also correlated with family income. Less than 10% of respondents with family incomes under $125,000 were legacy applicants, compared to around a third of those with annual household incomes greater than $750,000. About half of our respondents said they already knew between one and three Duke undergraduates — including members of their own class — before they were accepted to the University. The next-largest group of respondents, 28.00%, knew no one. Another 10.00% knew between four and six Duke undergraduates, 4.40% knew between seven and nine and 6.40% knew more than 10. The proportion of respondents who considered Duke their top choice during the application process rose with increases in the number of preexisting connections to other students. Respondents with more of these connections were generally more likely to apply Early Decision, forgo submitting standardized test scores, hire a private admissions counselor and be interviewed by an alum. They also tended to have higher household incomes. Most respondents, 68.00%, did not hire a private admissions counselor, and only about a third of our respondents said their high school’s college admissions counselor assisted them substantially in the application process. The majority, 53.20%, said they consulted their high school’s counselor very infrequently or not at all. About 14.40% said their school did not have a college counselor on staff. Respondents from private high schools were much more likely than their peers to work closely with a school-provided college admissions counselor. The same was true for students from smaller schools.

title: Faculty committee calls for overhaul of Duke’s PPD policy after finding ‘fundamental deficiencies’
subtitle: The 23-page report identified “fundamental deficiencies” in the University’s PPD policy and recommended creating a separate committee tasked with drafting a new “expressive activity” policy to be voted on by the council at the end of the next academic year.
author: Dylan Halper
text: A faculty committee charged with reviewing Duke’s controversial Pickets, Protests and Demonstrations policy called for its complete overhaul in a March report presented Thursday to Academic Council. The 23-page report identified “fundamental deficiencies” in the University’s PPD policy and recommended creating a separate committee tasked with drafting a new “expressive activity” policy to be voted on by the council at the end of the next academic year.
