Brown, Cornell, the University of Pennsylvania, Dartmouth and Harvard all require SAT or ACT scores for undergraduate applicants. Princeton will require that applicants report scores from one of the two tests beginning with those seeking enrollment in fall 2028, while Columbia does not require standardized test score submission. According to the undergraduate admissions website , 90 percent of Yale first years who enrolled last fall submitted either SAT or ACT scores. Around two-thirds of those students submitted an AP or IB score, and 61 percent submitted scores from multiple exams. “Academic strength is the admissions committee’s first and most important consideration when evaluating candidates,” Jeremiah Quinlan ’03, the dean of undergraduate admissions, said in the press release . “This central pillar of our work has remained constant, even as our policy on tests has changed in recent years.” Quinlan, through a spokesperson, declined to comment beyond his quotes in the release. The council’s review of admissions policies and procedures concluded at the end of the 2025-26 academic year. According to its website , the group issued guidance “to prevent misrepresentation on applications and increase transparency across the admissions process—a focus shared by the Committee on Trust in Higher Education.” The decision to reinstate mandatory SAT or ACT score submission also followed the Committee on Trust in Higher Education’s recommendation in its April report that the University clarify admissions standards. The report floated the requirement of minimum SAT scores for admission or a Yale-specific entrance exam. The Trust Committee identified hyper-selective admissions as one of three “immediate” factors causing declining public trust in Yale and higher education. “When selective admissions seem so inexplicable — or, worse, tilted in ways that benefit the already advantaged — it should come as no surprise that many Americans do not trust the process,” the committee’s report reads. “We recommend that the university embrace a standard of candor: It should only use criteria for admission that it is willing to describe publicly and defend openly.” Following the report’s release, University President Maurie McInnis wrote in a message to the Yale community that she had accepted many of the committee’s findings and referred its recommendations on admissions to the Presidential Council on Yale College Admissions. In a May interview with the News, McInnis said she did not give the council a deadline for when the group should make recommendations. Yale College accepted 4.2 percent of applicants in its most recent admissions cycle.

title: After Harvard passes grade cap, Yale faculty say ‘bravo’ for first step
subtitle: In an effort to curb grade inflation, 70 percent of Harvard’s faculty voted to cap A grades starting in fall 2027 — a measure that Yale professors said could trigger peer institutions to follow suit. Some professors criticized the new system’s ethos.
author: Jolynda Wang
text: Conversations around grade inflation in the Ivy League turned into action on Wednesday when Harvard announced that 70 percent of its faculty voted in favor of a policy to cap A grades at 20 percent for each course, allowing for up to four additional A’s per class, beginning in fall 2027. Wednesday’s vote marked a major shift by Harvard to confront grade inflation that coincides with similar efforts at Yale, which are in earlier stages. Yale professors, while decrying that grade inflation contributes to student stress, perfectionism and difficulty distinguishing academic performance, have described the issue as a collective action problem, in which stricter grading by individual faculty — and universities — comparatively disadvantages their own students. In a statement after Wednesday’s vote, Amanda Claybaugh ’93, Harvard’s dean of undergraduate education who has spearheaded the school’s effort to curb grade inflation, expressed hope that the vote drives Harvard’s peers to follow its lead. “This is a consequential vote,” Claybaugh wrote. “It will, I believe, strengthen the academic culture of Harvard; it will also, I hope, encourage other institutions to confront similar questions with the same level of rigor and courage.” In interviews with the News, Yale professors expressed support for Harvard’s new grade cap as a meaningful step by a leading university to combat grade inflation. Many still pointed out what they described as imperfections in the system, including a lack of nuance between seminars and lectures, the possibility of a proliferation of A-minuses and a focus away from intellectual growth. Yale Law School professor Sarath Sanga LAW ’14 said “bravo to Harvard” in a Zoom interview, praising its faculty for taking concrete action on grade inflation. Sanga said that although he thinks Harvard’s solution is imperfect, it is preferable to maintaining the status quo of grade distribution, which he described as “beyond terrible and illogical.” “This is Harvard’s attempt to make a baby step toward putting together the best possible pedagogical environment for the students,” Sanga said. “By virtue of making the right step in the right direction and being the first one to do it publicly, I mean, that takes guts, right?” Grade inflation has reached similar heights at Yale and Harvard. At Harvard, after its administration urged faculty to combat grade inflation in October, the share of A’s fell from 60.2 percent in the 2024–25 school year to 53.4 percent last fall. At Yale, 58.3 percent of grades were A’s in the 2022–23 school year — part of a distribution that Yale College Dean Pericles Lewis described as “pretty consistent” over the past four years. Yale College’s class of 2026 set a record-high cutoff for Latin honors this month, with summa cum laude requiring a 3.99 GPA. Harvard’s cutoff for summa cum laude last year was 3.989. In April, Yale’s Committee on Trust in Higher Education released a report that includes remedies for grade inflation. The report recommends that the University registrar “immediately” start including grade percentiles per course in transcripts to reflect the “context for each grade.” It also recommended a “3.0 mean, or some other college-wide standard,” which it acknowledged will require “further conversation among the faculty” and urged that the conversation “begin now.” Yale College’s Standing Committee on Teaching, Learning and Advising — convened by Lewis in conjunction with University President Maurie McInnis — is currently reviewing grade inflation at Yale. In February, Lewis told the News that the standing committee’s members were closely monitoring the grading policies at Harvard, noting that peer universities are “competitors” and that he doesn’t want “an A at Yale to be seen as a lesser A.” When asked about the Harvard vote on Wednesday, Lewis wrote to the News that grading practices “will be a topic of discussion” for Yale faculty in the upcoming school year. Economics professor Ray Fair speculated in a phone interview that if Harvard significantly reduced the number of A grades while Yale continued awarding them at much higher rates, a Harvard A could come to carry more weight than a Yale A. That discrepancy could potentially affect Yale’s reputation and ultimately incentivize the University to adopt similar reforms, Fair said. Fair chaired the University’s ad hoc committee on grading policy, which convened in the 2012–13 and 2013–14 academic years. That committee’s proposal recommended a set of grading guidelines as well as that Yale College switch from a letter-grade system to a 100-point scale, which faced fierce student backlash and was ultimately dropped by faculty. “Yale tried in 2014 to be a leader in this, and the faculty rejected that. However, now Harvard accepted something similar, and so somebody has started this process,” Fair said, referring to efforts to combat grade inflation. “One would hope that — if you think grade inflation is a problem — the Harvard move would trigger other peer institutions to do the same thing or do something similar.” Sanga, who helped write the Committee on Trust in Higher Education’s report, described the committee’s recommendations as a “better-designed rule” than Harvard’s grade cap. Including course percentiles and aiming for an overall 3.0 average GPA would provide students with clearer, standardized feedback about their academic strengths while also making transcripts easier for outside readers to interpret, he said. But Sanga recognized that universities often hesitate to act until peer institutions move first, and he noted that Harvard’s prominence could pressure schools like Yale to reconsider their own grading practices. “There’s no denying that Harvard is the American flagship. This is the American global leader in higher education, whether you like it or not,” Sanga said, making note of Harvard’s resources. “It has this kind of leadership status even when it’s not perfect, maybe especially when it’s not perfect. And so I’m looking at what they just accomplished there.” Political science professor Dan Mattingly ’04 GRD ’08 referred to Harvard’s policy as “the least terrible option in a sea of terrible options” to combat grade inflation. He said the policy would reduce professors’ ability to independently assess student work and expressed hope for more “nuanced” reforms. Still, he compared Harvard’s move to “very strong medicine” that will “force people into a new equilibrium.” If more elite universities adopt measures against grade inflation, those reforms will become more “durable,” Mattingly added. Philosophy professor Thomas Pogge also described grade inflation as a “nationwide phenomenon” fueled by competition among universities, noting that prominent institutions like Yale and Harvard have a particular responsibility to lead reform efforts rather than continue escalating grades upward. “Highly visible institutions are much more able to take the first step,” Pogge said in a Zoom interview. Some Yale professors, however, pointed out that Harvard’s new system does not account for nuances between course styles and leaves room for the proliferation of A-minuses. Global affairs professor Ted Wittenstein ’04 LAW ’12 wrote in the email to the News that he was of “two minds” about the new Harvard policy. While he supported the Harvard grade cap for its immediacy and simplicity, Wittenstein raised concerns that a universal cap would fail to account for differences between large introductory lecture courses and smaller advanced seminars. Pogge also noted that Harvard’s policy places no cap on A-minus grades, potentially creating what he described as a “loophole.” Physics professor A. Douglas Stone echoed this concern. “They allow unlimited A-minuses it seems and this seems a huge loophole in that instructors not wanting to upset students can have only two grades, A and A-, and still not really differentiate different levels of achievement,” Stone wrote in an email to the News. “I hope we make changes which require a larger range of grades.” Astronomy professor Charles Bailyn ’81 wrote in an email to the News that he thinks Harvard’s proposal does not go “nearly far enough” to address the underlying problems with modern grading systems, arguing that the traditional scale of A through F remains fundamentally flawed. But Bailyn added that he would vote in favor of any Yale proposal to combat grade inflation — “Anything is better than the current ‘system,’” he wrote. Five professors said in interviews with the News that the broader goal of grading reform should be to return attention to intellectual growth and academic challenge rather than perfecting numbers on a transcript. English senior lecturer Heather Klemann GRD ’08 ’13 said in a Zoom interview that determining a percentage cap on grades can feel arbitrary and emphasized the importance of restoring students’ “love of learning.” Film and Media Studies professor Charles Musser ’73 questioned the fundamental concern about grade inflation and opposed Harvard’s proposed cap A grades, stating that the policy would undermine “academic freedom.” He said in a phone interview with the News that his goal is to inspire his students so that all of them produce strong enough work to receive an A. Musser added that Yale’s highly selective admissions process naturally results in a student body capable of earning excellent grades. Rather than viewing high grades as evidence of inflation, he said, they reflect the academic strength of admitted students. Grading systems should focus on encouraging intellectual growth rather than manufacturing an “artificial” distribution of grades, he noted. According to a 2023 report by Fair, 79 percent of grades in Yale College in the 2022–23 school year were A’s or A-minuses.

subtitle: 4,140 members of the class of 2026 received their diplomas on Monday.
text: VOL. CXLIX New Haven, Connecticut Tuesday, June 16, 2026

title: At Law School Commencement, Boasberg ’85 LAW ’90 stresses moral fiber
subtitle: At Yale Law School’s 2026 Commencement ceremony, James Boasberg ’85 LAW ’90 encouraged graduates to choose career paths that align with their personal values. The federal judge’s legal battle over Trump’s deportation flights made headlines last year.
author: Sasha Cabral
text: Judge James Boasberg ’85 LAW ’90 spoke at Yale Law School’s 2026 Commencement ceremony on Monday to approximately 240 graduates and their guests. The event, which was held in Payne Whitney Gymnasium’s Lanman Center, followed the University-wide Commencement on Old Campus. Boasberg, the chief judge of the United States District Court for the District of Columbia, has been the subject of a firestorm of criticism from the right for his rulings against the Trump administration — particularly his order halting deportation flights of Venezuelan nationals in March 2025. In keeping with recent years, the Law School did not publicly announce Boasberg as the Commencement speaker in its announcement about the ceremony. In addition to the Commencement speech, the ceremony traditionally features remarks by the dean of the Law School and a speech from a faculty member. The faculty speaker this year was Nicholas R. Parrillo GRD ’01 ’05 ’12 LAW ’04. Former Dean Heather Gerken spoke about her departure from the Law School to lead the Ford Foundation, and current Dean Cristina Rodríguez ’95 LAW ’00 also addressed attendees. After Parrillo’s speech, Rodríguez introduced Boasberg, remarking on his participation in theater and basketball during his time at Yale. She added that the judge has made rulings on topics “at the heart of our democracy.” Last spring, Boasberg made headlines when he pursued a contempt investigation into the second Trump administration over its refusal to comply with his order to cease deportation flights to El Salvador — an order that prompted Trump, along with Republican lawmakers, to call for the judge’s impeachment. Last month , a court of appeals ordered the termination of the contempt investigation, citing the lack of a “clear and specific” order from Boasberg mandating that the Venezuelan nationals not be transferred into Salvadoran custody. Last July, former United States Attorney General Pam Bondi’s office filed a misconduct complaint against Boasberg, alleging that he had attempted to inappropriately influence other judges to raise concerns about the Trump administration’s actions. The complaint was ultimately dismissed by a federal appeals court. Two months ago, Trump characterized Boasberg as “wacky, nasty, crooked and totally out of control” after the judge quashed subpoenas in the Justice Department’s investigation into Federal Reserve chair Jerome Powell. Days later, Supreme Court Chief Justice John Roberts rebuked personal criticism of federal judges, saying, “it’s got to stop.” Roberts also criticized Trump’s calls for Boasberg’s impeachment last year. Boasberg did not address political matters in his speech, instead offering advice to graduating students. He advised graduates to seek out occupational opportunities with a “mission” that corresponds with their personal values and reminded them, “your life is larger than your career.” He recounted a pivotal moment from his own career where he realized he did not want to continue in a private practice role that involved denying some individuals’ insurance coverage. Boasberg said he found more fulfillment working for the government. In his speech, Boasberg also praised the instruction he received at the Law School from professors including Harold Koh and Guido Calabresi ’53 LAW ’58, who taught about “heroes in the law” that demonstrated “what committed lawyers can do to improve society.” He cited the work of judges like Thurgood Marshall and Frank Johnson, who championed civil rights and racial equality. Koh said that Boasberg’s speech reflected the judge’s own legacy in upholding the rule of law. “Chief Judge Boasberg is one of our finest graduates and judges,” Koh wrote to the News. “His deeply humane and principled speech both inspired our students and challenged them to stand up for the rule of law, as he himself has so courageously done.” Boasberg acknowledged that students might embark on a career in a large corporate firm for financial stability, but advised them to question constantly whether the job is the right one for them. He warned against the practice of choosing a career for the sake of keeping one’s options open. “But when you are representing a public entity, for example, a municipality, a state, or a federal government, there’s a meaningful difference between isolated disagreements with your client and helping to defend policies that are wholly antithetical to your core values,” Boasberg said. “I encourage all of you to have your own red line. Each of you will put it in a different place.” “If you were asked to cross that red line,” he said, “you must decide whether you can do so and still look at yourself in the mirror, or whether it’s time to start searching for a new job.” Lastly, Boasberg encouraged students to pursue unconventional legal careers, despite the uncertainty that might come with such paths. “You will find Yale Law graduates in an amazing array of positions, and mind-bogglingly varied millieus. But you won’t be one of them if you just sit in your cubicle, hoping that one day, someone will come by and tap you on the shoulder with something interesting,” Boasberg said. “Be imaginative, be creative and don’t be bound by the most traditional options.” During his time at the Law School, Boasberg said he lived at 61 Lake Place with seven other students, including current Supreme Court Justice Brett Kavanaugh ’87 LAW ’90. In his speech on Monday, Boasberg said the group still gathers annually. Last year, the Law School’s commencement speaker was Elizabeth Prelogar, the solicitor general under the Biden administration.

title: Unions rally outside of Commencement amid lagging contract talks
subtitle: As the class of 2026 gathered on Old Campus, Yale’s two primary unions demonstrated outside of Phelps Gate for increased wages and benefits. While the rally surprised many Commencement guests, they largely showed support for its cause.
author: Nellie Kenney
text: Around 400 Yale workers marched outside of Phelps Gate during Monday’s Commencement ceremony to call for higher wages. Local 34, the Yale clerical and technical workers’ union, and Local 35, the Yale service and maintenance workers’ union, are seeking new contracts with Yale as their current memorandum of agreement nears its end date of January 2027. On Monday morning, union members gathered around a pair of tents facing the New Haven Green, distributing picket signs and bright red t-shirts that read “WE CAN’T KEEP UP!” They then marched back and forth along the block of College Street adjacent to Old Campus, displaying large banners that read “CONGRATULATIONS” and “YALE SETTLE.” Monday’s protest marked the first time that Yale unions rallied outside of Commencement since 2017, when graduate students gathered to call on Yale to open contract negotiations. Graduates traditionally use Phelps Gate to enter Old Campus for the ceremony. Contract talks between the unions and the University have hit a snag, according to Local 35 President Bob Proto. In a Friday interview, Proto said that “the University has canceled meetings, the University is not negotiating with us.” “We’re here to inform the parents and the alumni that we’re in a dispute,” New Haven alder Brian Wingate, who is also a vice president of Local 35, said, adding that the protesters “want to be very respectful” of the graduates. In a statement emailed to the News, University spokesperson Karen Peart wrote that Yale “has met with the unions and will provide a comprehensive package proposal to the unions next month that will include responses to each of their proposals.” Proto said that Yale’s approach to negotiations so far has threatened its relationship with unions. “Since 2003, we were able to settle peaceful contracts, right up to this moment,” he said on Friday. “At this moment, we’re concerned that the present administration is moving away from labor peace.” In 2003, Yale’s unions engaged in over a year of unsuccessful negotiations, which culminated in a three-week strike before they struck an agreement with the University. Locals 34 and 35 have negotiated four successful contracts since, with the most recent ratified in 2021. A webpage titled “Know Before You Go” on Yale’s Commencement website notes that the University is “committed to free expression, including peaceful dissent.” Asked about the timing of the rally, Connie Lubinski, a research assistant at the Yale School of Medicine, said that she thought it was “important to have the families and alumni know” about the importance of union members’ work. She added that a better contract would include “better raises” and “lower healthcare costs.” As they emerged through Phelps Gate, many Commencement attendees stopped to film the rally on their phones. Others waved at the union members, and a few cheered. Kiva Bank, Staff Photographer Eight Commencement attendees who saw the protest as they left the ceremony told the News that they had not previously known about Yale workers’ contract negotiations. “It’s interesting. I didn’t expect it,” Prentiss Carter, the father of a graduating senior, said after he left the ceremony. He added that “in this day and age, it’s understandable” that union members are seeking higher wages. Michael Asare, also the father of a senior, said he hailed from a “union town.” “I’m on their side,” he said of the union members, adding that Yale, given the size of its endowment, “should be able to help these people.” Clay Ijams, another parent, said that he would have expected Yale to be willing to meet union member’s demands. Ijams added that he “wouldn’t pick graduation” as a time for the rally and that he “wouldn’t think it would be most effective.” Jairus Rhoades ’26 called the rally “beautiful.” “I think it’s really in tone with students standing up during conferral of degrees,” Rhoades said, referring to scattered protestors who held up signs during the ceremony, including one that read “DIVEST FROM WAR INVEST IN NEW HAVEN.” In her email to the News, Peart wrote that the University is negotiating new contracts with the two unions and “looks forward to a positive, productive process that results in a fair agreement for all parties.” Proto said that the unions’ negotiators had been met with “PowerPoint after PowerPoint about the University’s financial situation,” and particularly the recent hike in the federal endowment tax . Yale administrators have estimated that the tax hike, which was signed into law last summer and goes into effect this July, will cost the University $300 million annually. Yale “already benefits” from its corporate income tax and property tax exemptions, Proto said. “You would think the responsible thing that they would do is to provide good, sustainable jobs with benefits that keep the neighborhoods where you can afford to buy groceries, pay your bills, even dream about buying a house,” he said. Peart wrote that the University “hopes to find common ground during the negotiation process to meet its operational needs while continuing to provide exceptional pay and benefits to our valuable union members for this weekend and the entire year.” Locals 34 and 35 together represent nearly 5,500 Yale employees, according to an April 13 letter sent by the unions’ leaders to the Board of Alders president. Correction, May 19: A previous version of this article misquoted Local 35 President Bob Proto and, due to an editing error, mischaracterized his statement. Proto said "the University is not negotiating with us," not "the University will not negotiate with us." He did not describe an impasse, but noted impediments to contract talks between the unions and the University.

title: Yale confers 4,140 degrees at Commencement amid muted demonstrations
subtitle: During Monday morning’s ceremony, traditional proceedings took place on Old Campus alongside a union rally outside of Phelps Gate. Some students in the audience raised signs of protest before administrators asked them to lower them.
author: Leo Nyberg, Aria Lynn-Skov
text: At Yale’s 325th Commencement on Monday morning, University leaders delivered prayers and traditional remarks in celebration of the class of 2026. Graduating students from Yale College and the 14 graduate and professional schools — along with parents, friends and alumni — packed Old Campus for the ceremony. During the event, protesters both inside and outside Old Campus staged demonstrations — with graduating students holding signs calling on Yale to divest from Israel and weapons manufacturers, and unions rallying on College St. to demand higher wages — though events on stage went uninterrupted. This year, Yale conferred 4,140 degrees, according to University spokesperson Karen Peart. “We salute your effort, your diligence, your talent and your intellect,” Yale president Maurie McInnis said in her greetings at the event. “We also join you in expressing gratitude for all who have supported you in these endeavors. Your families and friends, teachers and other members of the Yale community.” After McInnis’ remarks, University Chaplain Maytal Saltiel led the audience in a prayer. The deans of each of Yale’s 15 constituent schools then took turns tipping their caps to the president and presenting candidates for degrees, affirming that all had been recommended by the faculty and approved by the Yale Corporation. McInnis tipped her cap in response before awarding the degrees for each school, beginning with the 1,455 bachelors degrees conferred on Yale College students and concluding with the doctoral degrees from the Graduate School of Arts and Sciences — whose recipients were addressed in Latin. As the president conferred each set of degrees upon students “by the authority vested” in her, she admitted them to “all rights and responsibilities” of their respective degrees. Each set of conferrals was followed by a round of applause from the audience. During that segment of the ceremony, small groups of students scattered across the audience stood and held up fabric signs calling for divestment from weapons manufacturers and reinvestment in New Haven, among other demands. One sign read “THESE DEGREES CONFERRED IN BLOOD,” while another read “END US EMPIRE DIVEST FROM ISRAEL.” Isobel McClure, Contributing Photographer The signs faced audience members behind them, not the stage. Peart wrote on Monday that the students were not in compliance with University policies on protests, “as the banners blocked the view for others in their vicinity.” “University officials asked them to sit down and lower the banners, and the students complied,” Peart wrote. “The university was not aware in advance of any plans to protest.” The first item on the “Know Before You Go” webpage of Yale’s commencement website states that “Yale is committed to free expression, including peaceful dissent” and that “University guidelines and policies are in place for all Old Campus ceremonies to maintain operations, safety, and security.” On stage, the Commencement ceremony proceeded without disruption. Meanwhile, just outside Phelps Gate, around 400 Yale union members had gathered, many of whom donned red shirts that read: “WE CAN’T KEEP UP!” The demonstrators waved signs, distributed flyers and chanted calls for the University to guarantee wage increases that match rising costs of living in its next contract. After the deans presented their degree candidates on Old Campus, the Yale Glee Club led a hymn before McInnis and University Provost Scott Strobel presented eight individuals with honorary degrees. The University held a private ceremony to celebrate these recipients on Sunday evening. “The eight individuals we honor this morning serve as examples to you, our graduates, to encourage you to aspire to excellence, and to value those elements of human character that they embody,” McInnis said at Commencement. “Creativity, curiosity, discipline, integrity and a passion for public service. Their presence here also honors you, who graduate today and all of us who are here to salute you.” In a Friday press release , Yale announced seven of the eight individuals who received degrees on Monday. Ingrid Mattson, who is a Canadian scholar, activist and professor of Islamic studies, was not included in the release but was honored first in the ceremony. “The university announced some of the honorands earlier than we had in the past to build excitement for the ceremony,” Peart wrote. “It honored each of their personal choices for inclusion in advance coverage about their degree. Post-ceremony coverage includes all honorands.” The seven other recipients included former Baltimore Mayor Kurt Schmoke ’71, political scientist Robert Putnam GRD ’65 ’70 and Olympic gold medalist Katie Ledecky. Ledecky drew the loudest and longest cheers from the crowd on Monday. Divinity School Dean Gregory Sterling offered a benediction to close the ceremony, after which graduates, administrators, parents and guests exited Old Campus to music from the Yale University Concert Band. Yale College students went on to receive their diplomas at afternoon residential college ceremonies. The prayers at this year’s Commencement were nonsectarian. In 1722, Yale’s third rector, Timothy Cutler, was fired by the trustees for ending his Commencement address with an Anglican prayer, a scandal in the then largely Puritan Yale College and Connecticut colony.

title: McInnis stresses gratitude, collective memory in Baccalaureate speech
subtitle: In her second Baccalaureate address, University President Maurie McInnis drew on Yale’s history, urging graduating seniors to value their shared experiences. A reading by Yale College Dean Pericles Lewis honored the seniors’ upcoming life journeys.
author: Aria Lynn-Skov
text: Gathered on Old Campus on Sunday morning, the graduating class of 2026, alongside their guests, Yale faculty and administrators, listened as University President Maurie McInnis delivered her second Baccalaureate address — a speech rooted in history. Drawing from the legacy of former Yale President Theodore Dwight Woolsey and the collaborative spirit of the class of 1850, McInnis urged seniors to value memories both remarkable and mundane in their lives after graduation. Sunday morning’s ceremony also included readings and prayers from Yale College Dean Pericles Lewis, University Chaplain Maytal Saltiel and several students. McInnis’ speech centered around the subjective and ever-changing nature of memory, drawing from Maurice Halbwachs’ book “On Collective Memory,” which she said influenced her own scholarship as a graduate student. She said coming together on any scale — whether with close friends or as a graduating class — means creating stories that “have a way of combining and overlapping and becoming your own.” This act, McInnis said, is “the work of collective memory.” “It’s this shared remembrance that binds you together as the class of 2026, and that binds you to Yale, and connects you with all the graduates who’ve come before you,” McInnis said. She added that graduates’ interpretation of these memories will “fluctuate” over time because just as “the future is undetermined, the past is undetermined, too.” With the “fundamental” question of how graduates would use their “memories of Yale to shape the future” in mind, McInnis urged students to “hold on to both the glorious and the forgettable” memories. Graduates don’t know yet how episodes from college years or concepts from classes will influence their future, and it all “deserves to be part of your class’s shared collage of retrospection,” McInnis said. “It’s all part of your total effort to remember together. And that commitment to remember together is what will continue to unite you,” she said. McInnis reflected on gratitude during her speech, using the statue on Old Campus of a cross-legged Theodore Dwight Woolsey, the 10th president of Yale, as an introduction to the class of 1850’s dedication to preserving their shared memories. Woolsey taught political economy while serving as president, and McInnis highlighted his lasting legacy as an educator as well as an administrator. Decades after they had graduated, alumni joined forces to donate the statue in honor of the professor who “had changed their lives,” demonstrating their “shared gratitude.” “But the monuments you’ll build to them won’t be as massive and bronze. Their monuments will be the possibilities that you’ll fulfill, the possibilities that they first opened for you here,” McInnis said about that type of influential professor. “This is how your memories open the way for your future possibilities.” In addition to honoring their president and professor Woolsey, the class of 1850 was particularly dedicated to “staying in relationship with one another,” McInnis said, describing the compilation of biographies the class created nearly three decades after graduation. In a May 6 interview, McInnis spoke about how she involves her background as an art historian in the process of writing her Commencement addresses. She noted that she likes to “anchor” her speeches in “at least in a single work of art, if not more.” In her first Baccalaureate address , which she gave last spring, McInnis focused on “The Battle of Bunker Hill,” a Revolutionary War-era painting by John Trumbull. During this year’s address, McInnis praised the achievements of Yale graduates in the past and present alike. She praised the graduates of 1850 — one a founder of the National Academy of Sciences, another a congressman judged among the “most efficient members of the Ways and Means Committee” — as well as this year’s graduates — founders of health and music companies, actors in productions like “Fiddler on the Roof.” McInnis closed her speech with a call for seniors to use shared memory as a way of remaining connected to each other. “Class of 2026, though the stories you tell will change, may your hearts turn ever toward each other, in gratitude that you could spend these years together,” she said. “And may your hearts turn ever toward humanity, as you take what you’ve learned here, and shape the future that will reshape the past.” CC Wolf ’26 said McInnis’ speech sent a befitting message for the graduating class. “I think it resonated with a lot of people,” Wolf said. “I was speaking with the girls I was with, and a lot of the comments that she made about the future and taking Yale with you, I think, is something that a lot of graduates feel, especially as we look towards whatever comes next.” Way Lee ’26 described McInnis’ address as “neutral” and “pretty straightforward,” noting that it did not include a “drastic opinion or plea.” Baccalaureate — the first, and most formal , of the three main Commencement weekend events — is a centuries-old tradition. The University’s president, like McInnis, traditionally delivers the ceremony’s main address, while the dean of Yale College delivers a selected reading. For this year’s reading, Lewis chose the poem “Ithaka” by the Greek poet Constantine Cavafy. The poem addresses an unnamed auditor who sets out on a journey and seems to be Homer’s Odysseus, “one of the most famous travelers in literature,” Lewis said. Four years ago, when the class of 2026 had just entered Yale as first years, Lewis shared in his welcoming address a quotation from Alfred Tennyson’s poem “Ulysses.” Ulysses is the Latin name for Odysseus. This year, Lewis emphasized a message of making the most of life’s journey, reading the poem that begins with the line, “As you set out for Ithaka, hope your road is a long one, full of adventure, full of discovery.” “As Cavafy addresses his traveler, I think each of you can imagine he is speaking directly to you,” Lewis told the class of 2026. Woolsey — Yale’s president from 1846 to 1871 — oversaw the arrival of the University’s first international students during his tenure, according to the Yale Alumni Magazine . Correction, May 18: A previous version of this article misstated the date of McInnis’ recent interview with the News. The interview took place on May 6, not May 8.

title: New Haven is the only city fighting Trump’s ‘slush’ fund in court. Why?
subtitle: The city’s lawsuit opposing the federal government’s $1.8 billion “anti-weaponization fund” — in which it was the only municipal plaintiff — offers a window into why New Haven officials have repeatedly turned to the courts in Trump’s second term.
author: Adele Haeg
text: Since President Donald Trump moved back into the White House, New Haven’s new legal counsel has been busy. The city has repeatedly sued the federal government over the past year and a half, joining six lawsuits and 24 other amicus briefs against the Trump administration. New Haven has argued against funding freezes for environmental initiatives and SNAP benefits , and supported Minnesota in its lawsuit over immigration enforcement in the state. Mayor Justin Elicker has praised a “growing nationwide coalition of local governments” who have taken similar action — but with its latest suit, New Haven stands apart. Last month, the city was the only one to join a high-profile lawsuit seeking to block the president’s $1.8 billion “anti-weaponization fund” in a Virginia district court, and none of the other suits that have been filed in opposition to the fund have municipal plaintiffs. New Haven joined the lawsuit a month after the Trump administration sued the city for its immigration “sanctuary policies” — in that case, New Haven and Connecticut officials were the only defendants. Soon after filing the lawsuit opposing Trump’s fund, New Haven and its fellow plaintiffs — a fired Jan. 6 prosecutor, a California professor formerly charged with assaulting a federal officer, the National Abortion Federation and a government watchdog group — requested emergency relief to block the federal government from disbursing money through the program. The next day, Judge Leonie Brinkema granted a temporary injunction in the case until June 12, and last Friday, the Department of Justice told Brinkema that the fund was “not going forward.” As of Tuesday, the future of the slush fund remains unclear . Allie Jacobs, the corporation counsel for New Haven, wrote in an email to the News that the city thinks its strategy in the latest lawsuit has been successful so far. However, it is not clear how much of an impact New Haven’s involvement in the suit has had on whether the fund will ultimately move forward. The other lawsuits against the “slush" fund are ongoing, and the program has come under bipartisan fire in Congress. The mayor said he is not pursuing legal action for the purpose of political visibility. “If there were a case that was simply symbolic, I’m not sure we would join,” Elicker said in a phone interview with the News. “It’s about us making sure that we have impacts that help New Haven residents.” “The punchline for a lot of Democratic elected officials is that it may both be the right political move and the right move for ‘small-d democracy’” to sue the Trump administration, Dan Barrett, legal director of the Connecticut American Civil Liberties Union, said in a phone interview with the News. Maybe, he added, “people will look back and say, ‘well, at least somebody tried to fight back.’” How did New Haven get involved in the lawsuit? In May, lawyers from the nonprofit Democracy Forward — which is representing the plaintiffs in the suit — approached Elicker and asked if New Haven would join in suing the Trump administration for setting up their “Anti-Weaponization Fund.” The fund — a result of a settlement with the Trump administration in a legal dispute over his tax returns — was set up to compensate allies of the Republican Party who “suffered weaponization” by the DOJ under former President Joe Biden. Along with the three other plaintiffs, Democracy Forward planned to prosecute the Trump administration for misuse of taxpayer funds and “creating a politically discriminatory process ” in their disbursement, alleging that it would reward only Republicans who claimed weaponization. According to Jacobs, New Haven had “a preexisting relationship with Democracy Forward,” which previously represented plaintiffs suing the Trump administration for blocking SNAP benefits. New Haven was one of eight municipalities who participated in that lawsuit. Elicker said that after hearing from Democracy Forward, he considered his options in conversations with legal counsel, recognizing that there were “pros and cons” of suing. Democracy Forward would cover legal fees, and Elicker said that the suit aligned with “our values as a city.” “We’re defending our residents from what is in my view a real misuse of federal funds and taxpayer dollars,” the mayor added. Elicker said that one risk was that the lawsuit might “attract undue attention” from the federal government with New Haven as the only municipal plaintiff. But for Elicker, that did not mean “New Haven should not join these lawsuits but that more municipalities should join these lawsuits.” When lawyers are compiling lawsuits and “trying to get as many viable plaintiffs as you can find, you notice that each one checks a different box,” Barrett said, speculating that New Haven was brought into the suit because it had previously been sued for its “sanctuary policies.” He said it was not surprising New Haven has developed a relationship with Democracy Forward, adding that it is “common” for municipalities to collaborate with similar nonprofits. “There are certainly situations in which municipalities really have to be on the front lines litigating, particularly with some grant programs that municipalities will directly interact with the federal government over,” Barrett said. “In other cases, it’s less clear, but this probably serves a democratic purpose.” New Haven as an “anti-monarchist” city Apart from the city’s adherence to its “sanctuary policies” and other municipal priorities and systems that Elicker said the federal administration is “dismantling,” Jacobs suggested that it is in New Haven’s character as a city to take legal action in the way that it has been. It is “unusual” for cities to sue the federal government, Jacobs said — especially one like New Haven, with only 135,000 residents. “It takes courage for a smaller municipality to stand up to a much larger, much more well-resourced federal government,” she wrote. Jacobs said she recognized legal and historical imperatives for legal action against the Trump administration. “New Haven has a deep history of anti-monarchist principles, dating back to the 1600s and 1700s when the people of New Haven rebelled against the British Crown,” Jacobs wrote, “and we have been fighting against tyranny and injustice ever since.” City Historian Michael Morand ’87 DIV ’93 likened the city’s opposition to the chief executive to the actions of Roger Sherman, a founding father and the first mayor of New Haven. Sherman believed that a “person or persons ought to be appointed by and accountable to the Legislature only, which was the depository of the supreme will of the Society,” Morand wrote, quoting James Madison. “I’ve gotten a lot of positive feedback from residents about the stance that New Haven has taken at this moment,” Elicker said. The city’s position in the case reflects the discontent with Trump administration policies that have drawn thousands of New Haven residents to protests this year. Though there are “other communities that might not be as supportive to their leadership” taking a confrontational stance, Elicker said he feels he has New Haven’s support. “ We have a good team and we’ll continue to respond to opportunities that come in the future,” Elicker said. “Things are so volatile and unpredictable right now, it’s hard to come up with a specific plan.” Elicker was first sworn into office in January 2020.

title: Unilever research facility set to harness New Haven biotech scene
subtitle: The multinational consumer goods company, whose portfolio includes Dove, Axe and Vaseline, announced plans last week to invest $270 million in an innovation center at 2 Church St. Local leaders touted the investment as a boon for the city.
author: Evelyn Ronan, Nellie Kenney
text: The consumer goods company Unilever will invest $270 million in a new research and development center in downtown New Haven, the company announced in a press conference last Thursday. The center is set to open at 2 Church St. in 2029. At the conference, which was livestreamed , Mayor Justin Elicker and Gov. Ned Lamont SOM ’80 heralded the investment as a sign of growth for New Haven and the state. Elicker lauded the city’s “booming” bioscience sector, which Unilever hopes to harness in studying “skin biology, the microbiome and the skin-brain axis,” according to a press release. In a statement emailed to the News, Leo Aquino, the company’s senior vice president of research and development, cited New Haven’s “ecosystem” of universities and quantum computing investment as a reason for the move. The center will apply “AI-powered, digitally integrated science” to product development and will house “formulation, fragrance, packaging, consumer insights, clinical testing” all “under one roof,” Aquino wrote. The Church Street location is set to employ around 300 workers. The Unilever development news arrives amid a steady flow of biotech developments in New Haven, funded by both Yale and the state. In September, Connecticut announced a $50 million grant to facilitate biotech growth in the city, alongside separate grants for startups QuantumCT and BioCT, which received $10 million and $3 million from the state, respectively. The building at 2 Church St. is set to be constructed by Winstanley Enterprises, a commercial real estate developer that has acquired properties across New Haven, CT Insider reported . In 2013, Winstanley Enterprises began construction on a tower at 100 College St. which now houses Yale’s psychology and neuroscience departments and Alexion Pharmaceuticals, a biopharmaceutical company. In 2024, the group finished construction on a building at 101 College St. which also houses Alexion, as well as biotech lab facilities. Both are located about a quarter of a mile from 2 Church St., where the building housing Unilever is set to stand. “New Haven places us at the center of some of the world’s leading medical schools and research institutes — including Yale — and we fully intend to build on those relationships as the center develops,” Aquino wrote. Becca Schelling GRD ’29, the director of content for the Yale Biotech Club, suggested that the Unilever investment could make the city a more attractive destination for recent graduates. The club supports student engagement with the local biotech sector by partnering with companies through externship and informational programs, Schelling said in a phone interview. Having more biotech companies in downtown New Haven “makes it easier” for the club to “facilitate those interactions with students,” she said. Although Schelling said that she had “not seen” members of the club staying in the city after graduating, she guessed that “if the opportunities presented themselves in New Haven, people would take them.” The Connecticut Department of Economic and Community Development will contribute a $10 million loan to support the construction of the 2 Church St. building, part of an effort to retain jobs for Connecticut residents, CT Insider reported. The new lab will replace Unilever’s longtime research and development lab in nearby Trumbull, Connecticut. David Schleicher, a Yale Law School professor who focuses on local government and property law, wrote in an email to the News that Unilever could have relocated out-of-state, like General Electric did in 2016 when it left Fairfield, Connecticut. A News article from the time described criticism that painted General Electric’s departure as an example of “the state’s chronic inability to grow its high-tech industry to full potential.” Ward 7 Alder Christine Kim ’99 wrote in a Monday email to the News that an “anchor tenant” of Unilever’s scale would mean “daytime population, foot traffic for our small businesses, and energy on blocks that have had vacancies.” Kim’s ward includes 2 Church St. as well as a cluster of nearby Winstanley bioscience developments. Kim added that she is keeping an eye on the “community impacts” of the center. “These are world-class scientists and engineers, many with families, who’ll need housing in an already-tight market. I don’t want our growth to price out the residents who built this city,” she wrote. Kim’s conversations with constituents about the future Unilever center have yielded both “genuine excitement about jobs and a livelier downtown” and “real questions about housing costs, parking, who gets hired, and what the public puts in versus gets back,” she wrote, noting that many are still “taking it in.” Aquino did not comment on which future roles would be filled by Unilever employees currently based in Trumbull and which would be filled through local hiring, but he noted that “pinpointing an area that worked for talent retention and attracting future talent” was one of the “key elements” in choosing the New Haven location. When asked about affordability concerns, Elicker wrote that New Haven is a “growing city” and is “committed to growing inclusively.” Elicker also pointed to the city’s goal of constructing 10,000 new housing units by 2035, a goal which New Haven is “well on our way” to achieving, he wrote. The 2 Church St. building will contribute millions of dollars in tax revenue to help support municipal services, Elicker wrote, adding that constructions at 100 College St. and 101 College St. contributed “more than the annual budget for the Parks Department or New Haven Free Public Library” in taxes to the city last year. Schleicher wrote that he did not think Unilever’s plans would substantially alter the city’s housing market, noting that Yale University and Yale New Haven Hospital each employ more than 10,000 employees, much more than Unilever’s expected staff of 300. Unilever will share the 2 Church St. space with biotech startup QuantumCT, according to the New Haven Independent .

title: Witnessing ICE arrests and nonprofit cuts, locals decry Trump policies
subtitle: Donald Trump’s second presidential term has jolted New Haven with arrests by Immigration and Customs Enforcement and slashed funding for and access to social services. Local activists and nonprofit leaders continue to protest.
author: Nellie Kenney
text: A group of New Haveners, clad in bright colors and carrying paper cranes, sang out advice for resisting Immigration and Customs Enforcement officers at a recent May Day rally on the New Haven Green: “Don’t say anything, don’t sign anything. Don’t let them trick you into opening the door.” Local activists — like these members of Singing Resistance New Haven, a chapter of the national organization — have mobilized in response to sweeping federal policy changes and their local effects since the beginning of Donald Trump’s second presidential term. New Haven advocates described an atmosphere of anxiety amid both the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown and federal cuts to social services, which have jeopardized local access to food. The “biggest thing, both for attorneys and for non-citizens living in the U.S.” is the “uncertainty and how quickly things are changing,” Maureen Abell, an immigration attorney at the New Haven Legal Assistance Association, said in a phone interview last Friday. Federal immigration officers have arrested at least five people in New Haven this year as part of the president’s ongoing mass deportation operation . ICE arrests increased by around 300 percent in New England in the first seven months of Trump’s presidency, according to data from the Immigration Research Initiative. Abell pointed to cases of federal immigration officers arresting people while leaving court or at green-card interviews . Although these arrests comprised “a small percentage of cases,” she said, the practice is “still having a huge effect through how afraid it is making people to engage with the system.” ICE detained a woman from the Hill neighborhood last June and a Wilbur Cross High School student the following month. The student, Esdrás Zabaleta-Ramirez, was later released following a ruling by a federal immigration judge. In January 2026, ICE arrested three people in downtown New Haven and another person in a New Haven courthouse . In March, federal agents arrested a nursing student at Southern Connecticut State University, Keyla Vasquez-Zuniga, who was granted bond last week. Those arrests have sparked an array of local protests. In July 2025, over a hundred people gathered at Wilbur Cross to protest Zabaleta-Ramirez’ detention. Teachers at the school led a letter-writing campaign attesting to the student’s schoolwork and character. Last month, students and activists rallied at Southern Connecticut State University, calling for Vasquez-Zuniga’s release. New Haven has also been the site of protests against ICE activity at the national level. In response to the deaths of Renée Good and Alex Pretti this January during the Trump administration’s crackdown in Minneapolis, locals formed the New Haven chapter of Singing Resistance and organized four demonstrations , each with at least a hundred attendees, in January and February. As organizers gather to protest immigration enforcement actions, nearby city organizations continue to grapple with federal funding cuts and locals describe increased food insecurity due to Trump-era policy. Integrated Refugee and Immigrant Services, a local nonprofit that aids refugee resettlement, closed its New Haven office in April 2025 after its funding was slashed by the federal government. The organization was also forced to lay off 30 percent of its staff, Maggie Mitchell Salem, the nonprofit’s executive director, told the News in February. In 2024, Integrated Refugee and Immigrant Services had a budget of over $20 million, most of which was provided by the federal government, Mitchell Salem said in a phone interview last Friday. In 2025, following federal funding cuts, its budget dropped to around $11 million, and this year, it hovers around $7 million, Mitchell Salem said — a number which will continue to decrease as funding to support Afghan arrivals “sunsets” in September. Mitchell Salem called the fallout of federal cuts “painful,” adding that for many immigrants, the organization’s New Haven location was one of the “first places they went to receive support and services” in the United States. “We’re having to make some very tough choices, and we have to figure out what the people we serve need most,” she said. “What, reasonably, can IRIS do through our food pantry, through our array of program services? And then what do others do, perhaps better than us? And how can we all work together to de-duplicate, to make sure that the most essential services remain available while having to cut back on others?” The Trump administration’s policy has altered the nonprofit’s partnerships as well as its funding, Mitchell Salem said. In October, the nonprofit announced that it would not receive refugees from the United States Refugee Admissions Program after the Trump administration moved to prioritize the resettlement of Afrikaners, a white minority group in South Africa. “We have never really questioned who the federal government was sending. And now we are,” Mitchell Salem said. Other local nonprofits have felt the impacts of federal actions through their effects on the populations that these organizations seek to help. Steve Werlin, the executive director of New Haven’s Downtown Evening Soup Kitchen, wrote in an email to the News that the greatest effects of federal policy “on the food assistance side” have been changes brought on by the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. The act, which Trump signed last July, tightened the work requirements around eligibility for the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, restricted eligibility for non-citizens, made changes to benefit calculations and shifted payment responsibilities onto states, according to the National Conference of State Legislatures. “Many of the people we serve have lost eligibility completely, or have seen their monthly allocations through SNAP decreased,” Werlin wrote. He also noted blocked funding to the Emergency Food and Shelter Program, which had been “pumping millions of dollars of food assistance funding directly to community providers across the country.” Werlin hypothesized that the “atmosphere of fear” created by immigration crackdowns has “made it more difficult for people to access food.” Kimberly Hart, a member of the board of directors for the Loaves and Fishes Food Pantry, has seen a “drop in migrants coming to the pantry,” she said. Hart said that changes to the SNAP program “disrupt” the lives of “have-nots.” SNAP was “working very well” before the changes, she said. 6,626 New Haveners — about 17.4 percent of the city’s total voters — voted for Trump in 2024, according to the News’ coverage at the time.

title: New Haven fights back against an antagonistic Trump administration
subtitle: Mayor Justin Elicker and state leaders have emphasized the disconnect between their offices’ priorities and those of an increasingly hostile federal government, which sued the city two months ago over its “sanctuary policies.”
author: Evelyn Ronan, Elijah Hurewitz-Ravitch
text: Since the beginning of President Donald Trump’s second term, New Haven has been targeted by the federal government as part of its crackdown against progressive and sanctuary cities. The city has responded with a series of coalition lawsuits and amicus briefs opposing the federal government, with local and state officials seeking to distance their political priorities with those of the Trump administration. In March, the federal government sued New Haven back, alleging that the city’s policies impede cooperation between local authorities and federal immigration enforcement. New Haven has joined five lawsuits and 24 amicus briefs since Trump took office, challenging policies related to immigration enforcement, federal funding, municipal authority and cuts to social welfare programs. In January 2025, the federal government released a memo which froze roughly $3 trillion in nonprofit and local government funds that provide the essential short-term cash flow allowing cities like New Haven to maintain regular operations. The motion was rescinded just as Mayor Justin Elicker was condemning the action at a Hartford press conference. A month later, the city signed an amicus brief alleging that the National Institutes of Health’s research funding cuts would destabilize the scientific research ecosystem and pose threats to early-career researchers, lamenting the “slash-and-burn philosophy” of the Trump administration regarding crucial funding, according to previous interviews with the News. Mayor Justin Elicker offered his first major commentary on the federal blows last February at his annual State of the City address, where he briefly alluded to the federal targeting of sanctuary cities like New Haven and disapproved of the Trump administration’s aims to defund diversity, equity and inclusion initiatives. Soon after, New Haven joined several Democrat-led municipalities in a lawsuit arguing that Trump’s crackdown on illegal immigration violated several clauses that protect municipal autonomy and unjustly targeted cities like New Haven for their “sanctuary” status. Last March, when announcing his proposed budget for fiscal year 2026, Elicker emphasized the difference between New Haven’s political posture and that of the Trump administration. Elicker also criticized the unpredictability of the Trump administration’s financial cutbacks, which he said makes it difficult for municipalities to draw up spending plans. He noted that New Haven is exercising “fiscal caution,” and that public entities like New Haven Public Schools rely on funding from the federal government. A month later, New Haven signed on to another lawsuit, which alleged that recent funding freezes to environment-related initiatives — including the termination of $20 billion in grant funding, $30 million of which was designated for New Haven — were illegal. That March, Elicker lamented the federal government “turning its back on cities” despite binding agreements in an interview with the News. Later that year, in October, New Haven joined another lawsuit which challenged the Department of Homeland Security’s updated terms and conditions for grant recipients. New regulations required recipients to agree not to “operate any programs that advance or promote DEI, DEIA, or discriminatory equity ideology .” The lawsuit coincided with last year’s New Haven mayoral race. Facing a Republican challenger , Elicker continued to emphasize New Haven’s “progressive values.” In a debate , he called the city the “most welcoming” in the world, alluding again to its sanctuary status. Allie Jacobs, the city’s primary legal counsel, said that New Haven weighs the benefits of joining the legal fight against the federal government against the fact that doing so could invite more scrutiny from Trump. “We assess whether the city, its interest, or its residents would be significantly adversely impacted by a particular law, policy, or judicial decision,” Jacobs wrote in an email to the News. “Sometimes that takes the form of joining a lawsuit if we have standing, sometimes that takes the form of an amicus brief, and sometimes that takes the form of other advocacy approaches.” When announcing New Haven’s participation in the lawsuit against the DHS, Elicker said that he was not concerned with drawing extra attention to New Haven. “I hear from some of my mayoral colleagues out there that they don’t want to make their cities a target,” Elicker said at a press conference at the time. “Now is a time that we should not be hiding. Now is a time that we should be standing up and fighting for the values of our community, both because at some point we’re all going to be a target anyway, but more importantly, because this is the right thing to do.” Days after New Haven joined the lawsuit against the DHS, Elicker contrasted the city’s balanced budget with the financial gridlock at the federal level that had resulted in a shutdown. “We in New Haven are continuing to do things differently by providing transparent, reliable, and stable budgeting,” he said at a press conference on the city’s fiscal outlook. “It’s what our residents deserve.” Two weeks later, New Haven joined a lawsuit filed by a coalition of municipalities, charities, religious organizations, business and unions against the Trump administration’s decision not to fund Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, or SNAP, benefits in the wake of the government shutdown. In response to the federal government’s cuts to SNAP funding — which Elicker condemned as “cruelty” that “blocked food from the table of 30,000 New Haveners” in a November interview with the News — Connecticut set aside $500 million for an emergency relief fund that subsidized food stamps during the government shutdown. In December 2025, New Haven joined a fifth lawsuit against the federal government, led by the Public Rights Project, over the federal government’s attempt to claw back over $12 billion in local funding designated for transit and public healthcare programs. The federal government declared that states would need to change policies on DEI initiatives, immigration, and abortion to obtain frozen funds. Aside from the slew of lawsuits, New Haven has joined in the national effort against the federal deputization of municipal governments and a national crackdown on immigration. This January, New Haven signed onto an amicus brief backing Minnesota in its lawsuit against the officials who authorized the federal government’s mass deportations. Elicker doubled down on his effort to distance New Haven ideologically from the Trump administration at the swearing-in ceremony for city officials this year, emphasizing New Haven’s values of “tolerance,” “respect for diversity” and “compassion for our most vulnerable.” He said that the Trump administration’s haphazard funding cuts force city officials to remain vigilant. Now, a year and a half since his first major action against the federal government, Elicker has continued to define his own ideological posture, as well as New Haven’s, as antithetical to Trump’s. In his State of the City address this February, the mayor doubled down on his previous criticism of the Trump administration, calling it an “endless dumpster fire of chaos and cruelty.” Following the dispatch of federal agents to carry out Operation Broken Trust, an Immigration and Customs Enforcement roundup that resulted in the arrest of 65 immigrants across Connecticut, the Trump administration last month filed its own lawsuit against New Haven. The Department of Justice complaint argued that New Haven’s policies illegally restrict local authorities’ collaboration with federal immigration enforcement, disregard federal law and threaten public safety. The suit, which also named Elicker, Connecticut Gov. Ned Lamont SOM ’80 and Attorney General William Tong as defendants, criticized New Haven’s enforcement of the Trust Act , which prevents the “deputization of local and state law enforcement for immigration enforcement.” A 2025 “Year in Review” message from Tong criticized the Trump administration’s “cruel, dangerous, and hurtful agenda,” adding that his office would not relent in its protection of Connecticut families. Rep. Rosa DeLauro, who represents New Haven in Congress, blasted Trump for “pursuing politically motivated lawsuits targeting our state for not carrying out ICE’s extreme enforcement agenda.” “President Trump’s actions are hurting working families, making it harder for them to afford basic necessities,” DeLauro said in a statement sent to the News by a spokesperson. “We must fight back, and New Haven and Connecticut are leading the way by challenging the Trump administration’s actions in court. Many of these cases have already been successful and restored funding to our communities.” In a phone interview with the News, Elicker said the city had been “prepared all along for a possible lawsuit” but emphasized New Haven’s compliance with federal law. “We can see that similar Trump lawsuits across the nation have already been thrown out,” the mayor said. “Trump is trying to force us to settle and carry out immigration policy, but that is not our job,” Elicker added. Several of the city’s legal challenges have already produced major rulings. One federal judge blocked the Trump administration from withholding federal funding from sanctuary jurisdictions, siding with New Haven, and another ordered the restoration of roughly $31 million in frozen environmental grants owed to the city, though the Trump administration has appealed portions of that ruling. Some legal disputes remain unresolved, including the litigation over DHS grant conditions and broader fights surrounding immigration enforcement. Elicker seems to have no intentions of backing down. He said that his approach differs from those of his colleagues across Connecticut. “Some have spoken out, but some have remained under the radar, and I don’t think that’s the right thing to do at this moment,” Elicker said. “We have to fight for what we believe in.” The Department of Justice does not currently have a permanent attorney general. Elijah Hurewitz-Ravitch contributed reporting.

title: Wooster Square and walkability encourage seniors to stay in New Haven
subtitle: Enticed by everything from fond memories to unique opportunities for research and music, seniors hailing from hometowns across the country say they will pursue career and educational opportunities in Yale’s home city.
author: Tajrian Khan
text: As seniors prepare to leave Yale’s campus and enter the workforce, a handful of them are choosing to remain in the Elm City next year. In a survey sent out to the class of 2026 by the News, 13 out of 115 respondents who said they will remain in the United States — or 11.3 percent — said they plan to live in Connecticut. According to data published by Yale’s Office of Career Strategy, around 12 percent of the class of 2025 stayed in Connecticut, compared to an average of 14 percent in the years since 2016. Rory Bricca ’26 says he hopes to stay in New Haven next year to teach music lessons at the Neighborhood Music School, as well as audition for choirs at the Yale Institute of Sacred Music and work on his own compositions. Bricca was born at the Yale New Haven Hospital and lived in Hamden until he was 10 years old, when his family moved to Tucson, Arizona. But he has “fond memories” of New Haven, he said, “especially East Rock and Wooster Square.” About a month ago, Bricca said he had a “gut feeling” that he wanted to stay in New Haven. “I’m hoping to just have a year where I can stay in this area and stay connected to the relationships I’ve built,” he said. Bricca is also excited by New Haven’s performing arts scene. “Nowhere else in the world could I be going to a friend’s musical or theater performance every week,” he said. “That’s just super fun.” Victor Nguyen ’26 plans to stay in New Haven next year to continue his research at a Yale School of Medicine neuroscience lab. “My current lab aligns with my research goals, and I didn’t want to have to start back at square one in a new location,” Nguyen said. At the same time, he thinks New Haven offers a “nice contrast” to his hometown of Fort Smith, Arkansas. When applying to colleges, he said he wanted to move to a city with a “fast living pace” he is excited to spend at least one more year in New Haven after four years at Yale. Jae-Hee Bae ’26 grew up in Dayton, Ohio, and first came to the east coast as a Yale student four years ago. But now that New Haven feels “comfortable and familiar,” she said, she is not ready to leave. “I think there’s a consensus that people want to leave New Haven after graduation,” Bae said. “But when you’re graduating, there’s a lot of change that happens and having some constant really relieves the pressure of all that change.” She loves that New Haven is “more pedestrian-friendly” than suburban Ohio and that residents are engaged in their communities. “I like that there are a lot of protests that happen on the Green, and there are a lot of events like the 5Ks and the Cherry Blossom Festival,” Bae said. “It shows that organizers really care about making it feel like home.” Like Nguyen, Bae is staying next year to work on mRNA research at the Yale School of Medicine’s Neugebauer Lab and apply to medical schools in the meantime. Many Yale students take a “gap year” before applying to medical school, and for those interested, research opportunities at Yale are “straightforward,” Bae said. “I feel comfortable knowing a handful of my friends are staying here,” Bae added. “I’m super excited to stay involved with a community that I’ve developed a relationship with.” Gia Cook ’26 will be working as a Woodbridge Fellow at Yale’s Office of Public Affairs and Communications. “I’m super-duper excited to be in New Haven,” Cook said. Originally from a suburb of Detroit, Cook said she lived in the “same little yellow house in the same street” her entire life until she came to Yale. “It’s been incredible living here, I don’t really have any dislikes,” Cook said, adding that she appreciated being “somewhere walkable” and having “access to such vibrant nightlife.” Yale University has been the largest employer of graduating students every year since 2016, when the Office of Career Strategy started publishing data on postgraduate plans.

title: After increased pledge, seniors split on whether Yale pays city enough
subtitle: The University announced that it would boost its yearly payments to New Haven in March. Some surveyed members of the class of 2026 said the contribution was too much, while others emphasized the importance of supporting the city they came to call home.
author: Chantal Eulenstein
text: Beginning next fiscal year, Yale will boost its annual payment to New Haven. Graduating seniors differ on whether they think the increased contribution is appropriate. Two months ago, University President Maurie McInnis and New Haven Mayor Justin Elicker announced an updated town-gown agreement that will see the University give its home city more than $230 million over the next seven years. A survey conducted by the News found that a plurality of graduating seniors — just under 50 percent of 147 respondents — feels that Yale’s new voluntary contribution is either “too little” or “somewhat little.” Elicker said in March that the deal “represents the largest voluntary payment” from any American university to a municipality. Yale will pay New Haven $29.9 million in the next fiscal year, and contributions will increase to $33.6 million by 2033. These sums represent a substantial increase from the previous agreement, under which Yale paid some $23 or $24 million annually. The new payment schedule followed months of negotiations between Yale and New Haven, and the agreement was announced as the class of 2026 looked ahead to graduation. Just over 44 percent of senior respondents said that the new commitment is “appropriate,” and nearly 11 percent said it was “too much” or “somewhat much.” “I think it’s important for Yale to give back to the city it is built in,” Estelle Balsirow ’26 wrote in an email to the News, adding that the survey results suggest that “a lot of seniors agree with that sentiment.” Other seniors were taken aback by the proportion of their classmates who felt that Yale’s contribution to New Haven was excessive. “I found it surprising that nearly 11 percent of people thought that Yale was giving too much money to New Haven,” Colin Kim ’26 wrote in a text to the News. “Yale as an academic institution siphons so many resources like land from the surrounding area, so I personally feel like the school should give more to rehabilitate the structural harms that it has caused to the city.” Yale owns over 850 acres in New Haven, according to CT Insider . Thanks to its status as a nonprofit, the University does not pay property taxes on any buildings that are used for academic purposes. That exemption is protected by an 1834 agreement between Yale and Connecticut. Efforts to push Yale to increase its payment ramped up in the months before the new deal was announced. In January, leaders of Yale’s Local 33, 34 and 35 unions joined city leaders and activists to demand that Yale up its annual contribution to $110 million. And in early March, nearly 500 people attended an online “solidarity summit” organized by New Haven Rising in which speakers denounced Yale for not contributing more. The University is New Haven’s second-largest taxpayer. The new town-gown agreement preserves an existing arrangement in which Yale makes payments equivalent to full property taxes for three years after purchasing a property for academic use, before reducing those payments by 10 percent each year. New initiatives announced alongside the updated agreement in March include a fund to support local nonprofits and a commitment to exploring the possibility of using vacant University land to address Connecticut’s affordable housing crisis . Some seniors believe that Yale’s volunteer contribution to New Haven could increase further. “I’m wondering what determines whether Yale is giving ‘too much,’” Kim wrote, “since I personally think that the quality of education that Yale provides would not suffer at all from the school giving more money to New Haven. In the end, isn’t that all that matters for answering the question of whether Yale pays New Haven ‘too much’ or ‘too little’?” For some seniors, attending Yale meant becoming a New Haven resident — which means that their alma mater needs to give back. “I hope that a stronger relationship from this agreement will form for both Yalies who call New Haven a temporary home and residents who will live here with their families for years to come,” Balsirow wrote. In the University’s first voluntary contribution agreement, signed in 1990, Yale pledged to give around $1.5 million to the city each year.

title: Even after monthslong scandal, ex-chief remembered for community ties
subtitle: Karl Jacobson resigned from his post as the chief of the New Haven Police Department in January after being confronted with allegations of larceny. But locals still remember him for his “comforting” leadership and focus on community engagement.
author: Evelyn Ronan
text: It hasn’t been a career-high year for Karl Jacobson. In January, Jacobson, then the chief of the New Haven Police Department, abruptly resigned from his post as chief and later turned himself in on an arrest warrant for two charges of larceny. He allegedly embezzled a combined $85,500 from two city funds, one intended for paying confidential informants in the narcotics unit and the other for the city’s Police Activity League. According to an affidavit, Jacobson bet more than $4 million on online gambling platforms during the time he was allegedly embezzling the funds. In April, the chief pleaded not guilty to the two larceny charges against him. But even as his department moves on from him — on May 5, the city swore in David Zannelli, who was among the three assistant chiefs who confronted Jacobson with allegations of embezzlement — New Haveners remembered his tenure as an era of progressive strides on community engagement. “He had the closest relationship to affected communities of any chief I’ve seen,” Paul Bass ’82, the founder of the New Haven Independent and longtime reporter on local policing, said in a phone interview. “I’ve been shocked at how little I’ve seen of that personal scandal reverberating at a larger scale.” Bass elaborated that the grievances about Jacobson allegedly stealing funds were limited to him and did not taint the department at large.
