In a phone interview with the News, Mayor Justin Elicker said he didn’t doubt that Jacobson “cared about New Haven.” But he struck a colder tone, emphasizing that the transparency-focused approach that Jacobson championed also extended to his removal. “It’s important to understand the fact that holding Jacobson accountable builds more trust in the community,” Elicker said. “When we talk about accountability, this is what we mean.” According to Bass, it wasn’t policy shifts that marked Jacobson’s term, but his overall “de-emphasization of arrest” and a focus on community ties. “A good policy doesn’t work if you don’t have trust of the community, if you haven’t been in people’s kitchens and living rooms during tough times and helped them navigate crisis,” Bass said. Zannelli, an 18-year veteran of the NHPD, has suggested he wants to continue Jacobson’s legacy of strengthening collaboration between the police and local community. Jacobson’s people-first approach took center stage in the wake of Randy Cox’s arrest . New Haven police arrested Cox on June 19, 2022. He was handcuffed and placed in a police car without a seatbelt . The officer driving the car, Oscar Diaz , made a hard stop, which caused Cox to slam into the front of the vehicle. In body cam footage released promptly after the incident, Cox could be heard saying that his neck felt broken. Diaz called for medical assistance but continued driving instead of waiting for paramedics to arrive and attend to Cox, who was then dragged face down into a holding cell, according to the police report . He could not move and repeatedly tried to get the officers to stop moving him. Officers in the footage can be heard saying Cox was “faking it” and doing “extra s—” before they dragged him out of the vehicle. The officers’ “reckless” treatment of Cox, a New Haven Police Department Internal Affairs report found, led to his ultimate paralysis. Cox is now in a long-term care facility . Jacobson moved to release bodycam footage of the incident and address the officers’ violation of conventional policy immediately after the incident. In March 2023, after the release of the Internal Affairs report, Jacobson called for the dismissal of the involved officers, who were fired in August of that year. “I think the community has asked us to be transparent and swift,” Jacobson said while announcing his choice to call for the officers’ dismissal, according to News coverage from the time. “We have put a lot of thought and investigation into this decision. We want to make sure that everything is done right, so that through this hearing, justice can be done.” Bass speculated that if Jacobson had handled the incident differently, “we would have the potential for riots.” “He had such a great connection with the people who were understandably upset,” Bass said. “He and the mayor got so much good information right away, rather than being defensive, which another chief would’ve been.” Michael Lawlor, a former state representative and a professor at the University of New Haven’s Henry C. Lee College of Criminal Justice and Forensic Sciences, said that Jacobson reached out to Cox’s family immediately after the incident. “That instinct to do something very uncomfortable immediately is a significant attribute of Karl,” Lawlor said. Jacobson later instituted a new policy in reaction to the Cox incident dubbed “ medical Miranda ,” where officers transporting prisoners are required to ask if they need medical attention. Jacobson also co-signed a judge’s decision not to expunge the records of the officers involved in Cox’s arrest. Diaz, the officer driving the police car during the arrest, was later reinstated under Jacobson’s leadership. “He was big on procedural justice,” Lawlor said, describing it as a central aspect of Jacobson’s policing philosophy. “If you’re a cop and you’re dealing with a citizen, it’s extremely important to explain what you’re doing. He really instilled among the rank-and-file cops the importance of this.” Lawlor said Jacobson also emphasized policing strategies that acquainted officers with particular neighborhoods — such as walking beats, in which officers patrol neighborhoods on foot rather than in a car. “When I came to New Haven, I became a better cop and a better person because I walked a beat,” Jacobson said in a WNHH Community Radio interview six months into his term as chief. 15 years on, he still maintained community ties from that time, Jacobson said in the interview. Lawlor said he considers Jacobson’s community-focused approach to be the crux of his term as police chief. “If you’re gonna be a police chief anywhere, you have to be sort of a politician,” Lawlor said.“A lot of cops have a tunnel vision approach to their job, but as a chief, you have to deal with the community, you have to explain things, and you have to be comforting. Karl really had that skillset.” Bass, the longtime reporter on local policing, contrasted Jacobson’s controversy to a 2007 NHPD scandal in which a group of top cops stole thousands of dollars of reserved police department funds , prompting the departure of then-chief Francisco Ortiz. The NHPD appointed James Lewis soon after in 2008, a chief who had never before worked in New Haven and took an “aggressive” approach to policing reminiscent of NHPD’s militaristic crackdown in the 1980s. Lewis was responsible for 20 arrests in his first week as chief. Bass said the fact that the city hired Zannelli “from within,” instead of hiring an outsider, “means this is so different from 2008,” adding that Zannelli maintained his predecessor’s community-focused approach to policing. “Zannelli has really good relationships with people from working in different neighborhoods,” Bass said. “He’s built up an extraordinary level of goodwill and trust in the community.” Karl Jacobson was sworn in as police chief on July 7, 2022.

title: After deportation flight controversy, an uncertain future for Avelo
subtitle: Local activists say that it was their pressure campaign that led to the suspension of Avelo Airlines’ partnership with the Department of Homeland Security. The airline has cited a cost-benefit analysis instead.
author: Qamar Al-Tameemi, Evelyn Ronan
text: 2025 was a tumultuous year for New Haven’s leading airline. After Avelo Airlines announced last April that it would begin chartering deportation flights for the Department of Homeland Security, activists in New Haven and across the country organized a boycott and put up billboards publicly decrying the company’s decision. Local organizations suspended their partnerships with the company. After nine months of pushback, Avelo announced in January that it would pause the charter flights. Avelo representatives and community activists presented different claims about why the company ultimately ended its contract with DHS — and what the episode means for Avelo’s future in New Haven. “They’ve got a lot of work ahead of them to earn back the trust of this state, and I hope that they can,” Connecticut Attorney General William Tong wrote in a statement after Avelo announced it would end its partnership with DHS in January. The Connecticut attorney general’s office declined to comment for this article, directing the News to the earlier statement. Avelo’s leadership initially described its partnership with DHS as a means of boosting revenue amid poor performance at Tweed-New Haven Airport, where the airline dominates traffic. In an email to Avelo employees announcing the contract, CEO Andrew Levy cited his company’s poor performance and noted that the first quarter of 2025 yielded its worst quarterly results since 2023, the News reported at the time. A petition created by the New Haven Immigrants Coalition entitled, “We Won’t fly Avelo until they stop ICE flights!” received over 40,000 signatures. Several protests occurred against the airline at Tweed Airport, with an April protest drawing a crowd of about 200, including Sen. Richard Blumenthal LAW ’73, three New Haven alders and state Rep. Juan Candelaria. Avelo announced in January that it would pause charter flights for DHS. In an internal email to Avelo staff obtained by the News, Levy said the partnership “provided short-term benefits” but acknowledged that the revenue was not high enough to “overcome its operational complexity and costs.” Courtney Goff, an Avelo spokesperson, wrote in an email to the News that the company’s partnership with DHS did not impact the number of customers that chose to fly Avelo, citing increased customers on commercial service in 2025 compared to 2024. “We stopped DHS flying because it was too infrequent to produce the anticipated revenue,” Goff wrote in an email to the News. “We chose to utilize the planes elsewhere and recapitalize our network and fleet.” But local activists said it was the local community’s pressure campaign that ended Avelo’s collaboration with the DHS. “They say it was about cost and complexity, but community pressure told the real story,” CT Students For A Dream, a local organization involved in protesting against the airline, wrote in a social media post in January. In a case study by the Coalition to Stop Avelo, a national activist group organized in light of the company’s contract with DHS, organizers attributed Avelo’s decision to a “sustained pressure campaign that Avelo could no longer ignore” and acknowledged the alliance of various activists involved in protesting against Avelo. “Even though we were the ones who started with the campaign, at the end, there was this national pressure that really changed everything around what happened with this company,” John Lugo, the community organizing director of the local immigrant activism group Unidad Latina en Accion, said in a phone interview. Protests against the airline occurred at several other airports across the country, including Wilmington Airport in Delaware and Mesa Gateway Airport in Arizona. New Hampshire state Rep. Seth Miller created several billboards that urged, “Don’t Fly Avelo.” Anne Watkins, an organizer with the New Haven Immigrants Coalition, highlighted the diverse alliance of community members and organizations that committed to the Avelo boycott, including groups like the New Haven Federation of Teachers and community organizing group New Haven Rising. Regarding perceptions of Avelo now, Watkins said she thinks “people have a lot of different opinions about it.” “Some people feel like, ‘Okay, they stopped the contract. We should fly them again.’ Some people feel, ‘Yeah, they stopped the contract, but they never apologized. They never acknowledged the harm that they caused.’” Watkins said. Several local organizations, including Faxon Law New Haven Road Race and Puerto Ricans United Inc. severed ties with Avelo in light of their decision to partner with DHS. The road race terminated their existing sponsorship agreement with the airline in July. Puerto Ricans United Inc., which had previously received support from the airline, preemptively cut ties with the airline ahead of its 2025 Puerto Rican Festival. In July 2025, Mayor Elicker prohibited the use of public funds to fly Avelo in a memo sent to city employees, as reported by NBC CT . In a phone interview on Tuesday, Elicker said the city had since lifted the policy. Puerto Ricans United Inc. board president Joe Rodriguez wrote in an email to the News that since first announcing it would not again partner with Avelo, the airline had not approached the organization for a new sponsorship, and that the non-profit did not “plan to proactively reach out.” According to data from the United States Department of Transportation, Avelo’s parent company, TEM Enterprises, carried out 88.35% of flights at Tweed Airport from March 2025 to February 2026. Evelyn Ronan contributed reporting.

title: New Haven board gets first regular report on Yale police use of force
subtitle: Under an agreement reached in December, New Haven’s Civilian Review Board is set to discuss its first quarterly report from the Yale Police Department. From January to March, the department recorded five use-of-force incidents and no complaints.
author: Adele Haeg, Evelyn Ronan
text: It’s official now — a New Haven oversight board will begin reviewing reports on the Yale Police Department’s use of force, starting with a report covering the first quarter of 2026. The two-page report , provided by the chair of New Haven’s Civilian Review Board, is the first one the board will review under a new agreement between Yale and the city. Board member Sam Fawcett said the board would discuss the report during their regularly scheduled monthly meeting on Monday. After months of negotiations, the New Haven review board and the University in December reached an agreement that authorizes the board to review use-of-force reports and civilian complaints against the Yale Police. The 2019 ordinance that created the Civilian Review Board requires such an agreement with the University, but the board had not exercised formal oversight of the Yale Police until now. “We appreciate our strengthened relationship with the New Haven Civilian Review Board and look forward to our continued partnership,” Duane Lovello, the head of Yale Public Safety, wrote in an emailed statement. Thursday’s report from the police department’s Professional Standards and Internal Affairs Unit included five incidents involving the use of force between Jan. 1 and March 31. The incidents are recorded when an officer uses physical force beyond routine handcuffing or verbal commands during an encounter. Each of the five incidents in the new report was “found to be reasonable and within policy” by the Yale Police, the report says. The review board’s chair, AnneMarie Rivera-Berrios, said the board has seen “huge changes” in how New Haven’s two departments have collaborated since the board was formed in 2019 . She said the Yale Police Department has been more responsive since the December agreement than it ever had since she became the board’s chair in 2022 . After the Yale Police Advisory Board dissolved last year, the YPD did not have a process for civilian review of complaints and use of force reports. A new Yale Public Safety Advisory Board saw its first members appointed in November 2025, but its charter lacked the specific authority to review such cases. Yale publishes its own annual use-of-force report . In 2025, there were 18 reportable use-of-force incidents, and the report claimed that force was only used to “safely gain compliance” from those resisting arrest. Under the new agreement, the Civilian Review Board will also review civilian complaints against the Yale Police Department, in addition to use-of-force reports. Between Jan. 1 and March 31, the department did not receive any such complaints, according to Thursday’s report. The Civilian Review Board will meet on Monday at 6 p.m. on the second floor of City Hall.

title: Effort to dedicate corner to Local 34 founding member moves forward
subtitle: Two former members of Local 34, which represents the University’s clerical and technical workers, are pushing to rename the corner of College and Elm streets after John Wilhelm ’67, a founding member of the union.
author: Brett Penk, Jingchu Sophia Zhang
text: New Haveners and Yale student leaders have collected about 320 signatures on a petition to name the corner of College and Elm streets in honor of a man who helped establish Yale’s first employee union. The petition required 250 signatures to qualify for review by the president of the Board of Alders, Tyisha Walker-Myers, who may assign it to one of the board’s committees, according to Ward 1 Alder Elias Theodore ’27. Theodore said he recently submitted the petition to City Hall for review. Under the proposal, the corner would be dedicated to John Wilhelm ’67, who helped found Local 34, which represents the University’s clerical and technical workers. Last year, the union celebrated the 40-year anniversary of its first contract with Yale — a milestone that motivated locals Aldo Cupo and Cheryl Bergman to spearhead the renaming efforts, they said. Cupo and Bergman were also founding members of Local 34 and are now members of the Yale Unions Retirees Association. The pair said in a phone interview that they wanted to honor Wilhelm’s contributions to the union. According to Cupo and Bergman, Wilhelm led Local 34’s first contract negotiation processes and pushed for pay equity for male and female workers. Later in his career, Wilhelm was elected president of UNITE HERE, a national labor union that encompasses Yale’s Locals 33, 34 and 35. He retired in 2012. Cupo and Bergman started the renaming process by reaching out to Theodore, whose district includes the corner where College Street meets Elm Street. “I was really excited when Aldo and Cheryl reached out,” Theodore said. “We all recognize this as an opportunity to help educate on this history.” In order to gain city approval to rename the street corner, Cupo and Bergman had to collect 250 signatures, at least 166 of which were mandated to come from downtown residents. Theodore assisted them by partnering with the undergraduate organizations Students Unite Now and the Yale College Democrats to collect signatures from Yalies, many of whom live in the downtown area. Audrey Landeros ’27, a community engagement fellow for the Yale College Democrats, described Yale students as being “incredibly positive, super enthusiastic to sign and give someone that honor” of having a street corner named for them. Cupo and Bergman said that the corner of College and Elm is symbolic for the union. For the past four decades, they said, Local 34 members have met at First and Summerfield United Methodist Church, which is nested in the northeast corner of the intersection. The pair described the street corner as the dividing line between New Haven and Yale’s campus. Wilhelm said in a phone interview that he was humbled to learn of the renaming effort. “If you had asked me if that was okay, I would have said no because I don’t like glorifying myself,” he said of the renaming effort. If approved, the sign would also commemorate the work of all Yale union members in the past 40 years, Wilhelm said. “I’m humbled and I’m proud of the work that I and others did when I was there,” he said. “I am also really proud of the amazing progress that the people who have led the union of Yale have made since I left.” Now that the petition has surpassed the 250-signature threshold, Theodore said, several steps are in order. If Walker-Myers assigns the renaming effort to a committee, it would then hold a public hearing in which locals can weigh in on the petition, he said. “Hopefully, we can get this done in the next four to six months, so we’re hoping for a ceremony at the corner of College and Elm Street in the fall, and we would love to have many people there,” Theodore said. Wilhelm said he would attend the ceremony. Local 34 signed its first contract with Yale in 1985. Jingchu Sophia Zhang contributed reporting.

title: Union influence dominates New Haven alder elections. Is it faltering?
subtitle: The Board of Alders has long been controlled by representatives whose campaigns were backed by local unions. A fall race between two Yalies for a downtown alder seat marked a rare loss for one of New Haven’s most influential political forces.
author: Elijah Hurewitz-Ravitch
text: Early last September, days before the Democratic primary for Ward 1 alder, Elias Theodore ’27 gathered his supporters at La Cocinita Mexican Cafe. As they wolfed down rice and fajitas, the floppy-haired New Havener gave an impromptu campaign speech . His opponent, Norah Laughter ’26, had the backing of the powerful Yale unions and New Haven Rising, an adjacent community advocacy organization. Laughter’s supporters, often identifying themselves as union workers, had taken to following would-be Theodore voters on walks to the polls, trying to persuade them to change their minds. “It has been intimidating at times these past few days,” Theodore told the crowd at La Cocinita. “I know that there are a lot of people with a lot of power and a lot of money who are against us, which is funny to say, because these are traditionally, I think, the groups that get seen as grassroots. We are the ones running the grassroots campaign.” In the last days of the race, Theodore bumped up against one of the most influential forces in New Haven politics: the coalition of Yale’s UNITE HERE unions and New Haven Rising. “The union really wants this seat, and that has become abundantly clear this past week,” Theodore said in September. “I want to be an alder who solely acts and votes on behalf of my constituents.” Three days after the event at La Cocinita, Theodore beat Laughter by 125 votes . The unusually competitive race to represent the downtown ward that encompasses part of Yale’s campus on the Board of Alders offered a window into the unions’ role in New Haven’s electoral politics. In a rare setback since their rise to political dominance, the unions’ organizing efforts fell short in swaying a local election. There are four unions headquartered in New Haven that belong to the UNITE HERE national labor organizing network. Local 217 represents hospitality workers across Connecticut. Local 34 represents Yale’s clerical and technical workers. Local 35 represents Yale’s service and maintenance staff, including dining hall workers. And Local 33, which officially unionized in 2023 after a protracted battle with Yale, represents the University’s graduate students. Together, the UNITE HERE unions represent thousands of Yale workers. And since 2012 — after the unions organized a slate of candidates that won seats on the Board of Alders — their candidates have maintained a majority on the board, the legislative arm of New Haven’s government. “We have 23 out of 30 allies on the Board of Aldermen,” Local 35 president Bob Proto said at a 2017 meeting of Locals 34 and 35, according to the New Haven Independent . Local 35’s chief steward, Ward 23 Alder Tyisha Walker-Myers is the board’s president. Elijah Hurewitz-Ravitch, Staff Photographer A machine Before their candidates won a majority on the Board of Alders in 2011, Yale’s unions spent decades building out their grassroots organizing power, according to Zachary Schwartz-Weinstein ’04, a historian of university labor. Then they made their push for City Hall. “You had regular, everyday, run-of-the-mill people running for political office to be able to make changes that people can feel,” Ward 22 Alder Jeanette Morrison, who was first elected as part of that 2011 slate, said. Since then, she has represented a district that includes a significant portion of Yale’s campus, including six residential colleges. Together, the unions spent around $200,000 on the 2011 effort, the New Haven Independent reported at the time. Union candidates won 14 of the 15 races against establishment Democratic candidates. In election cycles since then, political action committees associated with Locals 34 and 35 have contributed money to elect and reelect candidates to the Board of Alders. Many candidates have also received money from the Connecticut arm of the UNITE HERE TIP State and Local Fund, which is a “nonfederal” PAC, according to its website . Nonfederal PACs are not subject to federal campaign finance regulations. In fall 2025, the Connecticut TIP State and Local Fund spent $9,125 on local races. Much of that money went to political committees like “Eastside Dems United” that do not disclose the candidates they support. The Local 34 PAC spent $9,875 that fall. Both the Local 34 and the TIP PACs gave $1,500 to New Haven’s Democratic Town Committee, according to state campaign finance disclosures . Vincent Mauro Jr. , who chairs the committee, said that it solicits contributions from “all kinds of PACs” but that it gets a “significant portion” of its funding from UNITE HERE. That money goes towards registering voters and turning them out on election day, Mauro said. He added that the Democratic Town Committee is “politically aligned” with the local unions. “There’s a symmetry between good jobs, fair work, fair wages, a coalition built around not just the short term, but the long term future of the city of New Haven, its residents, its workers,” Mauro said. There are currently six first-term alders on the board, and three of them — Ward 8 Alder Mandy Martinelli, Ward 16 Alder Magda Natal and Ward 18 Alder Leland Moore — received money from union PACs in their campaigns, disclosures show. Of the 24 other alders, at least 16 have received financial support or endorsements from UNITE HERE unions. Two alders — Ward 2 Alder Frank Douglass Jr. and Ward 29 Alder Brian Wingate — are vice presidents of Local 35. That union’s chief steward is Walker-Myers, the ward 23 alder who is the president of the Board of Alders. Ward 25 is represented by Adam Marchand GRD ’99, the chief steward of Local 34. Over the years, union issues have come to dominate local politics, local experts say. David Schleicher, a Yale Law School professor and an expert on urban politics, said that “on the core things 35 and 34 care about” — the relationship with Yale — union-backed alders don’t have “too much range to do anything else.” Yale’s unions have long pushed for the University to give more to New Haven, and Mayor Justin Elicker — who beat a UNITE HERE-backed incumbent in 2019 but has since forged a sometimes uneasy alliance with organizers — consistently thanks the unions for their efforts on that front. In January, Walker-Myers joined other union leaders in front of Sheffield-Sterling-Strathcona Hall, which houses Yale President Maurie McInnis’ office, to call for Yale to pay $110 million to New Haven each year. In March, Elicker and McInnis stood on the second floor of City Hall to announce a new voluntary contribution deal in which Yale will pay New Haven around $230 million over the next seven years. Walker-Myers, who did not respond to repeated requests for comment, did not attend the announcement. The UNITE HERE-New Haven Rising coalition is “somewhere between” a local political party and an interest group, Schleicher said. The unions make endorsements and develop a platform on a variety of issues — some of which are directly related to their interests and some of which are not, he added. A major source of the unions’ power in aldermanic elections is simply the fact that there are not other organized groups that get involved, according to Schleicher, “which makes sense, because it’s a very small city.” In a Democratic-dominated urban context, the unions fill a void that competing political parties would otherwise occupy, Schleicher said. New Haven Rising and the unions largely operate in tandem, often hosting rallies together. The group is also financially tied to the UNITE HERE unions. The 501(c)(4) organization’s 990 filing from 2024 — the most recent year available — shows that it spent $378,078, most of which went toward compensation for officers and directors. Data from the United States Department of Labor shows that New Haven Rising received $326,950 from Local 34 that year, plus $348,985 from Local 217. As of publication, the Rev. Scott Marks — the director of New Haven Rising, vice chair of the Democratic Town Committee and a UNITE HERE union employee — has not agreed to an interview or responded to repeated requests for comment. The resistance Efforts to disrupt union dominance emerged not long after 2011. A slate called Take Back New Haven — spearheaded by then-Ward 7 Alder Doug Hausladen ’04, who now heads the city’s parking authority — coalesced in 2013, though it quickly fizzled out . Hausladen and several other alders in 2014 formed a coalition called the People’s Caucus that aimed to counter union influence on the board. The effort appears not to have gotten very far. In 2023, Frank Redente Jr. won the Democratic primary in Ward 15 against a UNITE HERE-backed incumbent. “Obviously, it’s public knowledge that they campaigned against me during my election,” Redente said, referring to the unions. He added that before that election, he had felt frustrated by the unions’ “blanket endorsement ” of candidates. Ward 10 Alder Anna Festa, who was elected in 2013 after an uncontested Democratic primary, was part of both the Take Back New Haven slate and the People’s Caucus, and she is the longest-serving member of the Board of Alders who was elected without union backing. “Every institution in the city, whether it’s labor or large employers or developers or government itself, should have a seat at the table, but no one should control the table,” Festa said. “At the end of the day, our responsibility is to the public, to make decisions that are fair, fiscally responsible, and in the best interest of the entire city.” Festa supports labor organizing but is skeptical of union hegemony, she said. “Their influence has to be balanced,” she said. “And our job is to make sure that no single group has so much influence that it limits our transparency and our accountability and our good decision making.” Leslie Radcliffe, a longtime civic leader and the former chair of the City Plan Commission, has been a frequent critic of the Yale unions. She is also a former steward of Local 34. “Every citizen in New Haven is not a Yale employee,” she added. “And when the focus of those alders are Yale, Yale, Yale, for me, it’s like, you’re ignoring the non-Yale employees.” There’s been another battle brewing in the Hill’s Ward 3, a district that includes Yale-New Haven Hospital and the Schools of Medicine and Public Health. Miguel Pittman, a businessman and restauranteur , has spent the past few years trying to disrupt UNITE HERE’s hold on Ward 3. After Ron Hurt, a close ally of the Yale unions and of New Haven Rising, resigned in 2024, Pittman faced off in a special election against Angel Hubbard, a community organizer backed by several local elected officials. Pittman lost by just 16 votes. He lost again to Hubbard in the Democratic primary in the fall and, running on both the Independent and Republican lines, in the general election . Each of those races has been highly competitive, driving up voter turnout in a ward that has had low political engagement in recent history. Hubbard said that the Yale unions have a strong influence on the board, particularly “because a lot of us are union workers. And you know, for any job, you will want a union.” She added that the UNITE HERE unions stay engaged in local politics at all times — not just during campaign season — and hold alders accountable. In Ward 3, Hubbard said, “when I hold community meetings, they are there. When the CMT has meetings, they are there. So they’re not just around when it’s campaigning time. No, they are always present.” In that sense, Hubbard said, the Yale unions are involved in “basically” all realms of city governance. Reflecting back on his primary, Theodore feels he underestimated how much it was shaped by larger forces. “I hadn’t realized that this race could be so imbued with city-wide dynamics and union dynamics,” he said in an interview this month. “There were a bunch of people in Local 34 and Local 35 out canvassing. And I think I was naive, but I didn’t think of this race much beyond the few hundred stakeholders who live in these downtown apartments and Yale undergrads.” After his win, Theodore said he had to work hard to repair ties the election had strained. Theodore said his campaign was not meant to affront union power, but he acknowledged that his outsider status created tension with one of New Haven’s strongest political forces. “I don’t want my campaign to seem — it was not one that was anti-union, and it was never meant to be one to degrade their power. But, yeah, I mean, I think, I think that dynamic was at play.” Now, seven months after that election, Theodore is a member of a board built in large part by the unions, and he is navigating relationships with the same people whose volunteers followed his supporters to the polls. “I’m just still, like, trying to be very conscious of — like, union relationships are good, but I think I still feel very, kind of, anxious about saying something that will be misconstrued,” he said. When pressed, Theodore declined to say why he is anxious about what he says. Schleicher thinks union power may be waning. “There was a period when everyone understood them to be the most powerful force in local politics,” Schleicher said. “It’s not clear that’s true anymore, and it’s also not clear that the place where they’re powerful, which is in the board, is not the center of politics, right? ” Factions? Or fantasy? John DeStefano, New Haven’s mayor from 1994 to 2013, pushed back on a narrative of union dominance. For DeStefano, it is inaccurate to define alders elected to the board with union support as “purely UNITE HERE alders.” The former mayor said that the unions have had a much larger impact on the local Democratic party than they have had on city governance. “For sure, UNITE HERE in 2011 decided to, in quotes, take control of the Board of Alders. But I don’t think it was to some purpose,” DeStefano said. “Maybe we all imagined this to be a bigger thing than it was.” Morrison, the Ward 22 alder who is a steward at a state employees union, agrees. She said that UNITE HERE-affiliated alders check their union affiliation at the door. But she said they never stop thinking “about their role as an advocate, regardless if they’re a union steward or a legislator.” “It’s about making sure that the needs of the people are met,” Morrison added. “That’s why I say it’s kind of blurred. It’s the same goal.” Mauro, the chair of the Democratic Town Committee, referring to the unions, said: “I think sometimes people make them out to be the big bad wolf. And I think that’s an unfair criticism.” For Douglass, the Ward 2 alder and Local 35 vice president, concerns about tension between union interests and constituent interests are unwarranted. “No, we’re gonna shut that down right there,” he said. “They can keep their opinion. I know why I’m here. I’m here for my community.” Ian Dunn, the UNITE HERE unions’ spokesperson, said that he was “a little reluctant to engage overall.” “We don’t want to engage in this story,” Dunn said, clarifying that he was referring to the Locals 33, 34, 35 and 217 and New Haven Rising coalition. He questioned whether speaking about that coalition’s approach to politics was part of its current “message” or “program.” “The zeitgeist in New Haven has often been, like, ‘The unions are bad. What the unions are doing in politics is bad.’ We’re like, ‘Why? Why? Why?’ It’s working people representing working people. What’s wrong with that?” Dunn added. Dunn framed the Yale unions’ political involvement as self-evident rather than strategic. He rejects the premise that unions need a formal rationale for political engagement. The mayor and the Board of Alders before 2011 were much more closely aligned with Yale than they are now, Dunn said. “Is that good for New Haven to have the billion dollar company — the company town controlling everything?” he asked. Dunn said that he could not answer questions about the political engagement of Locals 34 and 35 without approval. He said that Marks, the New Haven Rising director, was “totally the right person” to answer questions about the Yale unions. Marks did not respond to repeated requests for comment. “The beauty of a union, right, is it’s not any individual’s choice to do anything,” Dunn said. “We collectively decide what we’re going to do. It makes things really slow and painful sometimes.” When it comes to the Board of Alders, some say the split between alders that are aligned with Yale’s unions and those that are not is overstated. For Redente, walking into his first board meeting with a president who had actively campaigned against him was “weird, to say the least” — but he said that those dynamics quickly evaporated. “I don’t think there’s camps. No, I would have thought that going in, but being there, no, I think everybody’s been very welcoming,” Redente added. Ward 7 alder Christine Kim ’99, who was sworn in just last month, agreed that there are not distinct camps and said she has not observed “this kind of conspiratorial type of work going on.” “I had heard that there’s like a UNITE HERE and non-UNITE HERE,” Theodore said. “And I just — I think it’s not that black and white.” Theodore, who beat the unions’ candidate, is now backing an effort to name a street corner after John Wilhelm ’67, formerly an influential Local 35 employee, a founding organizer of Local 34 and a longtime president of UNITE HERE itself. The signpost would stand at the corner of Elm and College streets. That’s where Old Campus faces First and Summerfield United Methodist Church, the headquarters of New Haven Rising and the Yale unions.

title: Police cameras keep eyes on New Haven. Will city scrutinize them back?
subtitle: In the past five years, the New Haven Police Department has expanded a surveillance system that lets crime analysts see what’s happening across the city. Questions about the system’s effectiveness and privacy implications remain unanswered.
author: Adele Haeg
text: At first, they are hard to notice. But start to see them, and they seem to be everywhere. In downtown New Haven, there are two on the public library across from the Green, another on City Hall, four more between the district attorney’s office and the courthouse: surveillance cameras. Each sends footage directly to New Haven’s Real Time Crime Center, a surveillance hub in the city police department where analysts monitor citywide cameras 16 hours a day, responding to officers’ calls. Cameras are always recording, and footage is stored for at least two weeks, according to a spokesperson for the New Haven Police Department. Alexandra Kenealy, one of the center’s crime analysts, first saw a Real Time Crime Center while interning at the Bridgeport Police Department in 2023. She remembers experiencing a “wow factor” when she stepped in, she said, gesturing at several TV-size screens in front of her desk in New Haven’s Real Time Crime Center. “I didn’t realize that you can monitor the city like this,” Kenealy said as she clicked through camera feeds, touring the city from her computer. “It’s insane. But I felt a sense of safety.” In the past five years, the New Haven Police Department has expanded its surveillance system, installing hundreds of cameras, license plate readers and ShotSpotter microphones — which detect gunshots — across the city. The police have asked residents to connect their Ring cameras to a department registry and deployed drones with video capability. In 2022, the department also started using StarChase, a technology that allows officers to shoot rubber GPS trackers at cars instead of pursuing them at high speed. Officers don’t need warrants to deploy StarChase, which Officer Christian Bruckhart, the department spokesperson, said is generally considered an exception to Fourth Amendment privacy rights. This surveillance system grants the police department “a lot of power,” Ward 29 Alder Brian Wingate, who chairs the aldermanic Public Safety Committee, said. Alders held public hearings before purchasing cameras, but Wingate said that most residents still are not aware of how extensive New Haven’s surveillance system is. And it is continuing to expand. Last month, the city received a $750,000 grant from the state to expand StarChase and purchase more automated license plate readers and tire deflation devices. Kenealy said last month that the police department was looking to hire another crime analyst. “The biggest question I have is, where do you cross the line with privacy?” AnneMarie Rivera-Berrios, chair of the city’s Civilian Review Board, which handles civilian complaints against the police, said. “Is it okay to put these cameras in place if we don’t have these answers yet?” Martin Peralta, Head Photography Editor A surveillance hub When a shot is fired in New Haven, all of the city’s police officers are instantly alerted on their department-issued smartphones. Each time Kenealy gets a ShotSpotter notification, she immediately pulls up camera feeds in the area of the gunshot as dispatchers send officers to the scene. Kenealy’s task is to give officers what experts call a “tactical advantage” when they arrive at the scene. That could mean describing a suspect’s car, clothing and direction of travel over the radio, or sending photos or videos to cruiser computers, so officers know exactly who they are looking for and where they are going. Stationary and police-car cameras can also automatically identify license plate data, which officers can access and send to Kenealy. “Ten, fifteen years ago, when we didn’t have all these cameras, these people could have been like a ghost,” Kenealy said. According to Kenealy, the police department doesn’t have cameras “in every single intersection, but for the most part, our areas are pretty well covered” — particularly high-traffic areas or crime “hotspots.” There are cameras in each of the city’s 10 policing districts, but the city does not disclose their locations. When she’s not actively responding to a crime, Kenealy keeps cameras around District 1 — which covers downtown — on the main screens, she said, “since there's a lot of traffic” there. Kenealy said she most frequently responds to car thefts and other larcenies. She mentioned a recent case in which Real Time Crime Center technology helped the department apprehend a woman who had stolen $100 from a CVS pharmacy. “A crime is a crime, and if you can get a record of it, it definitely just helps for closing a case out,” she said. “No crime is too small.” The department stores camera footage for 15 to 30 days, and the footage can be searched as needed by anyone in the department, according to Kenealy. Andrew Guthrie Ferguson, an expert on privacy and policing and a law professor at George Washington University, said a Real Time Crime Center gives officers an “impressive level of observational power.” New Haven Mayor Justin Elicker said in a phone interview that “reasonable people” can disagree about whether cameras make cities safer, or just give that illusion. Even without the guarantee that they will deter crime, or proof that they allow the department to solve crimes faster, Elicker said that giving up a measure of anonymity is “a sacrifice that most people are willing to make.” ‘We needed every tool possible’ In 2001, the New York Police Department became the first department in the United States to set up a Real Time Crime Center, according to Kimberly Przeszlowski, an assistant professor of criminal justice at Quinnipiac University. New Haven started to develop its own in 2020, which Przeszlowski called a year of “peak growth” in Real Time Crime Centers nationwide. There was “peer pressure” on New Haven to level up, she added. Wingate also attributed New Haven’s increased spending on surveillance technology to the police department’s retention issues in the early 2020s. “We needed every tool possible to keep New Haven safe,” he said, emphasizing that the department “didn’t have a lot of manpower.” Bridgeport and Hartford both have Real Time Crime Centers. According to a press release from Milestone — a company from which both Hartford and New Haven buy cameras — Hartford had a “staggering 442% increase in case solvability for firearm assault incidents with video evidence” during a two-year study starting in January 2022. The company appears not to have conducted such a study in New Haven. Since 2021, the city has committed about $13 million to contracts with Axon, a police and military technology company, and Fusus, a surveillance company Axon acquired in 2024. That money has come from the city budget and funds from the American Rescue Plan Act, a 2021 pandemic relief bill, according to public records of the agreements. In 2021, the NHPD signed its first five-year contract with Axon for tasers, improvements to interview room equipment, dashboard cameras for police cruisers and body-worn cameras, which the department began using in 2017. The city bought 500 surveillance cameras that year, 300 of which have been installed so far, according to Elicker. Axon provides storage for camera footage on a website called evidence.com, according to Ferguson, the privacy expert. Elicker said the companies “have made written commitments to us that we own the data.” Ferguson said Axon has been “more transparent” than other surveillance companies, conducting bias checks on their technology. Still, he said, “the technology is moving faster than law and regulation.” “I think the cameras and the money that we spent on the surveillance has been beneficial,” Wingate said. Wingate said surveillance is one aspect of a “holistic” approach to policing, and that a recent downturn in crime has come as a result of “communication with the community.” Acting Police Chief David Zannelli, who has been with the police department for 18 years and whom Elicker nominated last month to become the permanent chief, emphasized that the new technology, particularly the citywide cameras, has increased the department’s efficiency. “The expansion of the cameras has really helped us in the last couple of years to recover vehicles and people involved in gun violence rather quickly,” Zannelli said in a phone interview. “Now that we have equipment that is set up there, we're able to respond quickly, and not just respond quickly, find the right person and the right car.” Data as to whether the Real Time Crime Center is helping the department solves crime faster is “pending,” Zannelli said. Martin Peralta, Head Photography Editor Concerns about expansion Axon and Milestone, two surveillance companies that the department has partnered with, both sell artificial intelligence technology. Other Real Time Crime Centers employ two types of surveillance cameras: closed-circuit television, or CCTV, and cameras with the capacity for video analytics, which can turn a city into “a trackable object tracing system,” according to Ferguson. Officers can use video analytics to search footage for specific objects. Bruckhart said the department does not use video analytics. He declined to specify the make or model of its cameras. “We don’t provide specifics for the cameras as we don’t want the exact capabilities of them made public,” Bruckhart wrote in an email. He added that the department uses “a variety of vendors depending on who is cheaper.” “One can have video without video analytics. It is just super time consuming to find or use anything for court,” Ferguson wrote in an email to the News. According to Bruckhart, right now, the New Haven police’s crime analysts and detectives manually search footage themselves, although it’s inconvenient. When the city reupped its first contract with Axon in 2024, it signed on to the possibility of implementing more advanced Axon technology, including Axon Investigate Pro and Axon Redaction Assistant, which Bruckhart said the department is not currently using. “We have a contract with Axon in which various products are negotiated,” like tasers, body cameras and data storage, according to Bruckhart, but the department does not “provide details on those negotiations because it could affect future contracts.” Under the 2024 contract, the department also purchased Draft One — an AI-assisted police report drafting tool — and Axon video drones with the ability to play pre-recorded messages. The chief state’s attorney and the state’s attorneys for Connecticut’s 13 judicial districts this month issued a moratorium “on the use of AI tools for drafting or narrating police reports,” according to Alaine Griffin, a spokesperson for the chief state’s attorney. The department had been “experimenting” with Draft One, according to Bruckhart. ‘I wouldn’t want Big Brother’ The New Haven Independent reported in 2024 that the city operates 190 “regular cameras” and 183 automated license plate readers. Przeszlowski, the Quinnipiac University professor, said the New Haven Police Department has access to footage from over a thousand cameras, some of which are businesses’ or private Ring cameras. Chelsea Infinity-Gonzalez, the director of public policy and advocacy of the American Civil Liberties Union of Connecticut, said that when a city expands its surveillance system like New Haven has, “the question isn’t just about the number of cameras, but what rules are actually in place to govern that data.” The ACLU of Connecticut has advocated for limits on the police’s retention of license plate reader data, meaning that data would be deleted after seven days if not saved. With automated license plate readers, “you can query your plate and see where it’s been across multiple locations,” Infinity-Gonzalez said, “so from a privacy perspective there’s a strong argument and we support having stricter retention limits.” Zannelli proposed that the department should be able to keep license plate data for six months. There is no current Connecticut law limiting license plate data retention. “How do we know that they’re not just going to hide their vehicle used in the drive-by shooting in a garage somewhere for a week and then just start driving around?” Zannelli said. “I wouldn’t want Big Brother, but that’s not the intent of it.” In 2017, before New Haven had a Real Time Crime Center, Sean O’Brien, the founder of the Privacy Lab at Yale Law School’s Information Society Project, decided he would try to map the city’s surveillance cameras. When O’Brien posted pictures of cameras on State Street that year to a website called Open Street Map, he said he “would come back the next day and there would be a traffic cone over one of them.” Others, he said, “would look like they’d been hit with baseball bats, or would be spray painted black.” He hasn’t mapped the new cameras — the Open Street Map of New Haven’s surveillance cameras was last updated by the Privacy Lab in 2017. For O’Brien, the police department’s surveillance has gone too far. “If your neighbor had a camera pointing into your window and you knew that they were watching it on their monitor or on their smart TV in their living room, watching a feed of your living room,” he said, it would be “very visceral and very creepy.” But, he said, “when we have cameras everywhere outside all the time, even if they’re effectively doing this at a massive scale, we don't have the same feelings because we can externalize it and not have to think about it as strongly.” O’Brien believes New Haveners should be paying attention to surveillance. “People deeply care about privacy,” O’Brien said, “yet feel powerless to do anything about it.” O’Brien has seen what he calls “privacy nihilism” take over. “Every once in a while, you know, perhaps we need to take a peek in to see what’s going on with that kind of level of power,” Wingate said of police surveillance. New Haven’s Real Time Crime Center is in the police department’s headquarters at 1 Union Ave.

title: 91 percent of senior class has used AI for schoolwork, News survey finds
subtitle: Results from a survey of the class of 2026 indicate that the use of artificial intelligence is widespread among graduating seniors. According to the survey, 48.5 percent of respondents said they used AI in writing their senior theses.
author: Hari Viswanathan
text: This year’s graduating seniors were first years when OpenAI released ChatGPT. As the first class with the opportunity to use large language models like ChatGPT during every year of their college career, most seniors have now incorporated artificial intelligence into their lives to assist them for various purposes, from aiding with problem sets to researching theses. According to an anonymous survey conducted by the News and filled out by 172 seniors, 91 percent of the class of 2026 have used AI for schoolwork. Only 9.1 percent of students surveyed said they had never used AI for schoolwork. A majority of students — 67.5 percent — reported using AI sometimes, often or very often. More than 75 percent of respondents said they have used AI in a problem set. About 64 percent of respondents said they have used AI to write a paper, while 48.5 percent of the surveyed seniors said they used AI to write their senior theses — slightly less than the 51.5 percent that said they did not. Joey Tan ’26, who is a computer science major, described AI as a “supplementary tool” that she utilizes alongside other forms of technology. “I’ve used it to look up things that I want, like a more in-depth answer compared to Google, for instance,” Tan said in a phone interview. “Or I’ll do a mix of Google searching and AI.” She said that she thinks one of the most evident impacts of AI is on office hours, adding that students can now use AI, instead of tutors and undergraduate learning assistants, to teach them content and help them through problems. “It’s kind of unfortunate, because I made so many of my computer science friends in office hours,” Tan said. “It was, like, a really important community for me. It was how I found people that take upper class electives with me later on.” Aviv Melamud ’26, another computer science major, said that using Claude — the large language model developed by Anthropic — to work through problems is like having a virtual teaching assistant. “It basically killed office hours,” Melamud said in a phone interview. Who is using AI? Male respondents were more likely to report that they used AI for schoolwork than female respondents. While 16.7 percent of male respondents said they used AI “very often” for schoolwork, only 1.6 percent of female respondents said they did. According to the survey results, students majoring in the sciences were most likely to report AI use for schoolwork, followed by those in the social sciences, those in interdisciplinary majors and those in the humanities. Brian Moore ’26 noted that in the humanities, AI can serve a similar function as writing partners in the Poorvu Center for Teaching and Learning, improving things like grammar, syntax and diction. “I tend to see this as polarizing. There’s a lot of folks who won’t touch AI with a 10-foot pole, and others who will readily adopt it,” Moore, who is majoring in humanities, said in a phone interview. Melamud said he thinks AI will not render STEM classes obsolete. He compared the experience of learning computer science with AI to that of learning math with the aid of a calculator. Professors will have to grapple seriously with the advancement of AI technology, Moore said, but he added that there are creative ways in which professors can weave AI into class assignments and pedagogy. “I guess, in some ways, AI is prompting us to re-examine what the philosophy of education is, right?” Moore said. ChatGPT was released on Nov. 30, 2022.

title: With high hopes, Yale continues to invest in Upper Science Hill
subtitle: The University has poured hundreds of millions of dollars into the development of Upper Science Hill, which University leaders say will significantly improve research opportunities at Yale. The first phase is set to be completed this fall.
author: Sara Agrawal
text: One corner on campus has experienced a particularly expensive transformation over the past four years. The University had poured hundreds of millions into the development of Upper Science Hill, with the aim of setting Yale apart in the fields of physics, the applied sciences and engineering. University leaders have consistently emphasized their hopes to place Yale at the forefront of cutting-edge scientific fields like quantum computing. “The USHD reflects a long-term commitment to securing Yale’s leadership in scientific discovery and innovation in order to contribute meaningfully to addressing the world’s present and future challenges,” Michael Crair, Yale’s vice provost for research, wrote in an email to the News, referring to the Upper Science Hill Development project. In a February interview with the News, Yale President Maurie McInnis identified investing in the physical sciences and engineering as the “top priority” of the University, and in her end-of-year message to the Yale community, she singled out the University’s “strides in reimagining Science Hill.” When Linda McMahon, the United States’ secretary of education, toured Yale in April, she called the construction on Upper Science Hill Construction “incredible.” Yale’s 2023–24 financial report stated that out of the University’s $614 million in capital spending, which includes “renovations” and “acquisitions and new construction,” 12 percent — around $75 million — went into biological and physical sciences. That figure rose to over $190 million — 29 percent of $661 million — in the 2025 fiscal year . When asked why he thought Yale was investing so heavily in engineering and physical sciences, physics professor Karsten Heeger emphasized the importance of Yale’s frontier scholarship in those fields as well as the humanities. “Yale has always been leading in various fields, including the humanities, for example,” Heeger, who is also the director of the Yale Wright Laboratory for experimental nuclear physics, particle physics and astrophysics, said. “Science and engineering and technology is absolutely essential for society and our modern world. If we want to be a leading research university, we have to be cutting-edge in those fields.” The development of Upper Science Hill is a decade-long project that has been planned in two phases. According to Heeger, the first phase, which began in 2022 and broke ground in 2024, is set to be completed this fall. This phase involves the construction of a new Advanced Instrumentation Development Center and replacing an old part of the Wright Lab. Heeger said that the renovation will bolster the Wright Lab with “state-of-the-art laboratories that will give us the vibration isolation, the environmental control, all of the things that we need, the infrastructure for cutting-edge research.” That transformation will provide new research opportunities for both undergraduate and graduate students, Heeger added. The second phase of the Upper Science Hill development project will see the completion of a new Physical Sciences and Engineering Building, or PSEB, Heeger said. “The idea was to build a building complex that brings together research groups from physics, physical sciences, and the engineering departments and mainly with the focus on quantum science and material science,” he said. Crair wrote that the building complex will foster “cross-disciplinary collaboration.” In total, the building will house around 50 research groups, according to Heeger. Heeger also said that the physics department has made new appointments in quantum science and atomic, molecular and optical physics, but that exact final number “remains to be seen.” The development of Upper Science Hill follows a 2018 report of the University Science Strategy Committee, or USSC, which states that “tomorrow’s leaders need a solid grounding in STEM so they can understand the world and shape its future” and outlines investments the University might make to improve STEM at Yale. The report suggested that the University invest in graduate students, advance instrumentation, update core facilities and promote diversity in the STEM pipeline. The report also emphasized that the School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, or SEAS, would play a vital role in the future of science at Yale and “must be fully integrated into the fabric of the University.” In 2022, following another report from a SEAS strategic planning committee in 2021, the University announced major investments in applied sciences and engineering and the separation of SEAS faculty from the Faculty of Arts and Science. A focus on quantum Quantum science has been one of the focuses of Yale’s investments in engineering and applied science. The field, which studies the smallest units within atoms that act both as particles and as waves, is used both in physics and in computing and was one of the “five ideas of priority investment” highlighted in the 2018 USSC report. Speaking about the research in Wright Lab, Heeger said that “quantum science is intrinsic to all what we do.” Shaun Pexton ’26, the co-president of Yale Undergraduate Quantum Computing, called quantum the “shining beacon of Yale engineering” in an interview with the News. Pexton said that he’s noticed growing attention around quantum during his time at Yale as the “race” of quantum technology has made its way into “political conversations” and as Yale has gained recognition as a place where “a lot of cool stuff is happening.” In her end-of-year message, President McInnis said that the new facilities on Science Hill will “supercharge quantum science at Yale.” The Yale Quantum Institute will be relocated from Hillhouse Avenue to the PSEB, according to its website . In the message, President McInnis also mentioned professor Michel Devoret, who won a Nobel Prize in 2025 for his work in quantum physics , and QuantumCT , a nonprofit launched in 2022 by Crair and Pamir Alpay, the interim provost of the University of Connecticut. McInnis’ wrote in the end-of-year message that through quantum technology, “we will make our home state more attractive to start-ups” and “create a talented workforce in this area.” When asked why he thought the University was heavily investing in and promoting quantum, Pexton said that technological development, especially in AI and quantum, have become indicators of a university’s advancement. “Yale wants to be at the forefront, and they are at the forefront,” Pexton said. The construction on Upper Science Hill is located at 268 to 280 Whitney Ave.

title: After admin banner ban, YSE grads don anti-ICE t-shirts in class photo
subtitle: In a student-organized unofficial class photo, members of the Yale School of the Environment’s class of 2026 wore shirts depicting melting ice, a double entendre intended to highlight both the changing political and global climates.
author: Michelle So
text: Since the school’s founding in 1900, graduates of the Yale School of the Environment have taken an annual class photo behind Marsh Hall, where the original Yale Forest School was located . For over a century, these photos have captured the school through class size increases, the emergence of color photography and shifts in student body demographics. In recent years, the class photos have become a platform for School of the Environment students to make broader political statements. Members of the class of 2024 posed with pro-Palestinian protest materials, according to members of class of 2026, before the school updated its class photo protocol to prohibit signs and banners. This year, some members of the class of 2026 organized an unofficial class photo in which students donned t-shirts that depicted melting ice cubes. “The shirts were meant to be symbolic and hold two meanings: climate change is melting ice, and abolish ICE,” Riki Bertoldi ENV ’26, one of the photo’s organizers, wrote in a text to the News. On April 29, after the graduating students lined up for their official photo, nearly all of them stayed behind to change into the shirts and pose for a new photo, according to Bertoldi. She said that the idea for the unofficial photo had been conceived “months before.” Emily Kaufman ENV ’26 wrote in an email to the News that past students have presented more “explicit messages” in their class photos. “I really loved the class of 2024 photo when we saw a lot of protest signs, and I believe there was a big banner that said, ‘War is an environmental issue,’” Adrian Huq ENV ’26 said in a Zoom interview. “That really resonated with me as a peace advocate and anti-militarist sort of climate activist.” Huq said that administrators discouraged protest-related photoshoots after the 2024 display. Paige Stein, the school’s chief communications and marketing officer, wrote in an email to the News that in order to “emphasize the collective, inclusive nature of our school community,” the School of the Environment updated its “class photo protocol.” “Official photos are now taken without banners or signs,” Stein added. “The School of the Environment adheres to the university’s wider policy on freedom of expression.” According to the website of the office of the secretary and vice president for university life, the University prohibits large “banners held by multiple individuals” at outdoor events, but does not specify a policy prohibiting signs or banners in official photos. This year, as the annual class photo neared, Huq and Bertoldi said they worked to navigate around this updated policy change. “We decided to get creative and use textiles instead as a medium to express a collective statement in a way that still felt visible and meaningful to our community,” Bertoldi said. According to Bertoldi, Kaufman led much of the planning and coordination, while Brandon Hoak ENV ’26 created the initial design, produced the stencil and led the screen-printing process. “We made sure to communicate our plans with both international students and the broader class and asked whether people would feel comfortable with the shirts being worn during the official class photo,” Bertoldi wrote in an email. “Some students expressed discomfort, so we decided to take the shirt photo separately after the official class photo.” Bertoldi said she and other organizers invited other students to join the unofficial photoshoot through a WhatsApp group chat called “YSE Class of 2026,” which included every member of the graduating class. Students who wished to opt out were given the option to. According to Kaufman, the photo’s organizers “felt it was important to continue the tradition in a more subtle yet still powerful way” after they had elected not to wear the shirts during the official photo. “Through the drawing of melting ice, we wanted to show that environmentalism cannot be separated from the injustices currently facing immigrant communities and that YSE will continue our longstanding tradition of speaking out despite administrative pushback,” Kaufman said. Marsh Hall is located at 360 Prospect St.

title: Yale is building 263 new wells for geothermal energy
subtitle: Geothermal energy offers a cleaner alternative to traditional carbon-based fuels. As Yale invests in hundreds of geothermal wells, the technology may be the University’s ticket to fulfilling its zero-emissions commitment.
author: Michelle So
text: Natural gas provides roughly half of Connecticut’s energy, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration. While burning the fuel produces half the emissions as does burning coal, natural gas is not as green as one might think. Any carbon-based form of energy produces carbon dioxide and other greenhouse gases when combusted for energy. Greenhouse gas emissions are responsible for trapping heat in Earth’s atmosphere, which contributes to global warming. Yale has committed itself to achieving zero emissions by 2050, a goal towards which the University has slowly been advancing. Alternative forms of energy, including geothermal technologies, may be the ticket there. “Yale is moving toward geothermal energy as part of the university’s commitment to achieve a zero‐emissions (zero‐carbon) campus by 2050,” Andy Bromage, the sustainability communications officer for the Yale Office of Facilities, wrote in an email to the News. In order to achieve that goal, Bromage said, drilling is underway to install 263 geothermal wells on Upper Science Hill. The new plant will support buildings including the Yale Science Building, the Sloane Physics Laboratory, the Sterling Chemistry Laboratory, the Kline Chemistry Laboratory, the Chemistry Research Building and the Bass Center. Electricity is generated when water is heated to turn turbines that produce steam. Yale currently employs several means of energy generation, including natural gas power plants on Tower Parkway and Congress Avenue, wind turbines on the roof of the Becton Engineering and Applied Sciences building and solar panels on Kroon Hall, Fisher Hall and West Campus. The core change in Yale’s energy-generating system involves the replacement of steam-based heating with low‐temperature water — heated to around 120 to 140 degrees Fahrenheit — generated by an electric heat pump that will draw heat from deep underground. “Any move away from using fossil resources (carbon) and toward using renewable resources (i.e., those that don’t move carbon from underground storage to the atmosphere) is a step in the right direction and is a good thing!” Hanno Erythropel, a researcher at the Yale University Center for Green Chemistry and Green Engineering, wrote in an email to the News. “That is certainly the case for the Yale geothermal energy project.” Erythropel added that more specificity regarding the amount of emitted carbon dioxide that could be offset by the project would be helpful. Yale’s move toward geothermal energy follows a trend of universities moving towards cleaner energy. In 2024, The New York Times reported that other universities and colleges, including Princeton, Carleton College and Ball State University, were also tapping into the technology. “I think it’s a good push for Yale as a university and as a whole to make good on their carbon capture initiative while being more sustainable,” Naomi Orcel ’27, the president of Club Geo, an Earth and planetary science student organization, said. Orcel said she was initially irked that the construction on Upper Science Hill took away parking spaces for faculty, but that she changed her mind on the project after hearing its mission. “I think it’s a really good thing that we’re using geothermal energy,” she added, “because it’s not an energy source people usually think to use; people usually think like solar or wind.” The shift toward geothermal energy extends beyond Yale. New Haven received around $9.5 million from the Environmental Protection Agency to build a networked geothermal system to provide clean heating and cooling to the New Haven Union Station. The funding would also support the construction of a mixed-income housing development adjacent to the station. Yale’s Upper Science Hill Development’s new facilities for quantum science, engineering and materials science are designed to hopefully support both new construction and existing Science Hill laboratory buildings by providing electrified thermal energy and reducing the need to add capacity to Yale’s existing power plants and steam distribution system. The Upper Science Hill Development is scheduled to be completed in 2030.

title: New student-built app aims to make dining hall menus more accessible
subtitle: YaleMeals, an app that was recently developed by students in the Yale Computer Society, allows users to view the menus at various dining halls, as well as input their dietary preferences and coordinate meals with friends.
author: Anya Geist
text: When Yassin Mudawi ’28, a transfer student, arrived on campus last fall, he said he found Yale’s dining system confusing: There were so many dining facilities, all with varying closures, transfer restrictions and menu changes. Mudawi recently led a team at the Yale Computer Society to launch YaleMeals, an app that aims to offer an intuitive, easy-to-use menu guide to Yale’s dining options. The app was downloaded over 700 times by April 7, according to Mudawi. “For a new student coming here, it seemed to be a pain point for me personally, and how I approach software is I find a pain point in my life and then try to fix it,” Mudawi said in an interview, referring to Yale’s dining system. Mudawi wanted to create an easily navigable app. The Yale Hospitality website, which uses Nutrislice to display menus, has a lot of “nested pages,” Mudawi said. Nutrislice requires users to visit new pages to view a dining hall’s menu for each meal on a given day. Mudawi said he brought his idea to Yale Computer Society co-President Ryan Fernandes ’28 last fall, and the club decided to take on YaleMeals as a project. On YaleMeals, Mudawi tried to make it simple to switch between locations, meals and days. The app also allows students to customize its settings, including for their dietary preferences. The app features a “Friends” tab, where users can invite and schedule meals with their peers. “I noticed there’s a big meal culture here at Yale, where everyone wants to grab, like, lunch together or dinner together, and it’s really wholesome. So why not try to promote that and encourage that?” Mudawi said. Mudawi said he and his team hope students can use YaleMeals even in the case that Yale Hospitality’s database changes. He added that he built the YaleMeals code to make it easy to switch to a new interface for a changed database. Yale Hospitality spokesperson Alexa Gotthardt wrote in an email to the News: “We value student innovation and recognize the creativity students bring in developing new tools and platforms. At the same time, we encourage students to rely on Yale Hospitality’s official menus page for the most accurate and up-to-date menu information.” Other students have created similar apps to YaleMeals in the past, including Bulldog Menus, which was founded by Edmundo Chavez Ramirez ’28. Regarding similar apps created outside of the Yale Computer Society, Lily Lin ’27, the club’s co-president, said, “Our goal is not to monopolize the market.” YaleMeals builds on the work of Yale Menus, a previously popular app that also listed each dining hall’s menus. Yale Menus was originally launched by Eric Foster ’20 and his brother David Foster ’23 in 2017, and it was relaunched by David Foster and Erik Boesen ’24 in 2020, Boesen wrote to the News. After Boesen graduated, the app became challenging to maintain. “We negotiated a license to sell Menus to Yale Hospitality over a period of roughly 4-5 years,” Boesen wrote to the News. Ultimately, the sale fell through. Boesen, realizing the app was out of date due to changes to Yale Hospitality’s databases and programming interfaces, passed it on to the Yale Computer Society. While Yale Menus was not a project created through the club, Boesen wrote, he had been a club member as an undergraduate. “Yale Hospitality has previously explored partnership opportunities related to student-developed dining apps, including the ‘Yale Menus’ app,” Gotthardt wrote to the News. “At that time, we determined it was not the right fit, largely due to the operational complexity involved in maintaining a separate platform alongside our existing systems, which serve the same purpose.” Ruby Lytton-Hirsch ’29 frequently uses Yale’s Nutrislice app to look at lunch menus around campus. Looking at the menu in advance allows her to see whether she prefers the Greens and Grains option or the regular offering. However, Lytton-Hirsch said the Nutrislice app can be challenging to use, particularly because she has to scroll to the bottom of the page to see the main options for each meal. Lytton-Hirsch said she thought the YaleMeals app looked “prettier and easier to navigate” than Nutrislice. Similarly, Hrishita Shah ’28 said she found YaleMeals more “intuitive” to use than Nutrislice, though she added that she does not mind using Nutrislice because she has gotten used to it. Shah, however, noted that the YaleMeals app lacks reviews of the menu items. “The old Yale Menus app, it had a lot of reviews, like the Commons cookies had, like, 50 hearts,” Shah said, adding that “not a lot of people” use YaleMeals. “So, like, the s’mores brownies have zero ratings.” Rishi Sankhe ’28 sometimes uses YaleMeals, though he does not always feel the need to check dining hall menus before visiting. He finds the app easy to use, he said. Sankhe noted that he thought the YaleMeals team was continuing to make good changes to the app, including adding pictures of the dishes. Some students have never used YaleMeals. Zach Pan ’27 said he had not heard of YaleMeals or even the Nutrislice website. “I show up at the dining hall and eat what’s there,” Pan said. “I know I’m not going to spend additional funds on food from other sources. So I enter the dining hall, and I eat the food in the dining hall.” The dining halls serve chicken tenders for lunch every other Thursday.

title: Yale study finds link between cancer and living near factory farms
subtitle: A recent Yale study published in the journal Environmental Research found that living near concentrated animal feedlots or factory farms may be linked to higher cancer rates.
author: Michelle So
text: Earlier this year, Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. announced a revamp of the familiar food pyramid, with protein sources like red meat and full-fat dairy at the top of the list of recommended foods. Yet, certain studies have found consumption of red meat to be linked to cardiovascular diseases and cancers of the colon . Now, simply living near sites of red meat production may be linked to cancers as well. A Yale study published in the journal Environmental Research examined whether living in areas with higher densities of animal feeding operations and concentrated animal feeding operations is associated with cancer incidence. The feeding operations, commonly known as factory farms, often contain livestock in cramped conditions. Concentrated operations exceed a threshold population of 1,000 beef cattle, 700 dairy cows or 2,500 hogs. “These facilities are known sources of environmental pollution, including air pollutants (e.g., ammonia, particulate matter, hydrogen sulfide) and water contaminants such as nitrates, which may have implications for human health,” Jiyoung Son, a researcher at the School of the Environment and the paper’s first author, wrote in an email to the News. Using county-level data from California, Iowa and Texas between 2000 and 2021, the researchers found that counties with higher factory farm density had higher overall cancer incidence. The increased cancer incidence prevailed despite variations in factory farm makeup; for example, dairy operations are more common in California, swine in Iowa and beef cattle in Texas. Cancer isn’t the only risk associated with living near a factory farm. When animals live in close proximity with one another, disease transmission becomes nearly unavoidable. Farmers often add antibiotics to animal feed to prevent disease-related deaths and bolster livestock growth. Overuse of antibiotics can cause bacteria to develop antibiotic resistance. When runoff from these feedlots enters the environment, these superbugs may infect humans and other animals and remain unresponsive to treatment — a scenario that is dangerous to both livestock and human health. Cole Haynes ’27, the co-president of Plant Forward Yalies, a student group that promotes sustainable food systems, said he was not shocked by the study’s results. “While the findings of this Yale study are deeply concerning, they are unfortunately not surprising. The public health impacts of animal agriculture are widespread and well documented,” Haynes wrote in an email to the News. The study’s researchers still say more detailed research is needed. Son suggested implementing environmental monitoring, especially of air quality and drinking water contamination, and improving waste management and reducing emissions. Consuming red and processed meats is also associated with “pro-inflammatory mechanisms” that increase gastrointestinal and colon cancers and cardiovascular disease, John Dinkler, a cardiologist and the associate head of Timothy Dwight College, said.
