They work in industries that have done more than almost any other to narrow the geography of American opportunity, to hollow out the places they came from and to make the kind of upward mobility Yale claims to represent harder for everyone else. For the rural or first-generation student whose family sacrificed for the degree, that pipeline is not a promise kept. For the country that subsidizes Yale's tax exemption, it is a negative return on its investment. For the faculty who came to contribute to something larger than credential production, it is a quiet embarrassment. And the trust report does not mention any of this. Yale’s report was still circulating when emails obtained from San Francisco’s mayor’s office revealed that the University’s engineering dean had been developing a business plan for a satellite campus in the Bay Area. Yale’s spokesperson says there are no current plans. The emails suggest the conversation is further along than that. The move would follow Vanderbilt’s recent acquisition of the financially insolvent California College of the Arts, in what one Atlantic writer called the national-chain model of undergraduate education. The schools with the resources to expand are doing so toward the cities where talent and capital have already concentrated. While Yale measures its national impact on a state-by-state map , counting clinical trials and patents, its expansion strategy points in the same direction as its graduates . Just days after the trust report’s release, Yale quietly revised its mission statement, excising its commitment to improving the world and educating aspiring leaders. What remains is the preservation and dissemination of “knowledge,” in a narrowed ambition that happens to position the University perfectly to become a national leader in artificial intelligence. Researchers writing in this year’s Business and Society journal warned that generative AI threatens to degrade our collective knowledge ecosystem through what they call “ epistemic pollution ” or the dissemination of low-quality, misleading and fabricated information at scale. And the University that just committed itself to preserving knowledge is racing toward the industry most likely to corrupt it. The report identifies Yale’s predicament as a trust problem, but it is more precisely a resentment problem. Americans increasingly see elite universities and their graduates as participants in a system designed to concentrate wealth and opportunity for a small class of people who already possess most of both at their expense . A university that has revised its mission to withdraw its vision from the world beyond its walls, tracks its impact in patents while half its graduates funnel into extractive industries and is considering expanding toward the same coastal cities those graduates already cluster in is not answering that resentment; it is confirming it. Most of us will leave Old Campus for the last time after commencement with some sadness. There will be tears for the end of our shortest, gladdest years of life. But the conferral of our rights and responsibilities is not a farewell. It is a beginning. Gone are the days of the University for God and for country. But a University that exists only for Yale is not much use to the country that sustains it. So if the institution no longer takes the obligations embedded in the words of our commencement ceremony seriously, then we must. We know the rights: Yale clubs, library access, reunions, The Game and others. The responsibilities are real, too, and they are ours now as well. MICHAEL NDUBISI is a former editor of the Yale Daily News’ Opinion desk and writer of the fortnightly column “A More Perfect Union.” He is a graduating senior in Saybrook College majoring in History and Political Science. He can be reached at michael.ndubisi@yale.edu .

title: Consider our legacy
author: Emily Hettinger
text: We made it. After four years of essays, problem sets and a million choices to make about extracurriculars and internships and social groups, all that’s left is to design quirky hats and enjoy the momentous occasion of our commencement with our friends and family. But in the midst of all this joy, I think we should make one final commitment. We need to grapple with the fact that we are the beneficiaries of a deeply broken and unequal system. And as the class of 2026, we must use the privileges of our education to do something about it. It needs to be said: Yale has a wealth problem, uncomfortable as it may be to acknowledge. According to the New York Times , Yale accepts more students from the top one percent of the United States’ income distribution than the entire bottom sixty percent combined. Daniel Markovits ’91 LAW ’00 says it well in his 2019 book, The Meritocracy Trap: The wealth-based admissions system we have concentrates wealth and power in the hands of a small few, at the expense of political unity, upward social mobility for most Americans and the life satisfaction of even the most successful among us. But, to me, the deeper risk is that the disproportionate wealth at Yale denigrates the very idea of the liberal arts education. There are many reasons to choose a liberal arts education, but probably the main value of the liberal arts, especially in this moment, is that they can, allegedly, prepare citizens to be critical thinkers. In her 1963 book, Eichmann in Jerusalem , philosopher Hannah Arendt describes evil as banal, that most people who do evil things are not evil people but just people following orders. Critical thinkers, in my view, are the ones who say no to banal orders to do evil things. Or at the very least, stand up for what is right, even when it is easy to stay silent. For a school that claims a devotion to the liberal arts, I am concerned by our acceptance of the banal orders we are confronted with. Yale sends a disproportionate number of its students into fields that bring wealth and prestige to their workers, often at the expense of the common good. Places where it is someone’s job to cut costs in United States detention centers, turbocharge opioid sales and invest in fossil fuels. Is this how we want to be remembered? I think things would be different if we reimagined who a Yale education was for. For middle and working class students, a college education, much less an elite college education, is not inevitable. Paying for a school like Yale as a middle class family usually means pinching every penny for years, often to end up in serious debt . And coming to Yale from an underfunded public school often means being the only person from your high school at Yale, which can be an incredibly lonely and daunting experience. Taken together, the opportunity cost to attend Yale for non-wealthy students is quite high. As such, the very choice to attend a fancy school where life is put on hold for four years, just to think and grow, is grounded in the critical thought that the liberal arts are meant to cultivate. For these students, a Yale education is a service to something much larger than themselves, an undertaking on behalf of the community that sacrificed much to make the dream possible. If we had more students for whom this education carried a strong sense of community responsibility, maybe more people would dedicate themselves to the many problems in our world that need solving. We, as a generation of Yalies, have the power to change the definition of a Yale student, to utterly transform our admissions system into one that prioritizes the public good over wealth. But in order to do that, we need to acknowledge that regardless of how we all arrived at Yale, we leave with far more to personally gain from the status quo than we ever will from a reformed system. All of us will have access to opportunities that very few are afforded, simply because of our Yale diploma. All of us who choose to have children will be the parents of Yale legacies who will have an explicit advantage in the Yale admissions process. And even if legacy preference goes away, our kids will have disproportionate access to fancy private schools, music and sports lessons, test prep and all sorts of other things that will look good on college applications. If we change the system, we directly jeopardize our own children’s chances of following in our footsteps at Yale. So, to my peers in the class of 2026, what do we want our legacy to be? Though it will involve personal sacrifice, I think we ought to lead a transformation of Yale’s culture to prioritize the public over the self. We can do better than agnostically educating a disproportionately wealthy student body to develop skills used for personal benefit instead of the public good. In a country with increasing distrust in elites , it is our duty to do better. As my parting contribution to the News, I ask the class of 2026 to do one thing: Pull the ladder up behind you. Reject the structures that we will soon benefit from and call for something better, even though it may cost your future children the opportunity to study at Yale. Commit to organizing against legacy admissions at our university, reinvest the thousands of dollars you might have spent on private school tuition into your local community, surround yourself with people who did not receive an Ivy League education and demand that Yale serve the public good over its own interests. If we succeed, we will be remembered for something much more than clever hats on Class Day. EMILY HETTINGER is a senior in Pierson College studying Psychology and Education and a staff columnist for the News. Contact her at emily.hettinger@yale.edu .

title: The death of college exploration
author: Ryan Wang
text: If you read my application to Yale, you would probably guess that I spent my bright college years flitting between economic research seminars, physics labs and a political campaign office. Instead, I spent my first week of classes grinding through applications for the Yale Student Investment Group or the Yale Undergraduate Consulting Group. By the end of sophomore year, I had climbed the ranks of the consulting group and was running its client acquisition team. Academically, I dropped my physics major and rarely strayed outside of economics and mathematics classes. Sometimes, I mourn for the kid who eagerly submitted his Yale application. I suspect I am not alone. In his 1852 treatise “ The Idea of a University ,” the English theologian John Henry Newman argued that a college education should cultivate the intellect broadly, not provide vocational training. Newman was not alone in his position. Yale’s own 1828 report on academic instruction declared: “Our object is not to teach that which is peculiar to any one of the professions; but to lay the foundation which is common to them all.” Yet for many of us Yalies, this vision has not materialized. Somewhere between 1828 and today, the dream of spending our college years exploring our passions and career interests remains only that — a dream. Exploration is dead. The question is: who killed it? My first suspect is one familiar to most Yalies: our future employers and graduate schools. In the past two decades, the college internship has devolved from an act of exploration into a prerequisite for entry. During my time at Yale, top consultancies have steadily moved recruiting timelines earlier. The proliferation of sophomore summer internships and pre-professional clubs pressures new students to dedicate their underclassmen years to breaking into the industry. My friends in other fields tell the same story. The aspiring investment banker needs a boutique stint before junior year, the software engineer needs a project portfolio to capture the attention of tech companies, the mechanical engineer needs lab experience before finishing their core requirements and the future policymaker needs a D.C. internship before their first political theory seminar. Yalies interested in further academic study face the same curse. Matriculant GPAs at top medical and law schools have crept up consistently, while aspiring doctors now spend thousands of undergraduate hours on clinical experience and research to remain competitive. Students drawn to doctoral studies face National Science Foundation and National Institutes of Health fellowship applications demanding research experience dating to freshman year. Career preparation has superseded exploration across campus. Yet, can our future employers and schools be fully to blame? I have a second suspect for the murder of exploration: us. When younger Yalies seek career advice over dining hall meals, I first ask if they have found their guiding purpose. The conversation usually plays out like this: “What is your north star?” “Right now, I’m locked in on securing that McKinsey internship.” “Yes, but what’s your end goal? Your own guiding mission?” “...I’m not sure yet. Maybe law school?” The ambition that carried us to New Haven is easily hijacked by arbitrary measures of success established by the student community. Within weeks of arriving, first-years are exposed to what “success” looks like in each realm of campus: “You should apply to YSIG if you’re interested in finance. They have a ton of alumni at Goldman Sachs.” “If you want a shot at a T14 school, you should have a 4.0 and a law journal publication.” Rather than charting their own paths, Yalies often co-opt a communal definition of success and work tirelessly to achieve it. First-semester me — the kid that raved about particle physics and economic policy in his Yale application — sleepwalked my way up the ranks of a consulting club without ever asking if that was what I had really wanted. So, who killed college exploration? Both suspects are culpable. Employers and schools impose aggressive recruitment timelines and stringent requirements, and ambitious students eagerly rise to the challenge. This hyper-competitive applicant pool encourages firms to raise their standards again, fueling an arms race. Consequently, higher education produces a generation of over-prepared, under-explored graduates. Students determined to succeed within a single field never discover what they truly want, arriving at 22 in a job they chose at 19 for reasons that were never really their own. Is there a way to break the cycle? Employers and graduate schools should ask which of their requirements still measure genuine ability, and reckon with what they lose by screening out candidates who spent college actually exploring. Absent institutional change, I find it naive to tell Yalies they should simply ignore post-graduate pressures. Instead, I recommend “strategic exploration:” invest seriously in at least two fields you actually care about, fulfill your distributional requirements early to explore the whole range of classes Yale has to offer, and ask whether your ambitions are genuinely yours or inherited from the students around you. I broke out of autopilot at the end of junior year. I rediscovered my love for economics, reneging on a full-time consulting offer to pursue academic research. The world today rewards the straightest path, yet there is a quiet tragedy in arriving at a destination you never chose for yourself. The kid you described in your Yale application is still in there — go find them. RYAN WANG is a senior in Jonathan Edwards College studying Economics and Mathematics. After Yale, Ryan will be working at the MIT Economics Department as a research fellow. Contact him at ryan.wang@yale.edu .

title: What is Yale for?
author: Matias Guevara Ruales
text: Why does one come to Yale? One reasonable answer is money. Just four years out of college, Yale alums earn almost double what the typical college graduate does, and the median lifetime return of coming here was recently estimated at 3.4 million dollars. Still, one could be forgiven for thinking that a Yale education ought to offer more than the material benefits that might accrue from the degree. You may have heard Dean Lewis make some such distinction during a public appearance at some point, and I echo it here because my concern is precisely one of Yale’s main selling points to prospective students: the idea that when you decide to enroll here, you’re signing up to get a “liberal education.” The phrase is familiar to all of us at American higher-learning institutions, but what has it come to mean, amid the onerous demands of our results-oriented culture? Can we still recognize in it the suggestion that there are at least two parts to the value of a college education that matter, only one of which produces “results” in the traditional sense? The first of those is plainly the accreditation of technical skills embodied by the diploma, which paves the way for the superior earnings elite schools are famous for. The second part, often called the liberal arts, is harder to discern. It has to do with the development of students as people — their personal flourishing, we might have said once, though that word now carries a sentimental ring that may itself be a symptom of the diminishing status of the liberal arts in our culture. But the liberal arts have a use, too. They are, in the words of the recent report by the Committee on Trust in Higher Education, “the foundational wisdom that will serve [students] throughout their lives.” No virtue has ever advertised a payoff in strict cost-benefit terms, yet “wisdom” does properly characterize the part of our education that makes us critical thinkers, generous friends and conscientious citizens. These attributes are crucial to any individual life, and to any free society, but in recent years we have begun to surrender them. Ultra-competitive job markets have meant that a growing number of students are allocating their time so as to maximize early-career outcomes at the expense of pursuits once considered worthwhile, like taking challenging courses outside of their majors or joining non-professional clubs. Even within the areas directly related to their post-graduate goals, students must often turn to artificial intelligence, dig up class materials from previous semesters or employ other workarounds that free up time for professional development but defeat the most basic point of academic work: to learn. Underlying this trend is the belief that wasting time on pursuits not easily translatable into attractive resume entries can be fatal for long-term success. But recent research says otherwise. A literature review about the highest levels of human achievement, published last year, analyzed data from top performers across different fields — from Nobel laureates to Olympic champions to the the world’s best chess players — and found that what distinguished them from their sub-elite peers was that the former showed a more gradual progression toward excellence and a larger amount of multidisciplinary practice early on. That is, they took the time to explore many different interests before they specialized and became best-in-class. The world is not organized to facilitate this kind of exploration. Universities emphasize early displays of merit over lifelong achievement, while corporations use their deep pockets to lure young graduates into highly specialized jobs. And on a cultural level, too, we tend to think of doing things simply because we’re passionate about them as somehow amateur in the pejorative sense. But doing things for their own sake should not demote our pursuits to the status of hobbies. On the contrary, when we treat a hobby with enough seriousness we elevate it to the category of a passion, and the power to do this lies, in no small part, with each of us. What would happen if we approached our education as an experience in itself valuable? For one, our efforts might begin to seem worthwhile even without anybody’s stamp of approval, and we, in turn, might become more resistant to failure. When we looked in the mirror and saw that we had played our cards wrong, squandered our time, disappointed the expectations of parents and friends, we would not give up, because our work would not be just a means, but an end in itself. Why does one come to Yale? Yes, to be successful. Yes, to have a good income. But we also come here to be students, become better persons, and find the thing, or things, worthy of our lives’ work. We come here, in short, to flourish. The role of administrators in protecting that sacred soil of our personal flourishing, the university, is paramount. Yale could do more to fight the utilitarian trends that endanger it presently. But we, too, have a part to play in reclaiming the privilege of a truly liberal education — a phrase whose roots lie in the idea that to be educated is to become more free. What could be more profitable than that? MATIAS GUEVARA RUALES is a senior in Jonathan Edwards College studying Humanities and Economics. He can be reached at matias.guevararuales@yale.edu .

title: On being wrong about myself
author: Zachary Jacobus
text: My older cousin mentioned to me in high school that he believed deciding where to attend college is a problem of insufficient information. The ability to know what you are looking for in a college, and to choose accordingly, is something that you only learn in college. At the time, this did not make much sense to me. College was not going to teach me what mattered in a college. Rather, knowing my interests was how I was meant to decide which college to choose. Chemistry caught my attention in high school. I was good at it and thought it was interesting, so this is what I planned to study in college. I spent countless hours trying to gauge which universities would be “best” for chemistry. The impossibility of this task reveals one way my cousin was correct. With my high school chemistry education, I could not make an intelligent decision on what subfields or researchers would interest me and therefore could not choose a college accordingly. I have learned that the ability to make such a decision comes through attending college: sitting in classes, talking to professors about their research and realizing I did not like certain types of lab work. But I believe his observation about the limits and evolving nature of self-understanding is true more broadly. Looking back, I was remarkably unaware of what could, and would, come to interest me. My point here is not a critique of my formerly closeminded self. I had some inkling, nudged on by my parents and a guidance counselor, that I could discover new interests. This inkling was a reason I chose Yale. But I needed experiences to draw these interests out of me. I arrived in New Haven wanting to explore broadly, and I assumed I would find something else if chemistry did not work out, but I had no idea what that something else would be. During my first semester, I joined the brief-writing team of Yale Undergraduate Moot Court, just for fun and because two suitemates were trying it. I loved it. I loved to read judges break down a situation that involved real people into chunks that the law could cover. I also loved the idea that I could write my own analysis, drawing from one Supreme Court justice here but contesting another there. I had always loved to read, write and argue, so it should not have been surprising that I would become interested in the law. But I could only find this part of myself in a new environment. Coming into college, I imagined myself as someone lacking visual artistic talent. Some people had it, and others, like me, didn’t. This didn’t seem like a problem, and I had no desire to learn an artistic craft. But after sophomore year I found myself increasingly interested in how to take a nice photograph. I decided to try a digital photography class junior spring. I am graduating having taken three photo classes, one each semester since the digital class. I am a film photography enthusiast and have learned to use a camera that “looks like it’s from the Three Stooges,” in the words of a passerby who watched me set up for a photo. I hope to take photos as long as I live and build my own darkroom one day. In hindsight, maybe this interest should not have been so unexpected either, given that several people in my family are visual artists. I now feel a connection to this legacy and think of it as something inside me. But I had never imagined this interest in photography until it emerged under experimentation and practice. Returning to my cousin’s point, it is not merely that my interests have changed in college. Instead, I feel that I have discovered parts of myself that existed but were waiting to be found, parts I could only find through the time and exploration that college provided. Law and photography are prime examples, but others include running, which I previously hated, and cinema. College is well-known as a potent time for self-discovery, but I aim to continue this process of growth and transformation through my life. Drawing from Yale and the tradition of the liberal arts education, I hope to always explore new things, be open-minded and remain open to wisdom from others, accepting the limits of my own self-knowledge. ZACHARY JACOBUS is a senior in Davenport College studying chemistry. He can be reached at zachary.jacobus@yale.edu .

title: So long, and thank you for reading
author: Milan Singh
text: When I came to Yale, I had no idea what I would do after graduation. I thought I might go into management consulting. Four years later, I’m taking a fellowship as a journalist; we’ll see how long that lasts. In a way, this is a bit of a full-circle moment for me: the first extracurricular I joined in college was the Opinion section of the Yale Daily News. I suppose now, as a graduating senior, is my time to dispense a little bit of advice to the class of 2030. I’ll try to keep things brief. Take your coursework seriously. There is an instrumental reason to do so: A high GPA is often a necessary but not sufficient condition for future employment or educational opportunities. But no less importantly, there is an intrinsic reason. College is one of the few times you will have in your life to focus solely on absorbing knowledge and learning things, particularly difficult things. Self-driven study and independent reading are valuable, but nothing will motivate you to drill a fact, equation or theory into your head like studying for an exam. You’re surrounded by top-notch teachers and brilliant classmates; you ought to try to soak up as much as you can from them. Really engage intellectually with your peers. Not just in the seminar or in the dining hall, but in public — in reading groups, or debating societies, or in one of the many campus publications, though preferably in the pages of the News. When I’ve asked classmates whether they’d be interested in writing an op-ed, the most common response I get is some version of “I want to, but I’m worried about potential backlash.” I think this particular fear is overblown, and it deprives the university of fruitful conversations. Take, for example, the last piece I wrote for the News. In response to a classmate’s article, I contended that the relative lack of conservative students and faculty at elite universities could, at least in part, be chalked up to conservatives having slightly lower IQs than liberals on average. That piece was probably my most provocative column for the News, and it was intentionally somewhat snarky. I received some emails from readers who did not agree with my argument, and a couple of my right-leaning classmates wrote op-eds responding to me. But that was pretty much it. I wrote down an opinion. Some people disagreed with it. I enjoyed reading their responses, and after a few weeks, everybody moved on. One thing I learned when I edited this section was that Opinion pieces often receive more views than anything else that the News publishes. Publishing an op-ed lets you, in whatever small way, influence discussions over how this University is run. The recent report of the Committee on Trust in Higher Education includes citations to eleven articles published in the Opinion section of the News, including one of my own. After exams finished, I rewatched Sir David Attenborough’s masterpiece documentary series “The Life of Birds” in honor of his 100th birthday. “Now, we have taken over the Earth, the sea and the sky. But with skill and care and knowledge, we can ensure that there is still a place on Earth for birds, in all their beauty and variety. If we want to — and surely, we should,” Sir David says at the end of the final episode. I first watched the series when I was in kindergarten. It has probably influenced my life more than any other piece of literature or film. I wrote my college essay on the question of what makes a species “invasive” or not. And while these days I mostly write about politics, my interest in the subject came about as a result of reading National Audubon Society emails comparing Mitt Romney and Barack Obama’s environmental platforms in the lead-up to the third-grade straw poll. There is a moral undertone in Attenborough’s closing monologue: With great power comes great responsibility. As Yalies, we have a similar obligation. Fairly or not, attending this small college endows us with outsized economic, political and cultural clout. Contrary to the University’s newly pared-down mission statement, I believe that “improving the world” is and should be a part of our core mission. We are extraordinarily lucky people. On average, Yale graduates tend to hail from wealthy families . Most of us have the enormous privilege of having grown up in the richest nation in the history of our species. We get to learn from the best scholars in the world for four years. And then we enter the labor market, and we face a choice. Take it from an economics major: Capital allocation is an important and socially valuable undertaking. It is also a very lucrative line of work. But the marginal Yale graduate who goes to work in investment banking or management consulting or at a pod shop isn’t making capital allocation that much more efficient. Our educations and upbringings have given us the skills and the knowledge to improve our country and our world, provided that we care enough to want to. And surely, we should. I will close out this last column with some thank yous. Thank you to James Prud’homme ’26 and Luca Girodon ’26, my two closest friends from my graduating class. Thank you to Josh Kalla ’14 GRD ’14 and Akhil Amar ’80 LAW ’84 for being such excellent teachers. Finally, thank you for reading. I hope I had something interesting to say. MILAN SINGH is a senior in Pierson College studying Economics and a former Opinion editor for the News. Contact him at milan.singh@yale.edu .

title: Getting it right
author: Tristan Hernandez
text: The first day I was the editor in chief of the Yale Daily News, I felt completely unprepared. All I knew was my own experience from my sophomore year: interviews with Dean Lewis, copy editing shifts every Tuesday, reports on sorority disaffiliation and Coke-Pepsi wars in the dining hall. Suddenly, I was sitting in the Berkeley College common room with the managing editors while protesters set up an encampment on Cross Campus. My new reality was answering calls and fact-checking updates, all while I still had to learn how to simply upload onto our website. I was out of my league, but people depended on me to do my job. Even though I was not sure if I was making the right calls, decisions had to be made, and the News still had to publish. In time, I learned what the job would require of me. I would give up my junior year to dedicate myself to the paper, writing class assignments ten minutes before the deadline and completing problem sets at 4:00 a.m. after the News’ print production finished. I knew more about McInnis’ administration and Yale’s racial demographic data than I did about my own classes. It was the year of a presidential election, federal attacks on university funding and international students, new policies on institutional neutrality and the aftermath of mass student protests. It was the closest I ever got to understanding this place. But while I experienced Yale from 202 York St., life went on without me. In retirement from the News this year, I nod along as my Pierson College friends recount stories from junior year and I pretend to understand. I lived off campus with a nocturnal schedule while they threw suite parties I could never make it to; my job asked me to be present for everyone else’s experience while I was absent from my own. I also reflect on the News itself in my tenure’s post-mortem — whether I did enough or if I made the correct choices. I find myself wondering what it meant to be a Latino editor in chief at a paper where you could count the non-white editors on one hand, what it meant to be a queer person given massive responsibility to shape narratives around our community. I don’t know if I did it right, and I doubt I ever will. As I reintegrated into campus life, the News kept going. I was happy to retire, leaving the work to a set of incoming editors who had new ideas and energy. I came into my senior year hoping to cram every Yale experience into the 40 weeks I had left in New Haven. I attended Feb Club every night, went back to my favorite carrel in the stacks, and spent more time in Pierson’s Lower Court than my own suite. For the first time, I felt like I was experiencing Yale, the one advertised to me in admissions brochures, even as I missed the newspaper work that had defined my first three years. Leaving Yale now, I don’t know what comes next. For four years, I went through college with a set of benchmarks I needed to meet — the News, FroCo, society. I knew what classes I wanted to take since I got admitted. But the best parts of Yale were the unplanned moments: New York trips made two hours before we left, impromptu hour-long conversations in the newsroom, showing up in the Pierson dining hall and finding someone to eat with. At the end of this year, I was talking to some of the first years I counseled as a FroCo. They’re younger and use slang I have never heard of and freak out when there’s a mouse in Vanderbilt Hall. The news I covered as a reporter happened when they were in high school. They make changes to their Yale plans constantly. On some level, I feel unqualified to give them advice because I had all these plans yet never knew what was the right decision. Now that I’m done, I find that I no longer worry about the right choice. My path was made, and maybe that’s enough. Yale is 325 years old, and we’re only here for four of them. People will fill the gaps when we leave, and this place will keep going when we’re gone. Someone will be sitting in the News building covering what comes next. They will feel unprepared. They won’t know if they’re getting it right either. The News will still have to publish. I spent four years planning Yale. When I leave, I’ll only remember the parts I didn’t. TRISTAN HERNANDEZ was the 147th editor in chief and president of the News. He is a graduating senior in Pierson College studying political science. He can be reached at tristan.hernandez@yale.edu .

title: Home address: 68 High Street
author: Yurii Stasiuk
text: I received my Yale acceptance letter weeks after Russia started bombing Ukraine. I had just turned 19 when the war started — one year past the threshold that determined, at the time, which Ukrainian men could leave the country during wartime. I was already studying abroad at the time. And so, for four years, I kept choosing not to go home — with a brief exception this year, when a change in law temporarily allowed me to visit Ukraine twice without being unable to leave — so I could continue attending Yale, so I could later use this privilege to help my country. So I told myself. For four years, Yale has been my literal home, the only constant I have had. I wrote 68 High St. on every form asking for my home address. Every winter break, when Yale closed its dorms, I found another friend to crash with, usually in a basement room. Since I couldn’t go home, every summer I got to live in another country — Poland, Georgia, Belgium and yes, one New York summer — and always worried I’d hit a visa-free stay limit, or that a temporary rental would end before I found another one. I always looked forward to returning home to New Haven. Yale gave me a bed to cry on at 5 a.m. when I learned someone I knew had been killed or when my city appeared once more in war headlines. Yale kept my water and electricity running when another Russian air strike left my family without them. Sometimes I had to pretend to be fine; it felt obscene to grieve on Yale’s campus while people at home were actually dying. I was not the victim, I told myself, and wouldn’t behave like one, at least not in public. Over time, my expressions of joy became increasingly sincere. For better or worse, Yale and its people had also provided me the luxury to forget about war, if only for brief moments. I started writing in a language I barely knew just a few years ago, and soon found reporting on New Haven’s budget and student protests more meaningful than writing papers on Said or Spinoza. 202 York St., the building of the very paper you are reading, became my second home. Junior year, I worked Goldman Sachs hours for free, editing stories through the year when Yale and the United States both got new presidents. I have met wonderful people who challenged and supported me through war news and 20-page papers, partied and pulled all-nighters with me. I got high on High Street; stayed past midnight to talk about religion, ice cream flavors, Iran, love; grabbed coffee at Koffee; swore a blood oath to my secret society. Not all late nights were spent crying. My four years in the safety of New Haven — yes, war at home really puts its purported danger into perspective — had helped me become who I needed to be. I never stopped questioning whether I should be here at all, staring at the leave-of-absence form every semester. But I stayed, and whatever I owe to that choice I intend to pay back. Soon, I will no longer be able to write 68 High St. as my address on official paperwork. My country is still at war. I don’t yet know what my next permanent address will be or how much constancy my future holds. I choose to believe that Yale will still remain a constant to a degree — I will call my college friends, maybe watch the Bulldogs crush The Game, or once again go to March Madness. Every once in a while, I will open the News to read about another student service getting cut. And I will cherish the four years when my Jonathan Edwards dorm felt like home, even as my rooms kept changing. YURII STASIUK is a senior in Jonathan Edwards College and a former print managing editor of the News. He can be reached at yurii.v.stasiuk@gmail.com .

title: The view from Yale isn’t the view from America
author: Manu Anpalagan
text: When I arrived at Yale, something was missing. There was no Republican group at one of the most prestigious and influential universities in the country. At an institution that trains future leaders of government, business and culture, there was no organized home for students who believed in the principles of the America First movement. So I started one . Serving as the founding president of Yale College Republicans for the last two-and-a-half years has been one of the most meaningful experiences of my time here. But our group’s very existence raises an uncomfortable question: How did Yale go so long without a Republican organization in the first place? The answer points to a deeper concern. Too many students and faculty at Yale live in a bubble — one that is intellectually insulated and socially detached from the realities facing the 340 million Americans living outside of our campus boundaries. Conversations here often assume that the dominant ideological viewpoints at Yale are representative of the country at large. They are not. And when our nation’s future leaders forget that, the consequences are real. For decades, elite institutions like Yale helped produce the architects of policies that devastated American communities. The bipartisan consensus around free trade hollowed out American manufacturing and shipped jobs overseas. The architects of the Iraq and Afghanistan wars, many of them attendees of elite universities, committed our nation to two decades of costly conflict with nothing to show for it. These decisions were shaped by a worldview that prioritized abstract global theories over the lived realities of working Americans. When I looked around Yale, I saw no home for a different kind of conservatism — one that put the American people first. Instead, what existed were organizations that echoed the same outdated, failed neoconservative ideas that had already led the country astray. They championed globalism, catered to elite interests and aligned themselves with Wall Street over Main Street. That is why I founded Yale College Republicans. I wanted to build a space for students who believe that conservatism should serve the American people — not multinational corporations, not foreign policy abstractions and not elite consensus. A space for what is known as America First conservatism. The future of the Republican Party will not be defined by figures like Nikki Haley or Mitch McConnell, who represent an out-of-touch establishment. It will be shaped by visionary leaders like Vice President JD Vance LAW ’13 and Senator Josh Hawley LAW ’06, who are willing to challenge both parties’ orthodoxies and fight for American workers , families and communities . At Yale, this divide is especially clear. There exists not just liberal elitism , but also a conservative elitism — an unholy union of a bipartisan establishment that advances policies disconnected from the needs of ordinary Americans. We must reject that. As students at Yale, we have a responsibility that goes beyond our own success. We are being prepared to lead institutions that will shape the future of this country. That means we must think beyond the narrow confines of campus life and consider the well being of all Americans, not just those who share our classrooms or our zip codes . Yale College Republicans, at its core, is an attempt to bridge that gap. It is a community rooted in the belief that conservatism should reflect the views, concerns and aspirations of everyday Americans. Lastly, to my fellow students, regardless of political affiliation: Step outside the Yale bubble. Engage with the country you are preparing to lead. Listen to people whose lives look nothing like yours. Because the strength of our nation depends on our ability to see, and serve, all of it. MANU ANPALAGAN served as the founding president of the Yale College Republicans. He is a senior in Branford College studying Economics and Political Science. He can be reached at manuneethy.anpalagan@yale.edu .

title: On conversation
author: Pericles Lewis
text: Graduating seniors: four years ago — before move-in, before shopping period, before the first blue book or lab report — I welcomed many of you during Bulldog Days and framed this place as more than somewhere you would attend. I asked you to think of Yale as a community you would have to practice: a community based on conversation. I invited you to learn with humility, to assume good intentions and to value complexity and nuance over self-assurance and stereotype. I opened those remarks with a question from Confucius: “Is it not a pleasure to learn...? Is it not a joy to have friends coming from afar?” Four years later, you can answer for yourselves. I hope that learning has been a pleasure — sometimes thrilling, sometimes difficult, maybe sometimes both — and that friendship has arrived from farther away than geography can measure. As you leave Yale College, I hope you take with you not only what you studied but also how you learned: in dialogue, across difference, with humility. I also hope that you do the same in the conversations ahead, wherever you land next. Commencement speeches usually urge you to go out and change the world. Maybe you will. But here is a more tangible and, in many ways, more urgent charge: to use conversation as a practice, and as a public good. The habits I named four years ago — openness to challenge, resistance to stereotype, generosity in interpretation — are not just campus niceties; they are civic equipment. In workplaces, communities, families and public life, you will enter conversations already underway, many of them fragile. The question is whether your contribution will narrow or widen them, whether you will flatten someone into a category or make room for the full complexity of their story. That charge matters because it is the antidote to what Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie calls “the danger of a single story.” In those Bulldog Days remarks, I shared her reminder that stereotypes are not always false; they are usually just incomplete. And if that is true of places and people, it is also true of a graduating class. The class of 2026 will be remembered, inevitably, through a few stories we repeat: the breakthroughs we celebrate, the conflicts we debate, the moments that made news. But you also carry thousands of narratives that rarely make headlines: the late shifts at a campus job followed by rehearsal, the times you cared for a friend in crisis, the decision to change majors or plans, the discipline of showing up week after week for people who depended on you. I also borrowed an image from Tennyson’s “Ulysses,” where “all experience is an arch wherethro’ / Gleams that untravell’d world” — an image for how education expands the horizon. Many of you have felt that expansion in obvious ways: new fields of study, research methods, languages, archives, data sets, artistic forms. But maybe you have also felt it in quieter, more human ways: the first time you ate with someone whose life story upended your assumptions, the first time you realized you had been casually repeating a stereotype, the first time you learned to ask a better question rather than deliver a faster answer. The horizon does not expand because Yale is magical; it expands because you do the work of moving toward the unfamiliar. You have also lived through four years of polarized, often bleak public dialogue. You have watched debates harden into slogans, and you have seen how quickly misunderstanding can become moral certainty. Yale is not immune to dogma — no university is — but I still believe we can aspire to a higher kind of conversation: one where arguments are tested rather than traded, where listening is not a concession but a skill and where the goal is not to win but to learn. That work is hard. It asks for patience when impatience feels righteous, for generosity when cynicism feels safer and for the courage to say, sometimes publicly and sometimes only to yourself, “I might be wrong.” So as you prepare for graduation, I come back to that original invitation — not to grade yourselves against it, but to use it as a bookend. Commencement tempts us to summarize four years into a single story: the headline accomplishment, the defining crisis, the proudest milestone, the hardest setback. But if Yale has taught you anything, I hope it is that your lives — and this class — are bigger than their easiest summaries. As you leave, keep practicing conversation, especially when it is hard. Assume good intentions. Value complexity and nuance over stereotype and certainty. Keep moving your horizons outward. And help one another, wherever you go, to build communities worthy of the people in them. Congratulations, class of 2026. PERICLES LEWIS is the Dean of Yale College. He is the Douglas Tracy Smith Professor of Comparative Literature and a professor of English, and formerly served as vice president for global strategy and vice provost for academic initiatives. Contact him at pericles.lewis@yale.edu .

title: Green light
author: Truth Templeton
text: Being at Yale has often produced in me a feeling difficult to describe, arising less from any one thing than from an accumulation of barely perceptible encounters. There are visible features of the institution — inherited traditions, improbable wealth, alumni who drift from seminar rooms into warmongering — but these have always seemed easier to account for than the opaque systems accompanying one through the day. One encounters them only in fragments: the green light beside a doorway, the soft click after an ID swipe, the verification of a NetID login, the absentminded gesture of holding one’s card above the dining hall reader while exchanging an “it’s nice to see you” with the wonderful people behind it. The Yale ID card gradually began to feel less like a mundane object than evidence of a structure whose scale I could sense more easily than understand. The first time I took my Yale ID photo, it was a banal event. My hair possessed a dry frizziness, my eyes carried an exhausted heaviness I perhaps mistook for some bureaucratic chic and I wore a gray V-neck sweater over a white T-shirt too large for me and sweater, the fabric gathered in folds at the neck like a vaguely Elizabethan ruff. My mom had reminded me repeatedly to take the photograph, and so I carried out the task as quickly and disgruntledly and ineffectively as possible beneath the kitchen light of our apartment, scarcely looking at myself before submitting the image through a Yale ID Center website that now returns only the message “redirect_error.” I still dislike the photograph. But what troubles me about it cannot be reduced entirely to vanity. It is the sense that this forgettable image no longer belongs entirely to me. Movement through Yale once depended upon looser, analog infrastructure. Most students carried three keys at all times: one for their residential college gate, one for the entryway and one for their suite, and even then often found themselves waiting outside other colleges until sympathetic students let them in. The transition to electronic ID systems in 1994 reorganized this relationship. Swipe cards were introduced as a safer, streamlined alternative to keys, particularly amid growing anxieties surrounding security and Yale’s relationship to New Haven. Yet over time, the Yale ID evolved far beyond a mechanism for unlocking doors. What once functioned primarily as a tool of physical access became an infrastructural node linking dormitory entry, dining halls, payroll systems, course registration, Wi-Fi authentication, library privileges, email accounts and countless other administrative systems. Yale’s identity architecture now operates through centralized databases, multifactor authentication systems, cloud storage environments and commercial identity-management platforms capable of coordinating enormous populations across physical and digital space. What appears as convenience conceals a sprawling infrastructure increasingly responsible for organizing institutional movement, access and belonging. Earlier this spring, I visited the Yale ID Center, a small office staffed, at least that afternoon, by no more than two or three employees. I asked about card production and vendor relationships. Before I had finished speaking, the staff member shook his head. Printing and issuing, he explained, happened “in-house,” but vendor information could not be disclosed. The restriction was framed primarily in terms of security, particularly following recent incidents involving counterfeit IDs. Yet turn over a Yale ID card, or look closely at any of the readers mounted beside entrances, and one encounters three small letters repeated across the system: HID. HID Global is one of the world’s largest manufacturers of access-control technologies, used across universities, hospitals, airports, corporations and government facilities. But the company’s origins lie within a far older communications and defense lineage. HID emerged in the mid-1990s out of a Hughes Aircraft spin-off alongside Palomar Products, whose engineers developed secure airborne communications and avionics systems used in military aircraft and surveillance platforms. The point here is not that Yale students are secretly participating in some concealed military apparatus, nor even that these overlaps are unusual. Much of modern technological life operates through these kinds of dual-use infrastructures, where systems developed for defense, surveillance, logistics or security gradually become absorbed into the ordinary organization of civilian life. What interests me is how thoroughly these histories disappear once the technologies become mundane. I still dislike the photograph on my Yale ID — tired expression, badly layered sweater and all — but perhaps part of what unsettles me is the knowledge that this image, once private and forgettable, now circulates through systems I scarcely understood. The difficulty is not simply that these infrastructures are large or technically complex, but that participation in them so rarely registers as participation at all. And maybe that is the feeling I have been trying to describe, this sense that we are surrounded by vast arrangements we encounter only in flashes and fragments, little clicks and green lights and passwords and scans, while the true scale of what we participate in remains mostly hidden from us. Which means that questions about responsibility, complicity, dual-use technologies, about what is good and what is dangerous, cannot even really begin until we learn to see where we already are and have been. TRUTH TEMPLETON is a senior in Benjamin Franklin College studying Ethics, Politics, & Economics. He can be reached at truth.templeton@yale.edu .

title: Times are changing, so we need each other
author: Estelle Balsirow
text: In 2022, I walked into Woolsey Hall for the first time. Masks lined the rows of the stage. Both my University and my country had a different president in those days. The hot, stuffy air doused my excitement in a layer of drowsiness. I didn’t know that linguistics, the major I’m graduating with, existed. It seemed that every single club and class at Yale was important, that every interaction I made could take me on a course for prestige, that if I made one wrong decision I could jeopardize generations of my family for years to come. I wondered if I could survive. Maybe I was hyperbolizing. Actually, I was definitely hyperbolizing. My sister says I have a knack for it. First-year Estelle didn’t know that ChatGPT would be released in November of that year. She didn’t know she would have to compete with robots to find a job, nor that there would be sweeping policy changes on mission statements and endowments reshaping campus and the United States. In December 2021, I cried in front of a computer as I opened my Yale decision. I couldn’t bring myself to press the view button. I was terrified that this decision would define my next four years. My sister, a state away, soothed me as I first laid eyes on my admissions letter. During the spring semester of junior year, I was a mess. I was dealing with the gradual decline of my grandmother, and every time I received a new rejection, the image of my grandmother, alone in her living room, whirred into focus in my head. I was overworking myself, trying to prove to a spectre in my mind that I was some exceptional Yalie that could do everything and anything. Instead, I was falling spectacularly behind. After my grandmother’s funeral service, my family held a memorial, shared pictures and memories, and laughed and cried at the times we couldn’t return to. In May 2026, during dead week, I ate ice cream with a friend and reminisced about our time together at Yale. We don’t have the cushion of the university to fall back on; we have to find housing, new friends, new people, new everything, and it feels like we’re learning how to be adults all over again. It seems that the worrying continues, like an annoying itch on the palm of your hand or a clump in your throat that can’t escape your esophagus. It makes you wonder if you can survive. In 1943, thousands of Kalmyks were deported to Siberia in the dead of winter. My family had around a hundred men fight in the Battle of Stalingrad, only for less than a fourth to come back, wounded mentally and physically. My maternal grandfather had to bury his mother in the snow and slept with horses in a flea-ridden barn for warmth, taken in by a man I don’t know the name of, who saved my grandfather’s life that winter. We learned about the proto-Indo-European language this year in my historical linguistics class. Thousands of years of language, of billions of humans who survived and thrived and lived to pass down their thoughts and stories to their next generation, created the languages we speak today. The way they did that? Through speaking with one another; through passing down stories with other humans. My family, the proto-Indo-Europeans, Americans from centuries ago: it’s not useful to say that they had it tougher than us, that they experienced actual hardship, or whatever complaints you hear your distant relative yell about to no one in particular. But maybe it gives a little comfort to say they had people in their lives to help them fight. That every feeling of emptiness, pain, and despair cannot erase the fact that they survived, huddled together with their friends, their allies through it all. It gives me comfort and a bit of drive to be that person in someone else’s story, to give a helping hand, a small hope that we can survive for one more day and keep going. It isn’t over. Times are new and unprecedented and scary. But we can reach out to others and put trust in our fellow humans to bring us comfort in uncertain times. Eat some ice cream with your friends. Check in with someone you haven’t talked to in a minute. Times are changing. We will need each other. ESTELLE BALSIROW is a senior in Jonathan Edwards College studying Linguistics with a certificate in Russian. Contact her at estelle.balsirow@yale.edu

title: Revisiting the race issue in the Engineering department
author: Deja Dunlap
text: Two years ago, I wrote an article about the race issue in the School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, or SEAS. Much has happened since then: the first freshman class at Yale following the end of affirmative action, Yale’s creation of an institutional voice policy and the Trump administration’s attacks against DEI, to name a few. On the precipice of my graduation, I wanted to revisit the issue that I spent so much of my Yale tenure considering. I want to start this article with a few facts that I recently learned. In 2015, former Yale President Peter Salovey GRD ’83 ’84 ’86 committed $50 million to increasing faculty diversity across all of Yale. In 2020, the commitment was upped by an additional $85 million and the launch of the five-year “Belonging at Yale” initiative. Now that we are one year out from this effort, I think it’s important to ask: Did Yale increase diversity in its faculty population? On the surface, the answer is yes. The publicly available database Yale shares about its faculty demographics shows, from 2013 to 2025, the number of Black, Hispanic, Indigenous and Pacific Islander tenured or tenured-track professors nearly tripled across departments. But how much of this increase was concentrated in the SEAS department? Four. Not 4 percent, four people. SEAS went from five underrepresented minority — or URM — faculty members in 2013 to nine in 2024. While the overall faculty in the SEAS department increased 44 percent, only four of them were from URM backgrounds. With this foregrounding, I still hold many of the opinions I expressed in my original article. The SEAS department has continued to not seriously consider the racial disparities in the same manner that they have other disparities like gender. Engineering continues to lack representational diversity in the workforce, a fact that has been exacerbated by the Trump administration’s attacks on DEI. And, for the rest of my time at Yale, I continued to be one of the only Black students in my math and engineering courses. Where I differ is in my relationship to this disparity. More specifically, I no longer feel an inclination to prove my worth. Perhaps this is a natural symptom of maturing, but I’ve come to believe that my talents speak for themselves. What I’ve come to realize is that, no matter if I were to graduate with a perfect GPA and the most prestigious post-graduate job, it wouldn’t be enough to change the culture. Whether my professors remember me by my name or as “the Black girl in the class” — personally offensive as it is — it won’t fix the systemic injustices minorities face in the field. One person’s exceptionalism doesn’t change a culture built on centuries of systematic exclusion and erasure. What, however, does change it is to address that such a culture even exists. While SEAS and Yale more broadly have made significant progress in terms of diversity, it is not enough. In an environment where all things DEI are heavily scrutinized, it may seem reckless to openly address something like this. But, as the recently published report from the Committee on Trust in Higher Education states, an emphasis on intellectual diversity is critical to nurturing an academically productive environment. While the report focused on political diversity, I would argue for the extension to all axes, including race. Besides the multiple documented benefits of diversity, it’s just the right thing to do. As Yale is one of the most televised universities in the world, what Yale chooses to do in this critical moment in history could have widespread effects on Black engineers everywhere. I’ll end the article urging for the same things I did before. Yale should publish racial student demographic information by major. SEAS should invest more in SEAS-affiliated affinity-oriented student organizations. SEAS should build and expand partnerships with Historically Black Colleges and Universities and Hispanic Serving Institutions to build diverse graduate student and faculty populations. And SEAS should invest in programs that improve minorities’ retention. By proactively addressing the race issue, hopefully Black engineering students in the class of 2036 will look back at this article in disbelief at the covert and overt instances of racism described, shocked that this is what SEAS was like in 2026. But if we do nothing, I promise future students will read this article and feel as though they could have written it themselves, how eerily similar their own experiences will have been. It’s time Yale addresses the race issue in the engineering department. DEJA DUNLAP is a senior Applied Mathematics major in Pauli Murray College and served in the National Society of Black Engineers, or NSBE, at Yale for two years and the Regional Executive Board of NSBE for one year. Contact her at deja@berkeley.edu .

title: Candy Crush hopes
author: William Ning
text: On the first Tuesday of this semester, I found myself having a drunken conversation in GHeav with a first year who mentioned a fear of settling down and committing to a single path. They had arrived at Yale with hopes for who they might become. But how could they decide on a specific future if they might change their minds? I sympathized with the concern: I asked myself the same question when I walked into Durfee Hall. I have never been good at deciding what I should do: Walking through one door has always felt like closing ten others. Yale was handing me a blank canvas, a sea of possibility; with the world at my fingertips, I wasn’t sure how I could do all I hoped to do. But sitting there in GHeav at 2 a.m., with friendships I once could never have imagined having, I realized I could not have guessed who would become my closest friends, or which pursuits would feel most meaningful to me. I had no clue what Yale would bring me. I didn’t know I would walk in circles around Old Campus for three hours playing Candy Crush, killing time with a friend while watching another friend go through a breakup. I had no idea I would walk to East Rock with a classmate just to watch the sunrise after five straight all-nighters finishing a random project. But now, looking back, there was no other way the past four years could have unfolded. In hindsight, I see several clean strands braiding my life together in a way that feels almost inevitable. Every spontaneous side quest has woven itself into what now feels like a coherent mosaic of these bright college years. And now we are at the end of it all. Having painted our Yale canvas, what once felt like pure possibility might begin to feel like lost opportunity: I will never be able to rush an a capella group or work at the Yale Farm or play intramural broomball again. It is so easy to let our memories shape the anxieties of the future — to believe that because something mattered deeply here, it cannot be replaced elsewhere. To look at the many directions our lives are now pulling us in and conclude that we are leaving something irreplaceable behind. What I should have told the first-year student in GHeav is that I had not left their question behind. They were standing at the beginning of their four years, afraid of what choosing would cost them; I am sitting at the end of four years of choosing, afraid now of who I have become. What we are both afraid of isn’t the possibility of making the wrong choice, but the realization that our choices slowly and steadily accumulate into a life. The discomfort of graduation, I think, is the unfamiliar feeling of having become someone specific. The canvas that once felt blank is now marked by inside jokes, routines, relationships and accidents of timing that have hardened into a self. And the strangeness of leaving Yale is the strangeness of recognizing that there is, finally, a “you.” But this is, I think, exactly what these four years were for. Being someone is not a burden; it is a beginning. The end of college is not really a closing of doors so much as it is the recognition that we were never meant to remain suspended in possibility forever. We have so much to hope for. We have so much time to turn our hopes into the rest of our lives. I hope all of the silly little decisions we make continue to make sense in hindsight — that they braid themselves into something that, from the other end, looks like it could only have gone this way. I hope we get long walks around unfamiliar cities, late-night laughs after dinner parties we won't remember, and friends we couldn't have predicted at twenty-two. I hope for the Candy Crush hours: for the time spent on things that don’t matter, in the company of people who do. I hope for myself. I hope for you. I hope for us. Class of 2026, let’s keep hoping. WILLIAM NING is a senior in Morse College studying Mathematics and Education Studies. Contact him at william.ning@yale.edu .

title: Thanks and farewell, Directed Studies
author: James Prud'homme
text: One of my closest friends has repeatedly urged me to write a piece for the Yale Daily News. I have always replied that I have no topic fitting for publication. As I near the end of my undergraduate years at Yale, however, I have realized there is something I can write about. I applied to Yale with the express intention of taking Directed Studies, or DS. The program, structured as an interdisciplinary interlacing of literary, philosophical, historical and political thought, seemed perfect for someone with my interests. DS has been valuable to me in ways that I could not have anticipated, and I thought, as a gesture of farewell and thanks, that I would try to express some of what those values are. The intellectual values are prominent and profound but not exactly what one might expect. Five values come immediately to mind. We became acquainted with the births and lives of ideas that continue to shape and challenge our thought. We learned to detect and track the intellectual conversations and arguments between thinkers as a mode and history of exchange, refinement and sometimes progress. We developed a habit of thinking against the grain of our preferences: discovering the unconventional, innovative and regularly radical origins and uses of ideas that we often assume as the conventionality of our own tacit agreements. We used rhetorical instruction as both rational and moral inquiry: learning from succeeding thinkers how to interpret their predecessors — and by extension any intellectual expositor — as participants in an ongoing exchange over truth and value, right and good. We followed reason to and beyond the limits of what it has sometimes seemed reasonable to say: The curriculum is full of examples of exorbitant modes of agreement and disagreement, including disagreements about the validity and function of such traditions of exchange themselves. But DS promotes other kinds of values, too. I met my closest friends in DS. The overlap between personal affection and shared intellectual memory has been probably my greatest pleasure at Yale. The professors teaching the courses are often prominent in their departments. The chance to learn from them is wonderful; the chance to learn from them when new to Yale is even better. I’ve had the pleasure of coming round in future years to other courses with the same teachers; these have served as continuations of relationships and also as spurs to notice how my thoughts have changed. DS is interlaced within itself; it also sets you up to interlace the years of your life in college with one another — and your own life with the lives of the people you meet within its precincts. Looking back, I see and feel these values patently — they have, as Wordsworth says of his memories of his younger self, “such self-presence in my heart.” In that spirit, I offer a miscellany of observations and advice for thriving in DS. Each semester, students in DS write nine short papers — three on literature, three on philosophy and three on historical and political thought — and take one final examination for each of the three tracks. Many are daunted by the workload. My experience has shown this to be, on the whole, unwarranted, or at least, largely self-correcting. Though the assignments are consistent — you will have a paper due most weeks — they are not overwhelming. With good scheduling — a skill we quickly developed — it is rare to face more than a single essay due each week. The rapid-fire paper-writing instructs itself through iteration; the ease and quality of writing naturally improves over the course of the semester. For the examinations, it is best to keep things simple. Review the most important sections of the texts for a few hours every day during reading period. Think of conversations between authors as conducted in the list of central passages. Use this image also for the timed essays: make a list of themes, assemble the dramatis personae of main authors and texts in conversation, assign their dialogue in the form of principal concepts and passages. Consider compiling brief outlines of trial essays. Use the peer tutors! Feedback from people in the same position, but with an objectivity you can’t give yourself, is invaluable. I was one of the first peer tutors, and last year, I served as head tutor. This year I oversee the tutors as writing supervisor. In his last letter to me, my friend Gordon Rogoff ’52 told me to “keep well, and keep deriving fun from great readings.” For those looking to collide with some of these “great readings,” in forms whose interest often lies in their not being what we expect from “greatness,” and who wish to interweave this learning with their lives, Directed Studies awaits. JAMES PRUD’HOMME is a senior living in Grace Hopper College who studies Humanities and Philosophy. Contact him at james.prudhomme@yale.edu .

title: Jesus and Grand Strategy
author: William Barbee
text: Few subjects may appear more disparate than the resurrection of Jesus Christ and the study of grand strategy. Yet, while in church for this year’s Easter services, I considered a connection between the two which enhanced my perspective on religion, on strategy and on Yale. Grand strategy, or “GS,” is a concept loose in its meaning. While often associated with the ways in which nations wage wars or governments enact policies, the term can refer to any action which requires a strategy to be achieved: athletic endeavors, job opportunities, romantic prospects. The best definition I have devised is to equate it to observing a round hole and attempting to find the square peg that best fits it. The principles one applies when conjoining limited means with limitless aspirations will never be perfect, but some may be more useful than others. And strategically, it is often wiser to make something less bad than to strive — and fail — to make something more perfect. Too often, however, discussions of grand strategy fall short of their grand implications and deal exclusively with matters of minute importance. Nowhere is this more true than at Yale, where the pursuit of perfection — rather than improvement — defines so much of what constitutes strategic success. Many of us have fallen prey to the belief that excellence might be achieved through the acquisition of wealth, influence, honor and acclaim. We see it in pre-professional clubs, where lucrative careers prove more enticing than personal fulfillment; in social settings, where peers may judge each other based on employment status rather than content of character; even in classrooms, where the rigid hierarchy of intelligence often prevails over the beautiful struggle of education . During my time in Yale’s Brady-Johnson Program in Grand Strategy, such academic sentiments were on full display. I learned much from my professors and colleagues, acquiring countless square pegs I will use later in life to fill countless round holes. But our class sought only to uncover whether a strategy had succeeded or failed in achieving its defined, material ends. Questions of moral strategy — how one might formulate ethical goals and the means to attain them — remained underexamined. It may be appropriate to exclude moral discussions from class if the goal is strictly to establish causal relationships between actions and events. But what happens when a strategy demands moral compromise? What happens when the desire for good ends attempts to justify the use of nefarious means? What happens when one believes that power is the only thing necessary to achieve change? Jesus was, by all accounts, a grand strategist. He recognized both his limitless aspirations — man’s salvation — and his limited means — man’s proclivity to sin — by which he might attain them. Yet, in doing so, he did not commit to obey the laws of power. He did not need to read the Melian Dialogue and Machiavelli to implement his vision. His was the strategy of success through failure: a necessary death for a perpetual resurrection. His was the strategy of complete commitment to leading a moral existence. His was the strategy not of this life, but of the next. Every student of grand strategy struggles to comprehend strategic lessons from the figure of Jesus. This is likely due to the fact that, despite its implicit “grandness,” grand strategy is actually quite limited in its scope of application. So many strategists seek to meet temporal means with temporal ends — victory in an election, preservation of an empire — that, when confronted with a truly grand strategy like that of Jesus, they suddenly recognize the smallness of their aims. Many graduates may experience a similar belittling at commencement. Students work tirelessly for years at their grand strategies of success, only to discover themselves as one drop in a sea of hats and gowns. Things previously considered important appear inconsequential when observed at a distance. Success amounts to no more than a handshake, a sheet of paper and a photo-op. Thus the importance of reminding ourselves that the grandest strategy of all is not how to succeed in life, but how to succeed in death. Whatever our temporal ends may be, our ultimate end remains the soft reprieve of the tomb; every strategy, therefore, must lead us to where we will be when we die, and whether or not our means of arriving there were just. Jesus’ story, religious professions aside, serves as perhaps the most useful square peg to be placed in the round hole of life. It is certainly the one which I consider to be remembered the least, and needed the most, at our university. As I close my Yale career, my final advice for all can be summarized no better than to follow the prescription of W. H. Auden: “Read The New Yorker, trust in God; and take short views.” WILLIAM BARBEE is a senior in Silliman College studying History and a staff columnist for the News. Contact him at william.barbee@yale.edu .

title: Leaving our legacy
author: Andrew Lipka
text: Last year, President McInnis chartered the Committee on Trust in Higher Education. The committee’s capstone report has arrived, with accompanying headlines . Thirty-two pages with statements of principles, diagnoses and 20 recommendations contain much that needed to be said. One can hope that the best ideas will receive the attention they deserve and find their way into implementation. But here’s the thing: Media headlines about the report have avoided its best parts, and many instead screamed a consistent meme: Legacy admissions must go. Bloomberg led with, “Yale Report Backs Curbing Legacy Admissions,” The Washington Post blared, “Yale considers changes to admissions” and Fortune echoed, “Admissions Process Questioned.” To be sure, other topics came up — notably including tuition cost — but legacy admissions was at the front, again and again. This is doubly problematic. The committee’s worthy primary theme, the primacy of academic excellence, got buried. Meanwhile, the report perpetrates an unjustified, unreasoned and frankly contradictory attack on legacy preference. In fact, the report mentions legacy only twice, principally through its recommendation that “Yale reduce preferences for special classes of applicants.” Why? It “distorts the admissions process.” In an 18-page bibliography, only one source is cited for this proposition. I applaud the committee’s choice to champion academic excellence. They even brought this same ethic to admissions when they asserted, “At Yale, the primacy of academic criteria should be non-negotiable.” They continued: “If the university claims that academic excellence is its mission, then its admissions process must visibly reflect that claim.” But much of what the public reads is instead the committee’s call — made essentially in passing — to eliminate legacy preference. This is damaging. And it is wrong. The committee was careless in throwing legacy preference to the dogs. By drowning out its real message, it was self-defeating . It should have known that the media would latch onto this. Legacy preference has long been a sexy topic. The public grossly exaggerates its extent and its degree. It is an easy target; it smacks of self-dealing, corruption and unfairness. But the committee offered no evidence to make that case. Its members should have known that this would drown out the good work they were trying to do. The public misunderstanding is in part due to the frequent mischaracterization of legacy preference at Yale. In truth, it is infrequent, mild and decreasing. Only 11 percent of the class of 2029 were alumni children; over the past 20 years, this number has decreased consistently. It basically takes the form of a tiebreaker when applicants are otherwise equal.
